pages.title: The Secret Architecture Behind AI Data Centers
pages.body:
#155: Why AI breakthroughs are really infrastructure breakthroughs
👋 Neo
here! This is a free preview of a subscriber-only post, exclusive to my
golden members. For the full experience, upgrade your subscription
.

===

🚨 Guest authors wanted. Are you a technical writer who writes about AI
engineering? Then reply to this email with one of the articles you’ve
written.
The Secret Architecture Behind AI Data Centers
Why AI breakthroughs are really infrastructure breakthroughs

Neo Kim  and Ashish
Jun 19
∙
Preview *Share this post & I’ll send you some rewards for the referrals.*

When people talk about Artificial Intelligence (*AI*), they often refer to
models such as Generative Pre-trained Transformers (*GPTs*), Large Language
Models (*LLMs*), and neural networks.

It sounds like magic, software that *thinks*…

But here’s the uncomfortable truth:

The biggest breakthroughs in AI are not coming from better models; they’re
coming from better infrastructure.

If AI were only about the models, there would be no need for huge,
warehouse-sized data centers packed with specialized hardware and powered
by massive amounts of electricity.

So what’s actually going on inside those buildings?

To make sense of everything that follows, we need a simple mental model.

Let’s find out one layer at a time…

Onward.
*Review the actual change, not the file list (Partner)
*AI writes more code than ever. Reviewing it shouldn’t mean scrolling forty
files in alphabetical order.
CodeRabbit Review
reorganizes any pull request from a flat file list into a structured,
layer-by-layer walkthrough - the logical reading order of the change, not
the order your platform happens to sort it. Every range gets its own
plain-language summary, with sequence diagrams, state machines, and ERDs
generated inline wherever a visual earns its place.

*Cohorts* group related files and chunks so you review one idea at a time.
*Layers* order them so foundational changes - data shapes, contracts - come
before the code that depends on them. *Code Peek* lets you click any
variable, function, class, or type to see its definition and usages without
leaving the tab, while *Semantic Diff* view cuts past formatting noise to
show what actually changed.

Open it straight from the Review Change Stack button in the PR Walkthrough.
Navigate cohorts and layers from the keyboard, comment against exact line
ranges, and submit native reviews, comments, and approvals post back to
GitHub or GitLab right where your team expects them.

*In the early access, available for free to everyone.*

From the team that pioneered AI code reviews. 2M reviews every week. 6M
repos. 15K customers.The review interface built for the way modern PRs are
actually written.

>>> *Review your next PR with CodeRabbit Review Today
(Thanks to CodeRabbit
for partnering on this post.)
I’d like to introduce *Ashish Prajapati
.
He is an AI infrastructure specialist and technology educator who helps
engineers understand the technologies powering modern AI.

With expertise spanning data centers, cloud computing, GPU infrastructure,
Generative AI, and Agentic AI, he focuses on turning complex concepts into
practical, real-world knowledge.

His training programs have reached over 39,000 learners worldwide, and
through his free mentoring initiative, Become a Solutions Architect (BeSA)
he has helped over 23,000 professionals accelerate their careers.

As AI adoption continues to grow, understanding the infrastructure behind
AI systems is becoming a valuable skill for architects, engineers, and IT
professionals.

For a limited time, this newsletter's readers can use code *SYSTEM-DESIGN
to get 25% off Ashish’s live NCA-AIIO bootcamp
.

This intensive one-day workshop covers the fundamentals of NVIDIA AI
infrastructure, GPU networking, accelerated computing, and AI operations,
and prepares you for the NVIDIA-Certified Associate: AI Infrastructure and
Operations (NCA-AIIO) certification.

*Learn, Prepare, and Pass NCA-AIIO in a Day
*Here’s what you’ll find inside this newsletter:* *The hidden architecture behind AI.* Why models are only the visible tip of a much larger infrastructure stack. *The physics that limit AI growth.* How power, cooling, and space shape every AI data center. *Why GPUs became the backbone of AI.* The architectural advantages that made modern AI training possible. *The real bottlenecks in modern AI.* Why memory, networking, and coordination determine performance at scale. *How thousands of GPUs work together as one system. *The software, networking, and orchestration layers that enable large-scale AI.

Golden members get all posts like these!…
Think of an AI Data Center as a Factory

Before we go deep, build this mental model; it will explain almost
everything that follows: AI Data Center = Factory Data = Raw material Graphics Processing Units (*GPUs*) = Machines on the factory floor Network = Internal transport system Storage = Warehouse holding raw materials Software = Assembly line logic Output = Trained intelligence

Now imagine a car factory.

It doesn’t just need skilled workers; instead, it needs conveyors,
warehouses, and power. Remove any of those, and production stops.

An AI data center works the same way…

AI infrastructure processes vast amounts of data using mathematical
operations, similar to how a factory transforms raw materials into finished
goods.

Remove any one ingredient, and the entire system stalls…

Once you see AI as a factory, the next question becomes obvious: *what
limits how fast this factory can run?*

Share
First Problem: Physics

*Before AI is a software problem, it’s a physics problem…*
Here are three hard physical constraints that shape every design decision
in an AI data center:
1. Power

Modern AI workloads rely on high-density GPUs that consume enormous amounts
of electricity.

A single server rack of GPUs can draw 80-120 kilowatts. i.e., roughly
equivalent to 40 homes running at full load simultaneously. Yet you can’t
just add more GPUs forever.

You’re limited by how much electricity your facility can physically receive
and distribute.
2. Cooling

More power means more heat, and heat is the enemy…

Here’s what happens if you don’t remove it fast enough: Hardware fails prematurely, Performance throttles back to avoid damage, In extreme cases, systems shut down entirely.

To manage the intense heat generated by AI workloads, modern data centers
are increasingly adopting *liquid cooling*, which is far more effective
than conventional air cooling.

Coolant is circulated through cold plates attached directly to GPUs and
CPUs, absorbing heat much more efficiently than air. The warmed liquid is
then pumped to heat exchangers, where the heat is removed before the
coolant is recirculated.

This approach enables higher rack densities, lower cooling power
consumption, and more consistent performance for large-scale AI training
and inference¹ workloads.
3. Space

Even if you had unlimited chips and unlimited power, you still need racks
to mount them and floor space to put those racks.

Scaling AI is not just software--it’s real estate, energy infrastructure,
and thermodynamics combined.
Takeaway

A data center is, at its core, a power plant combined with a massive,
precisely controlled air-conditioning system. Every engineering decision
flows from managing this reality.

After dealing with the physical challenges, the next question is: *how we
perform the computation?*
Compute: Why GPUs Took Over

To understand why GPUs dominate AI, picture a very simple task…

Imagine you have 1,000 fence posts to paint: Option A: One expert painter, working post by post. Slow, but the painter can handle any unexpected situation--a broken post, a curved section. Option B: 1,000 workers, each painting exactly one post simultaneously. Extremely fast, but each worker knows only one simple task.

That’s the difference between a CPU and a GPU.
CPU (Central Processing Unit) A few very powerful cores Handles complex, varied, sequential tasks Acts as the “manager” making decisions and coordinating work

GPU (Graphics Processing Unit) Thousands of smaller, simpler cores Handles same simple operation repeated millions of times, all at once Acts as the “factory workers” doing mass production

AI training repeatedly performs matrix multiplications billions of times,
making minor adjustments after each step. GPUs are specifically designed
for this type of work.
DPU (Data Processing Unit) As AI systems grow, a third processor becomes important. DPU handles networking, storage I/O, and security operations--tasks that would otherwise steal cycles from the GPU and CPU.

*Here’s the system view:* CPU = manager (decision-making) GPU = factory workers (mass production) DPU = logistics and operations (keeping materials moving and systems secure)

But even with powerful GPUs, computation alone isn’t the limiting factor;
the real challenge is keeping them fed with data…

Share
Hidden Bottleneck: Memory and Storage

Here’s something that surprises most people:

GPUs don’t fail because they’re slow. They fail because they’re
hungry—constantly starved for data.

Memory Hierarchy

Data doesn’t live in one place. It moves through a layered system, trading
speed for capacity: GPU Memory (HBM²): Blazingly fast but tiny; usually tens of gigabytes. This is the GPU’s working memory. CPU RAM: Larger (hundreds of gigabytes), but slower than HBM. SSD/NVMe³ Storage: Enormous capacity at a lower cost, but still slower. Network/Object Storage: Nearly unlimited, but the slowest link in the chain.

Every time the GPU needs data that isn’t already in its fast local memory,
it must wait... And a GPU sitting idle costs just as much money as one
doing useful work.

*Takeaway*

AI performance is not determined solely by how fast the GPU computes.
Instead, by how fast data can be fed into the GPU.

A faster chip with a slow data pipeline is like a faster car stuck in
traffic.
Tiered storage in practice

In real AI data centers, storage is organized into tiers that roughly map
to hot, warm, and cold data: *Hot*: NVMe SSDs holding active training datasets and model checkpoints. *Warm*: Parallel file systems⁴, such as Lustre, General Parallel File System (*GPFS*), provide high-speed shared access across many GPUs. *Cold*: Object storage, such as Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3), for storing raw datasets and older checkpoints.

As models grow larger, a single GPU is no longer enough…

Techniques such as model sharding⁵ and distributed training⁶ distribute the
model across multiple GPUs and memory layers.

And once data starts moving across machines, a new bottleneck appears: *how
those machines communicate with each other.*

*Reminder: this is a teaser of the subscriber-only newsletter series,
exclusive to my golden members.*

When you upgrade, you’ll get: *High-level architecture of real-world systems.* Deep dive into how popular real-world systems actually work. *How real-world systems handle scale, reliability, and performance.*

Unlock Full Access
Networking: Bottleneck Nobody Talks About

When training a large AI model, you typically use thousands of GPUs working
together rather than a single GPU…

And those thousands of GPUs must constantly communicate with each other,
sharing what they’ve learned at every step…
Every second a GPU waits for data or synchronization is a second of lost
productivity and wasted investment.
Cluster topology and fabrics

Modern AI clusters typically use leaf-spine network architectures⁷: *a
design built for high throughput and low latency at scale*.

Every GPU server can reach every other GPU server in *at most ‘two’ hops*.

Within a node, GPUs talk over NVLink (intra-node fabric). Between nodes,
traffic flows over InfiniBand or RDMA over Converged Ethernet (*RoCE*), the
inter-node fabric⁸.

There’s also a clear speed hierarchy: NVLink⁹: fastest path, a direct, private highway connecting GPUs within the same server. InfiniBand/RoCE¹⁰: very high-speed interconnect between separate servers. Imagine it as the “bullet train” of the data center. Ethernet: general-purpose, flexible, and ubiquitous, but not designed for AI’s most extreme bandwidth and latency demands.

Amdahl’s Law: Why More GPUs Aren’t Always Better

There’s a fundamental principle in distributed computing called *Amdahl’s
Law*.

In plain English: if parts of your system must wait for other parts, adding
more workers eventually stops helping.

In AI workloads, adding more GPUs increases computing power but also
increases communication overhead. As GPUs spend more time sharing and
synchronizing data, the bottleneck gradually shifts from computation to
communication.
Smarter data movement

Modern AI systems address this with several techniques: Remote Direct Memory Access (*RDMA*): Provides low-latency, high-throughput communication by enabling direct memory-to-memory data transfers between servers. RDMA kernel¹¹ bypass: Lets the network adapter access application memory directly from user space¹², eliminating unnecessary operating system overhead. GPUDirect: GPUs can read from and write to storage or network cards directly, bypassing the CPU entirely. Zero-copy pipelines: Data takes the shortest path from source to GPU, with no redundant copying along the way.

The goal of AI networking is to make thousands of GPUs behave like one
giant, unified processor, as if the data never had to travel at all.

*Once communication is in place, the next challenge is dividing the work
efficiently across all those GPUs…...*
Keep reading with a 7-day free trial

Subscribe to The System Design Newsletter to keep reading this post and get
7 days of free access to the full post archives.

pages.title: Three Features Three Claudes 2026 06 12
pages.body:
I ran three agents in parallel...Then merged everything.
                 
                 
                
                 
                
                 
                
                 
                
                 
    
February 5th is in my diary. I'm looking at three terminal windows, all
running Claude Code simultaneously, all working on the same project.

One is building a dark/light mode toggle. One is writing API endpoint
tests. One is adding code quality tooling. None of them knows the others
exist.

I watched all three work at the same time and remember thinking, "This is
genuinely new." This isn't a faster way to do what I was already doing.
This is a different thing entirely.

Here's the setup. Git has a feature called worktrees, you to check out
multiple branches of the same project into separate directories and work on
them independently. Each directory gets its own Claude session, its own
context window, its own isolated set of files to modify.

What this solves is the thing that makes parallel AI development feel
risky: two agents writing to the same file at the same time. With
worktrees, that can't happen.

They're operating in completely separate environments.

When all three were done, I committed each branch with a descriptive
message. Claude wrote those too, which matters more than it sounds when
you're about to merge three sets of changes, and then asked a fourth Claude
session to pull everything together and resolve any conflicts.

It found one. A pyproject.toml that two branches had both modified.
Resolved it in about thirty seconds.

The whole thing, three features built simultaneously, merged, tested, took
an afternoon. Features that would have been sequential, each requiring
their own setup, their own context, their own careful hand-holding through
implementation.

But here's what actually stuck with me. It wasn't the speed. It was that I
spent that afternoon thinking, about architecture, about what each feature
needed to know, about how the pieces would fit together after the merge.
Not implementing. Directing.

That's the shift I keep coming back to.

Have you ever tried running more than one AI session in parallel on the
same project? Did it work, or did it create more problems than it solved?

Talk soon,
Adrian
JSM

P.S. Next email, I want to talk about something I noticed when I spoke to a
business owner with 30 developers on his team.

Almost none of them were using AI seriously. What he said next is the
reason this course needs an enterprise angle, and why the timing right now
is unlike anything we've seen before., Croatia

-
*Share this post
& I'll send you some rewards for the referrals.*
I have a huge huge huge announcement for everyone…
INTRODUCING: *DevOps Mastermind*
This newsletter series will help you become a fast-growing and high-paying
engineer.
If you’ve ever thought:
*“I use Docker & Kubernetes, but I still don’t fully understand how they
work.”*
*“I want to master DevOps fundamentals instead of just memorizing
commands.”*
*“I should learn how modern infrastructure & real-world systems actually
work.”*
Then this is for you.
Here’s what you’ll get inside DevOps Mastermind:
-
*Architecture breakdown of core DevOps systems.*
-
Deep dives into Docker, Kubernetes, Terraform, and so on.
-
*Practical insights into how real-world systems achieve scalability,
automation, and reliability.*
Onward.
------------------------------
Your laptop is already running an operating system.
Now imagine starting another one on the same machine. A completely separate
system with its own files, memory, and processes. You can run Linux inside
Windows, or Windows inside a Mac. It boots independently, runs programs,
and behaves as if it has full control over the hardware.
But it doesn’t.
Underneath, there is still only one physical machine. One CPU, one pool of
memory, one disk. Yet multiple such systems can run at the same time, each
isolated from the others and each functioning as if it were the only system
present.
This idea changed computing…
Instead of buying a new server each time, you could split one powerful
machine into many smaller ones. Each workload gets its own space. Nothing
interferes with anything else.
That shift is what made cloud computing possible…
When you run an app on Amazon Web Services, Microsoft Azure, or Google
Cloud Platform, it runs inside a virtual machine. The entire cloud
infrastructure is built on this foundation.
*But how does it actually work? How can one machine present itself as many?
How are resources shared without breaking isolation?*
------------------------------
Build strong data foundations for agentic AI at scale (Partner)
AI experiments are easy.
But building trusted AI systems that scale across an enterprise is hard.
That’s exactly what leaders from Mercedes-Benz, Yahoo, Regeneron, and AWS
discuss in this on-demand virtual panel on agentic AI.
*Here’s what you’ll learn inside:*
-
*Practical strategies from enterprise leaders:* See how teams move from
isolated AI experiments to production-scale systems.
-
*Real-world guidance on governance and architecture:* Learn how industry
experts approach analytics, unified governance, and continuous learning.
-
*Frameworks for scaling intelligent systems:* Discover how to build AI
systems that drive business value.
-
*Pragmatic infrastructure decisions:* Learn how to select the right
databases and data foundations for AI-based applications.
Watch the *AWS panel
and see how enterprise teams are building trusted AI systems with speed,
governance, and control.
Watch the AWS Virtual Panel
(Thanks to AWS
for partnering on this post.)
------------------------------
I want to introduce *Ayaan
as a guest author.
He’s a self-taught DevOps engineer focused on Kubernetes & cloud
infrastructure. He builds in public and writes about cloud-native systems,
sharing daily technical tips and projects.
Check out his work and socials:
-
*Twitter
-
*Github
------------------------------
*Here’s what’s inside this newsletter:*
-
*The difference between emulation and virtualization, and why one
simulates hardware while the other shares it.*
-
What a virtual machine actually is, how it presents itself as a full
computer, and why the guest operating system believes it owns the hardware.
-
*How the hypervisor works, the different types it comes in, and how it
controls access to CPU, memory, storage, and networking.*
-
What happens under the hood when a virtual machine boots, from resource
allocation and virtual hardware setup to kernel startup and runtime
execution.
-
*How features like live migration, isolation, and resource controls make
virtual machines practical for modern infrastructure.*
-
Where virtual machines fit today, why cloud platforms depend on them,
and how they differ from containers.
Golden members get all posts like these!…
------------------------------
Emulation vs Virtualization
Before we get into how virtual machines work, there is one distinction
worth getting clear early.
Emulation is when software pretends to be completely different hardware.
Retro gaming emulators work this way. Your laptop takes each instruction
from the old console and turns it into something your modern chip
understands.
It works, but it is slow…
Virtualization is different. Instead of pretending to be different
hardware, it creates a version of the same hardware your machine already
has. Most instructions run directly on the real processor with no
translation needed.
The result is near-native speed.
*Think of it this way:*
-
Emulation is like hiring a translator for every sentence in a
conversation.
-
Virtualization is like realizing that both people already speak the same
language.
This distinction matters because speed is what made virtualization
practical at scale. You cannot run thousands of emulated systems on a
single server. With virtualization, you can.
And that is exactly what cloud providers do…
------------------------------
What a Virtual Machine Actually Is
A virtual machine is a complete computer abstraction built on top of a
physical machine.
It’s presented with a CPU, memory, storage, and a network interface, just
like a physical system. The operating system inside it interacts with these
components as if they were real hardware. It has no awareness that it is
sharing the underlying machine with anything else.
From its perspective, it owns the system.
This behavior is made possible by a layered design.
At the bottom is the physical hardware, which provides the actual compute,
memory, and storage resources. Above it sits the hypervisor, which controls
how those resources are allocated and accessed. On top of that, each
virtual machine runs its own operating system in isolation.
The hypervisor is the layer that makes the illusion possible.
Because the operating system never interacts with the hardware directly.
Instead, every request for CPU time, memory access, disk I/O, or network
communication is handled through the hypervisor. It decides how resources
are shared and ensures that each virtual machine remains isolated from the
others.
This abstraction enables three key capabilities:
-
*Isolation:* Each virtual machine runs independently, so a failure in
one doesn’t affect others.
-
*Portability:* Virtual machines can move between physical systems with
minimal changes.
-
*Efficient resource use:* Multiple workloads share the same hardware
instead of leaving it idle.
These properties are what made modern infrastructure possible.
Next, let’s look at the hypervisor: what it is, the different types, and
how it powers virtual machines…
------------------------------
Hypervisor: Brain Behind the Illusion
The hypervisor is the software layer that enables virtualization.
It sits between the physical hardware and the virtual machines running
above it. Every request a virtual machine makes, whether for CPU time,
memory, or storage, goes through the hypervisor. It’s responsible for
mapping those requests to the underlying hardware resources.
This layer controls all access to the hardware.
Type 1 vs Type 2
Not all hypervisors follow the same structure. The main difference comes
from where they run.
-
*Type 1 (bare-metal):* It runs on the physical hardware, with no host
operating system in between. Examples include VMware ESXi, Microsoft
Hyper-V, and Kernel-based Virtual Machine (*KVM*). Cloud environments
and data centers use these because they provide direct hardware access and
minimal overhead.
-
*Type 2 (hosted):* Runs on top of an existing operating system, like a
regular application. Tools such as VirtualBox and VMware Workstation fall
into this category. They are easier to use but introduce additional
overhead.
The difference lies in how the hypervisor accesses hardware and the number
of extra layers it adds…
Full Virtualization vs Paravirtualization
There are also different ways a hypervisor presents hardware to a virtual
machine.
In *full virtualization*, guest operating system runs without modification
and behaves as if it were on real hardware. The hypervisor handles all the
work required to maintain that abstraction.
In *paravirtualization*, the guest operating system is aware that it is
running in a virtual environment. Instead of relying only on emulated
hardware, it asks the hypervisor to handle some tasks, which reduces
overhead.
The trade-off is between compatibility and performance.
Full virtualization runs any unmodified operating system.
Paravirtualization is faster because the guest works with the hypervisor.
Hardware-Assisted Virtualization
Early hypervisors relied on software, making virtualization complex and
inefficient. This changed when processor manufacturers introduced hardware
support such as Intel VT-x (Intel Virtualization Technology for x86) and
AMD-V (AMD Virtualization).
With these features, CPU itself can differentiate between virtual machines
and the hypervisor. Sensitive operations pass control to the hypervisor
when needed. It manages access and keeps everything secure.
This made virtualization practical at scale.
Nested Virtualization
It is also possible to run a virtual machine inside another virtual
machine, a setup known as *nested virtualization*. You use this for
testing, development, and running tools like Kubernetes locally in virtual
machines (*VMs*).
But each additional layer introduces overhead and increases complexity. As
you add more virtualization layers, performance drops. This makes the
approach unsuitable for most production workloads.
It is useful in controlled scenarios, but not a general-purpose design.
Now you know what the hypervisor does. The next question is how it shares
the CPU across multiple virtual machines. The answer is CPU virtualization.
Let’s look at how it works…
------------------------------
CPU Virtualization
The CPU is the brain of any computer.
Every instruction, process, and calculation goes through the CPU. So when
you create a virtual machine, the question becomes: *how do different
operating systems share one CPU without getting in each other’s way?*
That is what CPU virtualization solves…
The Privilege Model
Operating systems are designed to be in charge.
When your OS needs to access hardware, manage memory, or control processes,
it does so with full authority. The processor has a concept called *privilege
levels* to enforce this.
Think of it like levels of trust, where the most trusted software gets the
most access.
In a normal system, the operating system sits at the highest privilege
level.
But in a virtual environment, the hypervisor needs to sit above the
operating system. It needs to intercept certain instructions and control
what the guest OS can and cannot do. So the guest OS gets moved down to a
lower privilege level, even though it still believes it is fully in charge.
The guest OS never knows the hypervisor demoted it.
This is the fundamental trick behind CPU virtualization. The guest OS
continues to behave as if it owns the processor. But when it tries to do
something that needs high-level access, the processor steps in and hands
control to the hypervisor.
Everything continues smoothly, just one level deeper…
vCPU and Scheduling
When you create a virtual machine, you assign it CPUs, but these are not
physical cores. They’re virtual CPUs (*vCPUs*) that represent processing
capacity managed by the hypervisor.
The hypervisor schedules vCPUs onto physical cores in the same way an
operating system schedules processes.
At any given moment, a physical core can execute only one thread of work.
The hypervisor switches between virtual machines, giving each one a slice
of CPU time. This switching happens fast enough that each virtual machine
appears to have continuous access to the processor.
*Modern CPUs may expose multiple logical threads per core through
technologies like Intel Hyper-Threading or AMD SMT (Simultaneous
Multithreading), but CPU time is still shared and scheduled across
workloads.*
In reality, all virtual machines share it.
This is called *time-slicing*. It’s the same idea your operating system
uses to run multiple applications at once.
Hypervisors can also assign more vCPUs than there are physical cores, a
practice known as *CPU overcommit*. For example, a machine with 16 cores
might run virtual machines totaling 32 or more vCPUs.
This works as long as not all virtual machines are busy at the same time.
When demand rises across multiple VMs, they compete for CPU time. This
causes contention and reduces performance.
Overcommit is a useful tool, but it depends on workload behavior.
VM-exit and VM-entry
Let’s go one level deeper into what actually happens when a guest OS tries
to do something it is not allowed to do on its own.
When a virtual machine tries to run a privileged instruction, the processor
stops it. Control is immediately transferred to the hypervisor. This event
is called a *VM-exit*.
The hypervisor then figures out what the guest was trying to do and handles
it safely.
Once the hypervisor has dealt with the request, it hands control back to
the virtual machine exactly where it left off. This is called a *VM-entry*.
From the guest OS’s perspective, nothing unusual happened. The CPU executed
the instruction and continued running.
The entire handoff takes microseconds.
This mechanism is what makes isolation possible. The guest OS cannot access
hardware it shouldn’t. Every sensitive operation triggers a VM-exit, which
the hypervisor controls. But it also introduces overhead. Every VM-exit and
VM-entry takes time, and if they happen too frequently, performance suffers.
Keeping VM exits to a minimum is one of the core challenges of CPU
virtualization.
NUMA Awareness and CPU Topology
This matters more in large systems and performance-sensitive workloads, but
it’s still worth understanding at a high level…
Modern servers often have more than one physical processor. Each processor
has its own local memory. Accessing memory that belongs to a different
processor is slower than accessing your own. This architecture is called
Non-Uniform Memory Access (*NUMA*).
The name describes the problem exactly. Not all memory accesses are equal.
In a virtual environment, this creates a subtle issue. A virtual machine
might run on cores from one processor but end up accessing memory that
belongs to another. The guest OS does not know this is happening. But the
performance hit is real.
The solution is NUMA-aware scheduling.
The hypervisor tries to keep a virtual machine’s vCPUs and its memory on
the same NUMA node. When it succeeds, the VM runs faster. When workloads
grow and resources get spread across nodes, the performance gap becomes
noticeable.
For most small deployments, this never comes up. For high-performance
databases or real-time systems running on large servers, it matters a lot.
Putting it together, CPU virtualization is not a single mechanism but a
combination of coordinated techniques.
The privilege model restricts direct hardware access. vCPU scheduling
creates the illusion of dedicated processing power. VM-exits and VM-entries
enforce safe boundaries for sensitive operations. NUMA awareness ensures
that physical hardware layout does not introduce hidden performance
penalties.
Each part addresses a specific challenge.
Together, they allow multiple virtual machines to share a single physical
CPU while operating independently and efficiently.
Next, let’s look at how memory is virtualized and how virtual machines
manage their view of system memory…
------------------------------
*Reminder: this is a teaser of the subscriber-only newsletter, exclusive to
my golden members.*
When you upgrade, you’ll get:
-
*Architecture breakdown of core DevOps systems.*
-
Deep dives into Docker, Kubernetes, Terraform, and so on.
-
*Practical insights into how real-world systems achieve scalability,
automation, and reliability.*
Unlock Full Access
Let’s keep going!
------------------------------
Memory Virtualization...
Keep reading with a 7-day free trial
7 days of free access to the full post archives.
Start trial
A subscription gets you:
Access to Design, Build, Scale
Access to Interview Academy
Access to Deep Dives

pages.title: How Virtual Machines Work - A Deep Dive
pages.body:
# How Virtual Machines Work - A Deep Dive

*System Design Newsletter #148 — Part 1: DevOps Mastermind*
*By Neo Kim & Ayaan (guest author)*

## Summary

A deep dive into how virtual machines work under the hood — from the fundamental distinction between emulation and virtualization, through hypervisor architecture (Type 1 vs Type 2), to the mechanisms of CPU virtualization including privilege models, vCPU scheduling, VM-exit/VM-entry cycles, and NUMA awareness. This is Part 1 of the DevOps Mastermind series, authored by Neo Kim with guest author Ayaan, a self-taught DevOps engineer focused on Kubernetes & cloud infrastructure.

## Key Concepts

### Emulation vs Virtualization

Emulation is when software pretends to be completely different hardware. Retro gaming emulators work this way — your laptop takes each instruction from the old console and turns it into something your modern chip understands. It works, but it is slow.

Virtualization is different. Instead of pretending to be different hardware, it creates a version of the same hardware your machine already has. Most instructions run directly on the real processor with no translation needed. The result is near-native speed.

- **Emulation** is like hiring a translator for every sentence in a conversation.
- **Virtualization** is like realizing that both people already speak the same language.

This distinction matters because speed is what made virtualization practical at scale. You cannot run thousands of emulated systems on a single server. With virtualization, you can.

### What a Virtual Machine Actually Is

A virtual machine is a complete computer abstraction built on top of a physical machine. It's presented with a CPU, memory, storage, and a network interface, just like a physical system. The operating system inside it interacts with these components as if they were real hardware. It has no awareness that it is sharing the underlying machine with anything else. From its perspective, it owns the system.

**Layered design:**
1. **Physical hardware** — provides actual compute, memory, and storage resources.
2. **Hypervisor** — controls how those resources are allocated and accessed.
3. **Virtual machines** — each runs its own operating system in isolation.

Because the operating system never interacts with the hardware directly, every request for CPU time, memory access, disk I/O, or network communication is handled through the hypervisor.

**Three key capabilities enabled by this abstraction:**
- **Isolation:** Each VM runs independently; a failure in one doesn't affect others.
- **Portability:** VMs can move between physical systems with minimal changes.
- **Efficient resource use:** Multiple workloads share the same hardware instead of leaving it idle.

### Hypervisor Types

The hypervisor is the software layer that enables virtualization. It sits between the physical hardware and the virtual machines, mapping every request to underlying hardware resources.

**Type 1 (Bare-Metal):** Runs directly on physical hardware with no host OS in between.
- Examples: VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM (Kernel-based Virtual Machine)
- Used in cloud environments and data centers
- Provides direct hardware access and minimal overhead

**Type 2 (Hosted):** Runs on top of an existing operating system like a regular application.
- Examples: VirtualBox, VMware Workstation
- Easier to use but introduces additional overhead

### Full Virtualization vs Paravirtualization

**Full Virtualization:** The guest operating system runs without modification and behaves as if it were on real hardware. The hypervisor handles all the work required to maintain that abstraction.

**Paravirtualization:** The guest operating system is aware that it is running in a virtual environment. Instead of relying only on emulated hardware, it asks the hypervisor to handle some tasks, reducing overhead.

The trade-off: full virtualization runs any unmodified OS; paravirtualization is faster because the guest works with the hypervisor.

### Hardware-Assisted Virtualization

Early hypervisors relied on software, making virtualization complex and inefficient. This changed when processor manufacturers introduced hardware support:

- **Intel VT-x** (Intel Virtualization Technology for x86)
- **AMD-V** (AMD Virtualization)

With these features, the CPU itself can differentiate between virtual machines and the hypervisor. Sensitive operations pass control to the hypervisor when needed. This made virtualization practical at scale.

### Nested Virtualization

It is also possible to run a VM inside another VM, a setup known as nested virtualization. Used for testing, development, and running tools like Kubernetes locally in VMs. But each additional layer introduces overhead and performance drops, making it unsuitable for most production workloads.

---

## CPU Virtualization

Every instruction, process, and calculation goes through the CPU. The core question: how do different operating systems share one CPU without getting in each other's way?

### The Privilege Model

Operating systems are designed to be in charge. The processor has privilege levels to enforce this — think of them as levels of trust where the most trusted software gets the most access. In a normal system, the OS sits at the highest privilege level.

In a virtual environment, the hypervisor needs to sit above the OS. It intercepts certain instructions and controls what the guest OS can and cannot do. The guest OS gets moved down to a lower privilege level — even though it still believes it is fully in charge. The guest OS never knows the hypervisor demoted it.

This is the fundamental trick behind CPU virtualization.

### vCPU and Scheduling

When you create a VM, you assign it virtual CPUs (vCPUs) that represent processing capacity managed by the hypervisor. The hypervisor schedules vCPUs onto physical cores the same way an OS schedules processes.

At any given moment, a physical core can execute only one thread of work. The hypervisor switches between VMs, giving each a slice of CPU time. This switching happens fast enough that each VM appears to have continuous access.

This is called **time-slicing** — the same idea your OS uses to run multiple apps at once.

**CPU overcommit:** Hypervisors can assign more vCPUs than physical cores exist (e.g., 16 cores running VMs totaling 32 vCPUs). This works as long as not all VMs are busy at the same time. When demand rises across multiple VMs, they compete for CPU time, causing contention and reduced performance.

### VM-exit and VM-entry

When a VM tries to run a privileged instruction, the processor stops it. Control is immediately transferred to the hypervisor. This event is called a **VM-exit**.

The hypervisor figures out what the guest was trying to do and handles it safely. Once done, it hands control back to the VM exactly where it left off. This is called a **VM-entry**. From the guest OS's perspective, nothing unusual happened.

The entire handoff takes microseconds. This mechanism makes isolation possible — every sensitive operation triggers a VM-exit that the hypervisor controls. But it also introduces overhead. Keeping VM exits to a minimum is one of the core challenges of CPU virtualization.

### NUMA Awareness and CPU Topology

Modern servers often have more than one physical processor. Each processor has its own local memory. Accessing memory that belongs to a different processor is slower — this architecture is called **Non-Uniform Memory Access (NUMA)**.

In a virtual environment, a VM might run on cores from one processor but access memory belonging to another. The guest OS doesn't know this is happening, but the performance hit is real.

The solution is **NUMA-aware scheduling**: the hypervisor tries to keep a VM's vCPUs and its memory on the same NUMA node. For most small deployments, this never comes up. For high-performance databases or real-time systems on large servers, it matters a lot.

**Summary of CPU virtualization mechanisms:**
- The privilege model restricts direct hardware access.
- vCPU scheduling creates the illusion of dedicated processing power.
- VM-exits and VM-entries enforce safe boundaries for sensitive operations.
- NUMA awareness ensures physical hardware layout doesn't introduce hidden performance penalties.

---

## Memory Virtualization

*(The remaining content on memory virtualization, live migration, and VM vs containers comparison is behind the paywall.)*

pages.title: Why Your 2026 Career Goals Are Already Dead 2026 06 16
pages.body:
# Why Your 2026 Career Goals Are Already Dead — And What to Do About It

**Source:** [[Zaim Mohzani]]'s career newsletter ([[Nation Building School]])
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ece778ab23e039
**Ingested:** 2026-06-23T20:11:00Z
**Provenance:** Self-forwarded by [[Zaim Mohzani]] from zaim@nationbuildingschool.com to info+zaim@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-16

It's June. How's that career goal from January going?

If you're being honest, probably: forgotten by March, barely remembered (and feeling guilty) in June and quietly dropped by the end of the year.

It's a timeframe problem, not a discipline problem.

## The Annual Goal Trap

A year is too long to create urgency and too vague to act on. "Get a new job this year" feels fine in January. There's time. So nothing happens. By June, the goal is still technically alive, but you haven't done anything that moves it forward. By December, you're setting the same goal again for next year.

Zaim's personal story: In January 2025, he set an annual goal: lose 6kg by December. Standard SMART goal. Then he came across [[Brian Moran]] and [[Michael Lennington]]'s book "The 12 Week Year," which argues most people don't actually operate in years. They operate in the last few weeks before a deadline. So the fix is to shrink the deadline.

He rewrote the goal: lose 6kg in 90 days, by 31 March. He hit it. The best part was that he never had to set that goal again — the habits built in those 90 days kept running on their own.

## Why This Works for Career Too

"Get promoted this year" doesn't tell you what to do on Monday.

"By 30 September, I will have had 3 conversations with people in my target department, applied to 5 internal roles, and presented one project to leadership" tells you exactly what to do on Monday.

One is a wish. The other is a plan.

Zaim's clients who set a 90-day goal like "land 3 offers from MNCs by end of Q3" with weekly actions attached mostly hit it or get close. The ones with "find a better job this year" mostly don't, because there's no Monday task attached.

## How to Set Yours

Pick ONE career goal for this quarter. Just one.

Write down:
- **My 90-day career goal is...**
- **This matters to me because...**
- **To complete this, I commit that by 30 September 2026, I will have...** (make this checkable. "3 offers" is checkable. "More clarity" is not.)
- **To make sure I complete it, I'm going to...** (weekly actions, not the outcome)

[Source: [[Zaim Mohzani]]'s email to info+zaim@malohacoast.com, 2026-06-16]

## X/Twitter Post

pages.title: Hermes Assistant To Chief Of Staff Ericosiu 2026 06 20
pages.body:
# How we turned Hermes from an assistant into our chief of staff

**Source:** X Article by ericosiu (@ericosiu)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee6b8b30fd1a2c
**Ingested:** 2026-06-22T12:30:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by Sayf to info@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-21

## Summary

A comprehensive X Article by [[ericosiu]] (Founder @ [[Single Brain]], [[Single Grain]]) detailing 12 jobs they give Hermes to turn it from a passive assistant into an autonomous operator that runs business workflows. The key insight: think of Hermes as a worker you *run*, not a chat window you *open*.

## Original URL

https://x.com/ericosiu/status/2068370407049011538

## Key Ideas

### 1. Branded artifacts on demand
Point Hermes at a Google design.md spec on GitHub and every visual comes out on-brand — carousels, one-pagers, case studies, ad concepts. Separate design files for separate brands.

### 2. Run it inside Slack (or Telegram/WhatsApp)
Slack gives threaded replies so multi-workstream collaboration stays organized. Knowledge moves faster between people when the agent lives where the work already happens.

### 3. Give yourself an agent you *work in*
Multiple threads running at once — each a self-contained workstream that progresses while you do something else. Your job shrinks to approving instead of doing.

### 4. Wire it into Linear
Hermes plugs into Linear alongside Claude Code and Codex. Opens tickets as work happens, routes them back for review. Closed loop keeps projects moving without hourly check-ins.

### 5. Use a /goal command
Define what "done" looks like, let the agent grind toward it while you work on something else. Running several harnesses in tandem — not one at a time.

### 6. Give it memory with Obsidian
Built-in memory isn't enough to run a business. Use Obsidian as the external memory store + QMD (Shopify's Tobi Lütke's QMD setup on GitHub). Daily decisions get logged. The agent compounds rather than waking up cold every morning.

### 7. Connect your APIs
API-connected agent reasons against real business data. Call/CRM layer: Granola, Gong, HubSpot. Content layer: X API, YouTube API. Every API connection turns the agent from a guesser into something that thinks with your data.

### 8. Build a content ingestor
Point it at everything published (X, YouTube, short form) plus meeting notes (Granola). Topics you get most fired up about internally are usually your best content.

### 9. Let it draft your emails
Two modes: draft-approve-send vs send-on-own-with-closed-loop. Revenue agents operate at send level for sponsorship replies and booking customer development calls. Can revive stalled deals or run trigger-based outreach.

### 10. Stack specialist agents underneath it
Build narrow specialists (cold outbound infrastructure, ad creatives at scale). Hermes sits on top and manages them. One person operates like a department.

### 11. Use OAuth, not tokens
Metered API tokens can climb to thousands/month. OAuth + Claude CLI → ~$200/month instead. This one change pays for the rest of the stack.

### 12. Go multi-device with Tailscale
All machines on one private network. Can reach any from anywhere. Gateway restart from phone via Termius if Hermes freezes. Without this, a frozen process at home strands you.

## Relevance to Maloha Coast

Sayf forwarded this because it directly mirrors how Maloha Coast uses Hermes — as an orchestrator agent that coordinates specialist sub-agents (MC for ops, Sammy for content). The article validates the approach and provides concrete implementation patterns (Obsidian memory, Linear integration, API wiring, multi-agent stacking) that could be applied or adapted.

## Author

**[[ericosiu]]** (@ericosiu) — Founder, [[Single Brain]] (MDR revenue agents with one unified brain). Also runs [[Single Grain]] (ad agency). Investor. YPO Beverly Hills. Podcasts: Marketing School, Leveling Up.

## Metrics
- 114 likes, 16 retweets, 6 replies, 363 bookmarks, 55k views
- Published: Sat Jun 20 16:28:28 +0000 2026

pages.title: karpathy's llm wiki gist
pages.body:
# karpathy's llm wiki gist

**Source:** email from Sayf Codes
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee3343b6e5b9e9
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T17:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf

**URL:** https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f

## Summary

[[Andrej Karpathy]]'s pattern for building personal knowledge bases using LLMs. The core insight: instead of just using RAG (retrieving chunks at query time, re-discovering knowledge each time), the LLM incrementally builds and maintains a **persistent, interlinked wiki** of markdown files that sits between you and your raw sources. Knowledge is compiled once and kept current.

### Key Architecture

**Three layers:**
1. **Raw sources** — immutable source documents
2. **The wiki** — LLM-generated markdown pages (summaries, entities, concepts, cross-references)
3. **The schema** — a CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md file that tells the LLM the wiki conventions and workflows

### Key Operations
- **Ingest** — drop a source, LLM processes it, updates 10-15 pages
- **Query** — ask questions against the wiki with cited answers; good answers get filed back in
- **Lint** — health-check for contradictions, stale claims, orphans, missing cross-references

### Why It Works
The bookkeeping burden (cross-references, consistency, updates) kills human wikis. LLMs handle it effortlessly. Humans curate sources and ask good questions; LLMs do the maintenance.

### Relevance to Maloha Coast
Directly relevant to gbrain architecture — Karpathy describes the same wiki-as-compound-artifact pattern that gbrain implements. The three-layer architecture (raw → wiki → schema) aligns with gbrain's raw sources → brain pages → resolver pattern. Worth reviewing as a reference for the gbrain design.

## Related
- [[gbrain]] — Maloha Coast's persistent knowledge base
- [[[[Andrej Karpathy]]]] — Author and AI researcher

**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T12:01:16Z

## Summary

Andrej Karpathy's "LLM Wiki" is a pattern for using LLMs to build and maintain a persistent, interlinked personal knowledge base. Instead of treating LLMs as retrieval engines over raw documents (RAG), the LLM incrementally constructs a wiki — structured markdown files — that sits between the user and source materials.

## Key Ideas

- **Persistent compounding artifact**: The wiki accumulates knowledge over time. Cross-references are pre-built, contradictions flagged, synthesis reflects everything read. Knowledge is compiled once and kept current, not re-derived per query.
- **LLM as maintainer**: The LLM writes and maintains the wiki; the user curates sources, explores, and asks the right questions. "Obsidian is the IDE; the LLM is the programmer; the wiki is the codebase."
- **Three-layer architecture**:
  1. **Raw sources** — immutable source documents (articles, papers, data)
  2. **The wiki** — LLM-generated markdown files (summaries, entities, concepts, cross-references)
  3. **The schema** — config file (e.g. CLAUDE.md) defining structure, conventions, and workflows
- **Operations**: Ingest (drop sources → LLM processes, updates 10-15 pages), Query (ask questions → LLM synthesizes with citations, files answers back), Lint (periodic health-check for contradictions, orphans, gaps)
- **Indexing**: `index.md` (content-oriented catalog) + `log.md` (chronological append-only record)
- **Optional CLI tools**: qmd for local search (BM25/vector hybrid), Obsidian Web Clipper for ingestion

## Why This Matters

- The most valuable insight: "The tedious part of maintaining a knowledge base is not the reading or thinking — it's the bookkeeping." LLMs eliminate the maintenance burden that causes humans to abandon wikis.
- "Your moat is your data" — this pattern directly enables the kind of autonomous, compounding knowledge system described in the Claude Second Brain newsletter (Level 5: fully autonomous recall).
- Related in spirit to Vannevar Bush's Memex (1945) — associative trails between documents, private and curated.

## Relevance to Maloha Coast

This is the architectural foundation for gbrain itself — and a reference for how to evolve gbrain beyond basic RAG into a truly autonomous knowledge system. The schema concept (CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md) maps to Hermes skills and resolver files.

## Entities

- [[Andrej Karpathy]] — Author of the LLM Wiki pattern. Former AI director at Tesla, AI researcher, creator of numerous influential educational resources.

pages.title: karpathy's llm wiki gist
pages.body:
# Karpathy's LLM Wiki — A Pattern for Persistent Knowledge Bases

**Source:** email (sayfcodes → info)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee3343b6e5b9e9
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T12:10:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf on 2026-06-20

## Summary

Andrej Karpathy's gist describes a pattern for building persistent, LLM-maintained knowledge bases (wikis) that compound over time — distinct from traditional RAG which re-derives answers from scratch on every query.

## Key Ideas

- **The core idea**: Instead of retrieving from raw documents at query time (RAG), the LLM incrementally builds and maintains a persistent wiki — interlinked markdown files that sit between you and raw sources. The wiki is a compounding artifact that gets richer with every source added.

- **Three-layer architecture**: (1) Raw sources (immutable, curated collection), (2) The wiki (LLM-generated markdown files — summaries, entities, concepts, synthesis — LLM writes, you read), (3) The schema (a CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md document that tells the LLM how the wiki is structured).

- **Operations**: Ingest (drop source → LLM reads, discusses, writes 10-15 wiki pages), Query (LLM searches wiki, synthesizes answers with citations — good answers get filed back into the wiki), Lint (periodic health-check for contradictions, orphans, gaps).

- **Indexing**: `index.md` (content catalog, updated on every ingest) and `log.md` (chronological record, parseable with grep/tail).

- **Why it works**: Humans abandon wikis because maintenance burden grows faster than value. LLMs don't get bored, don't forget cross-references, and can touch 15 files in one pass. The cost of maintenance is near zero.

## Relevance to Maloha Coast

This gist describes essentially the same pattern as gbrain — an LLM-maintained, interlinked wiki that compounds over time. Key differences: Karpathy's approach uses Obsidian as the IDE (human browses updates in real-time via graph view), while gbrain uses a CLI + dashboard. The concepts are directly applicable — the schema layer (CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md) mirrors gbrain's skill system for instructing agents how to maintain the knowledge base.

## Link

https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f

## Entities

- Andrej Karpathy — author of the gist, prominent AI researcher

## Summary

Stripe Online was a free two-hour virtual event on 3 June 2026 featuring leaders from [[Stripe]], [[Anthropic]], [[Google]], and [[Thoughtworks]]. The event covered AI-powered commerce, agentic purchasing flows, monetisation strategies for AI products, and payment optimisation for APAC markets.

## Keynote Themes

- **New rules of APAC commerce** — AI is rewriting discovery, decision, and purchase processes
- **Agentic commerce** — How AI agents entering purchase flows changes business requirements
- **Monetisation for AI products** — Usage-based to hybrid pricing models that scale

## Named Speakers & Topics

- **[[Yunong Xiao]]**, Head of Engineering, Claude AI and Agentic Commerce, [[Anthropic]] — How AI agents change people's interactions, decisions, and buying behaviour, and implications for customer journeys
- **[[Sapna Chadha]]**, Vice President, Southeast Asia, [[Google]] — AI transforming search into purchase, shortening the distance between discoverability and checkout
- **[[Mara Cajar-Robinson]]**, Director, Strategic AI Transformation, [[Thoughtworks]] — Practical steps for transforming operations for agentic commerce
- **[[Zac Liew]]**, Chief Commercial Officer, [[Coda Payments]] — Navigating payment complexity and scaling into new markets
- **[[Ahmed Abubakar]]**, CEO and Cofounder, [[ImagineArt]] — Lessons from building and monetising an AI-native product

## Breakout Track Sessions

- Agentic commerce: What your business needs to know now
- Monetisation strategies that work for AI products
- Customer case study: Lessons learned from [[ImagineArt]]
- The right payment method at the right time: How to test and win
- The global growth playbook: Scaling revenue without complexity
- Customer case study: Lessons learned from [[Coda Payments]]

## Relevance to [[Maloha Coast]]

Maloha Coast builds AI solutions for SMBs. The event content on agentic commerce, AI-native product monetisation, and APAC payment optimisation is directly relevant to consulting engagements. Named speakers and their companies are potential reference points for client positioning.

pages.title: Maloha Coast — Brand Identity
pages.body:
# Maloha Coast — Brand Identity

## Finalised Brand Decisions (locked June 2026)

### Colour Palette
- **Primary**: #2a6a8a (teal blue)
- **Accent**: #c4a86a (champagne gold)
- **Dark Navy**: #0a1628
- **Light backgrounds**: #f4f8fb, #ffffff

### Typography
- **Primary font**: Optima (humanist, elegant Roman forms)
- **Fallback stack**: 'Segoe UI', 'Candara', 'Palatino Linotype', 'Book Antiqua', Palatino, serif

### Brand Pattern
⬡ **Honeycomb Hexagon Grid** — Gold on Navy
- Hex lines: #c4a86a (champagne gold)
- Background field: #0a1628 (dark navy)
- Tile size: 90×52 px seamless tiling PNG
- Hosted at: https://iili.io/CK3nHLF.png
- Also available in 7 other colour variants (see skill references)

## Email Templates

The production email template is based on the "Ocean Deep" direction (selected from 5 brand concepts A-E).

### Template Structure
1. **Outer wrapper**: Gold-on-navy hex pattern background (via HTML `background` attribute on `<td>`, NOT CSS)
2. **Header**: Pattern visible with rgba(10,22,40,0.65) semi-transparent overlay → "MALOHA COAST" in white + "Sdn Bhd" in gold
3. **Body card**: White rounded card with generous padding
4. **Info cards**: Gold left accent border (#c4a86a) on light teal (#f4f8fb) background
5. **Signature**: Gold horizontal rule (#c4a86a) + name/title/company/tagline in brand colours
6. **Footer**: Same hex pattern background as header

### Gmail Compatibility (verified)
- Use HTML `background` attribute on `<td>` elements for patterns (CSS `background-image` with data URIs is stripped by Gmail)
- Use nested `<table>` with `rgba()` overlay for colour tinting
- Use inline styles only (no `<style>` blocks)
- Max width 600px for email card
- No JavaScript

### Skill Reference
The full workflow is in the `html-email-with-sheet-logging` skill:
- **SKILL.md**: /home/ubuntu/.hermes/skills/productivity/html-email-with-sheet-logging/SKILL.md
- **Production template**: references/ocean-deep-template.html
- **Header fragment**: templates/maloha-coast-header-fragment.html
- **Hex pattern variants**: references/hex-pattern-variants.md
- **Script to regenerate**: scripts/generate-hex-pattern.py

### Brand Concepts File
Full brand exploration HTML (5 palettes, 12 patterns, typography pairings):
/home/ubuntu/maloha-coast-brand-concepts.html

### Related
- [[maloha-coast]] — company overview
- [[google-workspace-setup]] — email infrastructure

# OpenRouter

> AI model routing platform providing unified API access to 300+ models across multiple providers, with configurable privacy and data policy controls.

## In Maloha Coast Knowledge

- Referenced in [[inbox/openrouter-provider-data-policies-2026-06-19]] regarding their provider data policy framework — zero-retention, training opt-out, and EU in-region routing for enterprise accounts.
- Featured in [[newsletters/msg-19edb0e0abd83fb0]] "Three features worth trying next" — onboarding email about streaming, structured outputs, and provider preferences.
- Referenced in [[newsletters/openrouter-data-policies-2026-06-19]] "How provider data policies work on OpenRouter".

## Related

- [[inbox/openrouter-provider-data-policies-2026-06-19]] — Newsletter detailing provider data policies and privacy controls
- [[newsletters/msg-19edb0e0abd83fb0]] — "Three features worth trying next" onboarding email
- [[newsletters/openrouter-data-policies-2026-06-19]] — "How provider data policies work on OpenRouter"

## Key Features

- **Streaming**: Set `stream=True` for token-by-token delivery, reducing perceived latency from seconds to first-token time.
- **Structured Outputs**: Pass a JSON schema via `response_format` for API-level enforcement — no regex parsing or retry loops needed.
- **Provider Preferences**: Control routing with `sort: "price"`, allowlists, latency floors, and throughput minimums.

<!-- timeline -->

## Timeline

- **2026-06-19** | Mentioned in "How provider data policies work on OpenRouter" — their onboarding email details zero-retention providers (AWS Bedrock, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, Together) and EU enterprise routing. [Source: email from OpenRouter Team, 2026-06-19]
- **2026-06-18** | "Three features worth trying next" onboarding email — highlights streaming (stream=True), structured outputs (response_format with JSON schema), and provider preferences (sort, allowlists, latency/throughput controls). All three can be combined in a single request. Recommends openrouter.ai/models, openrouter.ai/rankings, and openrouter.ai/activity. [Source: gmail://msg_19edb0e0abd83fb0]

pages.title: 15 Minute Gap Method 2026 06 14
pages.body:
# The 15-Minute Gap Method — Coaching Insight

**Source:** email from Zaim Mohzani re: Coaching insight — the 15-minute gap method
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eca04daacc7a09
**Ingested:** 2026-06-24T00:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Self-forward from Zaim to info+zaim@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-14

## Summary

Between KSI sessions, Zaim started using 15-minute gaps to jot down one observation about each student — what they're stuck on, what lit them up. After a month, clear patterns emerged.

## The 15-Minute Rule

Never let a gap go empty. Even one sentence per student compounds over time.

## Three Student Buckets

1. **Career pivoters** — stuck on "what if I fail" not "what do I want"
2. **Fresh grads** — over-indexing on salary, under-indexing on growth trajectory
3. **Mid-career** — all variations of "is this it?"

## Key Insight

Patterns emerged after a month that would never have been spotted otherwise. The method turns unstructured gap time into structural understanding of student archetypes.

pages.title: 3 Month Career Pivot Framework 2026 06 15
pages.body:
# The 3-Month Career Pivot Framework

Zaim shared this during a coaching session with a KSI senior analyst looking to move into product.

### Phase 1: Audit (Month 1)
- [[Skills Inventory]] — map current skills to target role requirements
- Identify the 20% skill gap that blocks 80% of interviews
- Don't learn everything — learn the gatekeepers

### Phase 2: Signal (Month 2)
- [[Side Project Strategy]] — build one public artifact, not ten private ones
- Write 2 LinkedIn posts/week documenting the pivot journey
- Engage with 3 target-company hiring managers

### Phase 3: Convert (Month 3)
- [[Salary Negotiation Playbook]] — comp bands for product roles in KL
- Reference calls with 2 people who made the same pivot
- First offer within 90 days

### Key Insight
> 'The career pivot gap isn't a skills problem — it's a positioning problem. You're not underqualified, you're framed wrong.' — Zaim

**Client:** KSI Senior Analyst → Product Manager
**Engagement:** Weekly coaching, 12 sessions
**Status:** Phase 2, on track

pages.title: Ai Data Center Architecture 2026 06 20
pages.body:
# AI Data Center Architecture — Why AI Breakthroughs Are Really Infrastructure Breakthroughs

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee23d6478fc08e
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T08:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by Sayf from System Design One newsletter (Neo Kim), 2026-06-20

**Original:** System Design One #155 by [[Neo Kim]] and [[Ashish Prajapati]]
**Date:** 2026-06-19

---

> **Key insight:** The biggest breakthroughs in AI are not coming from better models — they're coming from better infrastructure. AI data centers are factories, where GPUs are the factory floor machines and the network is the internal transport system.

---

## The Mental Model: AI Data Center as a Factory

- **AI Data Center = Factory**
- **Data = Raw material**
- **GPUs = Machines on the factory floor**
- **Network = Internal transport system**
- **Storage = Warehouse holding raw materials**
- **Software = Assembly line logic**
- **Output = Trained intelligence**

Remove any one ingredient and the entire system stalls.

---

## First Problem: Physics

Before AI is a software problem, it's a physics problem.

### 1. Power
A single server rack of GPUs can draw 80-120 kW — roughly 40 homes at full load. You're limited by how much electricity your facility can physically receive and distribute.

### 2. Cooling
More power = more heat. Modern data centers adopt **liquid cooling** — coolant circulated through cold plates attached directly to GPUs/CPUs, pumped to heat exchangers. Enables higher rack densities, lower cooling power consumption, and consistent performance at scale.

### 3. Space
Scaling AI is real estate, energy infrastructure, and thermodynamics combined, not just software.

---

## Compute: Why GPUs Took Over

- **CPU** = manager (a few powerful cores, complex sequential tasks, decision-making)
- **GPU** = factory workers (thousands of simpler cores, matrix multiplications in parallel)
- **DPU** = logistics (networking, storage I/O, security — keeps materials moving)

AI training repeatedly performs matrix multiplications billions of times. GPUs are purpose-built for this.

---

## Hidden Bottleneck: Memory and Storage

GPUs don't fail because they're slow. They fail because they're starved for data.

### Memory Hierarchy (speed vs capacity)

| Layer | Characteristics |
|-------|-----------------|
| GPU Memory (HBM) | Blazing fast but tiny (tens of GB) |
| CPU RAM | Hundreds of GB, slower than HBM |
| SSD/NVMe | Enormous capacity, slower still |
| Network/Object Storage | Nearly unlimited, slowest link |

### Tiered Storage in Practice

- **Hot:** NVMe SSDs — active training datasets, model checkpoints
- **Warm:** Parallel file systems (Lustre, GPFS) — high-speed shared access across many GPUs
- **Cold:** Object storage (Amazon S3) — raw datasets, older checkpoints

AI performance is determined by how fast data can be fed into the GPU, not just GPU compute speed.

---

## Networking: The Bottleneck Nobody Talks About

Thousands of GPUs must constantly communicate, sharing what they've learned at every step. Every second a GPU waits for data or synchronization is wasted investment.

### Cluster Topology

Leaf-spine network architectures — every GPU server reaches every other in at most 2 hops.

### Speed Hierarchy

- **NVLink** — fastest, a private highway between GPUs in the same server
- **InfiniBand/RoCE** — "bullet train" between separate servers
- **Ethernet** — general-purpose, not designed for AI's bandwidth/latency demands

### Amdahl's Law
Adding more GPUs increases computing power but also increases communication overhead. At a certain point, the bottleneck shifts from computation to communication.

### Smarter Data Movement Techniques

- **RDMA** — direct memory-to-memory transfers between servers, low-latency high-throughput
- **RDMA kernel bypass** — network adapter accesses application memory directly from user space
- **GPUDirect** — GPUs read/write to storage/network cards bypassing CPU entirely
- **Zero-copy pipelines** — data takes shortest path from source to GPU

The goal: make thousands of GPUs behave like one giant unified processor.

---

## People Mentioned

- [[Neo Kim]] — Author of The System Design Newsletter
- [[Ashish Prajapati]] — AI infrastructure specialist, technology educator; NVIDIA-Certified Associate; created BeSA (Become a Solutions Architect) mentoring program reaching 23,000+ professionals; training programs reached 39,000+ learners worldwide

---

## Related Concepts

- [[AI Data Centers]]
- [[GPU Infrastructure]]
- [[Distributed Training]]
- [[RDMA]]
- [[Liquid Cooling]]

pages.title: Aisyah Rahim Client Profile 2026 06 15
pages.body:
# Aisyah Rahim - Client Profile (Brand Manager at Unilever)

Zaim here. Adding a quick profile on a new client.

Name: Aisyah Rahim
Role: Senior Brand Manager at Unilever Malaysia
Context: Referred by Liyana from the KSI workshop last month. First session next Tuesday.

Key points from our intro call:
- 8 years in FMCG brand management, feeling stagnant
- Wants to pivot into tech but unsure where to start
- Main blocker: 'I don't have a technical background'
- Has actually done more data-driven work than she realises — ran consumer analytics for 3 product launches
- Salary anchor: RM 18k/month, worried about resetting to junior level

My read: Classic mid-career pivot case. The real problem isn't skills — it's confidence and narrative. She needs help reframing 'brand manager' as 'growth strategist who uses data.' Tech companies would kill for someone who can bridge consumer insights and product.

Plan for session 1: Audit her last 2 years of work through a tech lens. Find 3-5 specific examples where she already did 'product thinking' without calling it that.

— Zaim

pages.title: Agent Hierarchy

**Sammy** (profiles/sammy/) — Content creator. Handles LinkedIn posts, X threads, blog articles, brand voice, engagement drafting.

- **Accent**: #c4a86a (champagne gold)
- **Dark Navy**: #0a1628
- **Light backgrounds**: #f4f8fb, #ffffff

### Typography
- **Primary font**: Optima (humanist, elegant Roman forms)
- **Fallback stack**: 'Segoe UI', 'Candara', 'Palatino Linotype', 'Book Antiqua', Palatino, serif

### Brand Pattern
⬡ **Honeycomb Hexagon Grid** — Gold on Navy
- Hex lines: #c4a86a (champagne gold)
- Background field: #0a1628 (dark navy)
- Tile size: 90×52 px seamless tiling PNG
- Hosted at: https://iili.io/CK3nHLF.png
- Also available in 7 other colour variants (see skill references)

## Email Templates

The production email template is based on the "Ocean Deep" direction (selected from 5 brand concepts A-E).

### Template Structure
1. **Outer wrapper**: Gold-on-navy hex pattern background (via HTML `background` attribute on `<td>`, NOT CSS)
2. **Header**: Pattern visible with rgba(10,22,40,0.65) semi-transparent overlay → "MALOHA COAST" in white + "Sdn Bhd" in gold
3. **Body card**: White rounded card with generous padding
4. **Info cards**: Gold left accent border (#c4a86a) on light teal (#f4f8fb) background
5. **Signature**: Gold horizontal rule (#c4a86a) + name/title/company/tagline in brand colours
6. **Footer**: Same hex pattern background as header

### Gmail Compatibility (verified)
- Use HTML `background` attribute on `<td>` elements for patterns (CSS `background-image` with data URIs is stripped by Gmail)
- Use nested `<table>` with `rgba()` overlay for colour tinting
- Use inline styles only (no `<style>` blocks)
- Max width 600px for email card
- No JavaScript

### Skill Reference
The full workflow is in the `html-email-with-sheet-logging` skill:
- **SKILL.md**: /home/ubuntu/.hermes/skills/productivity/html-email-with-sheet-logging/SKILL.md
- **Production template**: references/ocean-deep-template.html
- **Header fragment**: templates/maloha-coast-header-fragment.html
- **Hex pattern variants**: references/hex-pattern-variants.md
- **Script to regenerate**: scripts/generate-hex-pattern.py

### Brand Concepts File
Full brand exploration HTML (5 palettes, 12 patterns, typography pairings):
/home/ubuntu/maloha-coast-brand-concepts.html

### Related
- [[maloha-coast]] — company overview
- [[google-workspace-setup]] — email infrastructure

# Career Coaching: The 3-Month Pivot Framework

**Source:** Email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eca58bcdb09c70
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T06:19:23Z
**Provenance:** +zaim routed — Zaim's client coaching material

Shared by Zaim during a coaching session with a KSI senior analyst looking to move into product.

## The 3-Month Career Pivot Framework

### Phase 1: Audit (Month 1)
- Skills Inventory — map current skills to target role requirements
- Identify the 20% skill gap that blocks 80% of interviews
- Don't learn everything — learn the gatekeepers

### Phase 2: Signal (Month 2)
- Side Project Strategy — build one public artifact, not ten private ones
- Write 2 LinkedIn posts/week documenting the pivot journey
- Engage with 3 target-company hiring managers

### Phase 3: Convert (Month 3)
- Salary Negotiation Playbook — comp bands for product roles in KL
- Reference calls with 2 people who made the same pivot
- First offer within 90 days

### Key Insight
> "The career pivot gap isn't a skills problem — it's a positioning problem. You're not underqualified, you're framed wrong." — Zaim

**Client:** KSI Senior Analyst → Product Manager
**Engagement:** Weekly coaching, 12 sessions
**Status:** Phase 2, on track

[Source: Zaim's coaching material, 2026-06-15]

pages.title: Claude Code 10x Skill Newsletter
pages.body:
# Fwd: The Skill That 10x'd My Claude Code Projects
**From:** Sayf Codes <sayfcodes@gmail.com>
**Date:** Mon, 8 Jun 2026 23:49:48 +0800
**Source:** email

> Forwarded message from Nate Herk <nate@aiautomationsociety.ai>

June 05, 2026 — AI Automation Society Newsletter

## The Skill That 10x'd My Claude Code Projects

Sharper Claude context, Carson's first automation win, and Anthropic's $965B IPO filing

### In This Issue
- The single technique that makes every Claude project sound less like everyone else and more like you
- Carson landed his first automation client with a free pilot — here's the exact move
- Anthropic just filed for IPO at $965 billion — what it means if Claude is your primary tool

### New Video: The Skill That 10x'd My Claude Code Projects

If your Claude outputs feel generic — technically correct but missing your voice — the problem is not the model. Everyone using Claude Opus 4.8 starts from the same foundation. The difference between outputs that sound like you and ones that sound like everyone else is context.

**The Grill Me skill** fixes that. Instead of a five-minute brain dump before you start building, it interviews you until it actually understands how you think. It asks one question at a time, checkpoints your answers to a markdown file in your project, and keeps going until there are no gaps. By the end, you have reusable context your AI pulls from every time you touch that skill or project.

**The mistake most people make:** Starting with a brain dump and calling it good enough. Five minutes of info-dumping gets your output quality to about 70% on iteration one. The Grill Me approach gets you to 90% from the start — fewer revision cycles, faster time to a deliverable clients actually like.

**How this helps you:** When Claude knows your voice, your decisions, and your process, it stops generating generic output. Your projects feel like yours. Clients notice. You spend less time fixing what the AI got wrong.

### Winning Is All We Do — [[Carson]] landed his first automation client

**What happened:** [[Carson]] built a lead automation for a local aesthetics clinic as a free pilot. The workflow routes contact data and handles follow-ups automatically. He's waiting on Facebook credentials to go live, but the clinic owner is already highly receptive. His plan: deliver the pilot, then position for a paid monthly retainer.

**The opportunity:** Local service businesses (clinics, salons, studios) are some of the easiest first clients to land. They have real lead problems, a low technology bar, and if you deliver, they talk to other business owners. A free pilot with a clear upsell path is one of the cleanest ways to get your first paying automation client.

### In the News — [[Anthropic]] filed for IPO at $965 billion

**The news:** On June 1, 2026, [[Anthropic]] — the company behind Claude — confidentially submitted an S-1 to the SEC for a proposed initial public offering. Valuation: $965 billion, putting it ahead of [[OpenAI]] ($852 billion after their last funding round). [[Anthropic]] joins SpaceX and [[OpenAI]] in what analysts are already calling a race to go public — all three expected to list this year.

**How it affects automation:** When your primary AI tool's parent company goes public, two things typically follow: more capital to build better models (good for you), and quarterly earnings pressure that tends to push enterprise-first roadmaps and price adjustments upward. This is not a reason to panic. It is a reason to build your skills and client base now, before the landscape shifts again.

### AIS Live — July 11-12, 2026

[[AI Automation Society]] hosts a two-day event where real practitioners walk through real client engagements on stage. Day 1: see the exact projects being sold right now and build one live. Day 2: exactly how to get clients, even if you're a beginner with no credibility yet. General Admission starts at $97 (early bird ends June 7).

### People Mentioned
- [[Nate Herk]] — Author of the original newsletter from [[AI Automation Society]]
- [[Carson]] — Practitioner who landed first automation client

### Companies Mentioned
- [[AI Automation Society]] — Publisher
- [[Anthropic]] — Filed IPO at $965B
- [[OpenAI]] — $852B valuation

---

*From: AI Automation Society (beehiiv)*

pages.title: ClawHub
pages.body:
# ClawHub

> Modular skill marketplace for [[OpenClaw]], hosting 5,400+ community-contributed skills. Skills plug into the OpenClaw agent framework via lifecycle hooks (before/after tool calls, messages, session start/end).

## Overview

ClawHub is the ecosystem of modular, composable skills that extend [[OpenClaw]]'s
capabilities. Skills are published and shared by the community, covering use
cases from automation and scraping to integration with external services.

## Architecture

Skills hook into OpenClaw's plugin system at lifecycle events:
- Before/after tool calls
- Before/after messages
- Session start/end

This enables audit logging, rate limiting, custom approval flows, and any
other extension without modifying core OpenClaw code.

## Related

- [[OpenClaw]] — the open-source AI agent framework ClawHub extends
- [[The Anatomy of OpenClaw]] — newsletter issue profiling OpenClaw and mentioning ClawHub's 5,400+ skills

## Timeline
