pages.title: Coaching 15 Minute Gap Method 2026 06 14
pages.body:
# Coaching Insight — The 15-Minute Gap Method

**Source:** email from Zaim Mohzani
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eca04daacc7a09
**Ingested:** 2026-06-25T14:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Self-forwarded by Zaim from info+zaim@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-14

## Summary

Zaim's coaching practice discovery: using the 15-minute gaps between KSI sessions to jot down one observation per student. After a month, patterns emerged that wouldn't have been visible otherwise.

## The Method

- **Rule:** Never let a gap go empty. Even one sentence per student compounds.
- **What to capture:** What they're stuck on, what lit them up
- **Timeframe:** 15-minute gaps between KSI sessions
- **Material:** One sentence per student per gap

## Patterns That Emerged (3 Buckets)

1. **Career pivoters** — stuck on "what if I fail" not "what do I want"
2. **Fresh grads** — over-indexing on salary, under-indexing on growth trajectory
3. **Mid-career** — all variations of "is this it?"

## Relevance

This is a lightweight but structured observation method that compounds into real pattern recognition. Could be applicable to coaching practice management at Maloha Coast.

pages.title: Cold Lead Response Conference Events
pages.body:
**Context:** Response to a conference producer who asked for examples after a cold outreach. Crafted for LinkedIn DM — punchy, concrete, pilot-friendly.

## The Response

Thanks for asking. Here are some practical ways I think a Hermes agent could fit into your events workflow if we set one up on your laptop or VPS:

**Speaker sourcing:** You feed it your target criteria once, and it scans hundreds of profiles, past talks, and decks. It comes back with a shortlist plus solid briefing notes in about 15 minutes instead of 3–4 hours of manual digging.

**Post-session repurposing:** Right after a talk, you drop in the recording or transcript and it generates a full LinkedIn article, 3-5 social posts, a newsletter summary, and key highlights. Turns one session into 30+ usable pieces.

**Sponsor deliverables:** It pulls from your existing docs and data to create pre-event copy, staff briefs, lead reports, and post-event ROI summaries — cutting out a big chunk of the 20 hours per sponsor tier your team usually spends.

**Business brain / company memory:** I'm actually building one of these for myself right now. We can set up a "gbrain" where the agent learns your past events, speakers, sponsors, what worked and what didn't. Then you can just ask it things like "What sponsors performed best last year?" or "Give me the checklist for a 500-person event" and it pulls from your own history.

**Closer:** Which one sounds like it would save you the most time or headache right now? Happy to walk through any of them in more detail or hop on a quick call to map it to how you currently work.

## Notes

- Crafted to be realistic, specific, and pilot-friendly
- No AI jargon — pitched as a tool they control
- Each example maps to a known pain point for conference producers
- The gbrain example doubles as a subtle demo of the product in use

pages.title: Company Memory Os Ph1 Prd 2026 06 09
pages.body:
# Company Memory OS (Phase 1 PRD)

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eaa49d639a6a73
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T04:30:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by Sayf Codes to info@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-09

A compact PRD for a **Company Memory Operating System** designed for Hermes to execute against. Initial delivery is service-first, software-second. The goal is not to sell software access — it's to deliver the outcome: "Never lose important decisions, context, relationships, or knowledge again."

## Problem

Small businesses, consultants, agencies, and founders lose critical knowledge because it is scattered across email, meeting transcripts, documents, chat conversations, and notes. Knowledge becomes unrecoverable after weeks or months. Current solutions focus on storage — users need retrieval and context.

## Target Customer

**Primary:** Consultants (20-100 clients, high email volume, multiple projects, knowledge-intensive work). **Secondary:** Agency owners, startup founders, fractional executives, professional services firms.

## Core Value Proposition

Transform company communications into a searchable memory system. Input: Emails, meeting transcripts, documents. Output: Searchable knowledge base, decision history, relationship intelligence, project memory, AI-assisted retrieval.

## Initial Offer: Productized Service (not SaaS)

Human-assisted onboarding. Knowledge Audit: customer provides emails, documents, meeting notes → system generates executive summary, project map, key decisions, action items, stakeholder map, recurring themes, knowledge gaps. Deliverable: Interactive dashboard + report.

## Success Metric

Customer says: "I found something valuable that I would not have found otherwise."

## MVP Scope

**Ingestion:** Email forwarding, Gmail export, meeting transcripts, markdown documents, PDFs.
**Processing:** Extract people, companies, projects, decisions, tasks, dates, relationships. Generate summaries, tags, backlinks, knowledge graph connections.
**Retrieval:** Natural language queries (What was decided about Project X? What commitments were made to Client Y? Show all discussions involving Person Z. What unresolved action items exist?)
**Dashboard:** Decision Timeline, Relationship Graph, Project Knowledge Base, Open Actions, Search.

## Go-To-Market

Acquire first 3 paying customers. Offer: Free Knowledge Audit. Target existing network (founders, consultants, agency owners). Message: "I've built a system that turns emails, meetings, and documents into a searchable company memory. Looking for 3 companies to test it with."

## Pricing Experiments

- **Stage 1:** Knowledge Audit — RM300-RM1000 (one-time)
- **Stage 2:** Continuous Memory Service — RM300-RM1000/month (ongoing ingestion, dashboard updates, search, reports)
- **Stage 3:** SaaS — only after 10+ paying customers, clear repeatable workflow, proven retention

## Strategic Principle

Do not optimize for software. Optimize for customer outcomes. Use Hermes + GBrain internally as operational infrastructure. The platform emerges from repeated customer success, not from building features first.

## North Star

Become the memory layer for small organizations. Long-term vision: Communication → Knowledge Graph → Institutional Memory → Organizational Intelligence.

[Source: email from Sayf Codes re: "Hermes Agent to ingest", 2026-06-09]

## Related

- [[Maloha Coast Navigator]] — Weekly AI strategy newsletter

pages.title: Embedding Cost Projection
pages.body:
## Source Data

- **CSV:** `/home/ubuntu/Downloads/openai_cost_2026-05-04_2026-06-03.csv`
- **Date of run:** June 3, 2026
- **Model:** `text-embedding-3-large` ($0.00013 / 1K tokens)
- **Pages embedded:** 6 brain pages (total 6,838 chars)
- **Tokens consumed:** 4,633
- **Cost:** $0.000602 USD

## Per-Page Average

~772 tokens/page → ~$0.0001/page

## Projection

| Pages | Est. Tokens | Est. Cost |
|-------|-------------|-----------|
| 6 (current) | 4.6K | $0.0006 |
| 50 | 38K | $0.005 |
| 100 | 77K | $0.01 |
| 1,000 | 772K | $0.10 |
| 10,000 | 7.7M | $1.00 |
| 100,000 | 77M | $10.00 |

## Key Insight

The real bottleneck is content curation (writing meaningful pages), not API pricing. Even embedding the full text of *War and Peace* (~775K tokens) would cost only ~$0.10.

## Verified

- 4,633 tokens × $0.00013/K = $0.00060229 ✓
- Cost scales linearly with token count
- No minimum or fixed fees on text-embedding-3-large

pages.title: Gateway Infrastructure
pages.body:
# Gateway Infrastructure

## Hardware
2GB VPS (Tencent Cloud, Ubuntu, 6.8.0-124). 2GB swap. ~50GB storage.

## Hermes Gateway

### Service
Systemd user service at `~/.config/systemd/user/hermes-gateway.service`. Auto-starts on boot. Linger enabled so it survives logout.

### Exit Code 75 (EX_TEMPFAIL)
The gateway uses exit code 75 as a "please restart me" signal to systemd. Triggered by `/restart`, `/update`, or SIGUSR1. The systemd config has `RestartForceExitStatus=75` so this always triggers a restart. See [[gateway-watchdog]] for crash-loop detection.

### Memory Profile
- Baseline: ~97MB RSS at startup
- After 14h moderate use: ~600MB (growth ~36MB/h)
- Peak observed: 1.0G
- Gradual leak under active use — tracked by watchdog growth-rate alert

### Manual Recovery
When stuck in a crash loop: `sudo -u ubuntu /home/ubuntu/.local/bin/hermes gateway start`

## Gateway Health Watchdog

Created Jun 4, 2026. A Python script at `~/.hermes/scripts/gateway-watchdog.py` that runs every 10 minutes via cron (`no_agent=True`, zero LLM tokens). Silent when healthy; alerts Discord #Home when thresholds are breached.

### Thresholds
- Gateway RSS >= 1.4GB (warning) / >= 1.6GB (critical)
- System free memory <= 300MB (warning) / <= 150MB (critical)
- Swap usage > 200MB
- >= 1 exit code 75 crash in last hour
- >= 1 SIGTERM kill in last hour
- Gateway uptime < 30 min
- Memory growth rate > 50MB/hour

### Related
- [[VPS Hardening]] — SSH, firewall, fail2ban baseline
- [[2026-06-04]] — watchdog setup and gateway crash investigation

pages.title: Google Workspace Intelligence 2026 04 22
pages.body:
# Google Workspace Intelligence — Product Update

**Source:** email from The Google Workspace Team, 2026-04-22
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19db821143bc39be
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T12:45:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded to info@malohacoast.com (Maloha Coast admin account)

Google introduced **Workspace Intelligence**, an underlying AI system that provides Gemini with a real-time understanding of work across Google Workspace (Gmail, Chat, Calendar, Drive).

## What It Does

Workspace Intelligence grounds all generative AI tasks in the user's Workspace data, eliminating the need to manually provide context to Gemini on every query. Example: asking Gemini in Docs to draft an update on "Project X" — it consults meeting notes, documentation, chat discussions, and produces a draft with full citations.

## Admin Controls (Effective April 22, 2026)

- New Admin Console settings allow control over which data sources (Gmail, Chat, Calendar, or Drive) Workspace Intelligence can use, at the organizational unit and group level.
- **Default ON** for all users.
- Data is never used to train generative AI models or for advertising.
- AI features respect user-level content access — responses are grounded only in content the user already has permission to view.
- Turning off a data source means Gemini won't actively search it, but users can still prompt about specific files from that source.
- Turning off certain data sources may cause users to lose access to specific AI features.

## Impact for Maloha Coast

As the Google Workspace admin for malohacoast.com, Sayf should review the new admin controls to decide whether to keep Workspace Intelligence default ON or restrict specific data sources.

pages.title: Hermes Profile Backup
pages.body:
## Hermes Profile Backup

The Hermes profile backup pushes all agent profiles to a private GitHub repo with secrets stripped.

### Repository
- **Repo:** `github.com/Maloha-Coast/hermes-profile-backup` (private)
- **Contents:** 1,186 files across 2 profiles (default + MC)
- **Includes:** SOUL.md, skills, cron, memories, config.yaml (stripped), setup.sh, .gitignore

### What gets backed up
- SOUL.md (agent identity)
- skills/ (all skill files)
- cron/ (scheduled jobs)
- memories/ (USER.md + MEMORY.md)
- config.yaml with API keys replaced by <YOUR_KEY_HERE>

### What gets EXCLUDED
- API keys, tokens, secrets (replaced with placeholders)
- Session logs and databases
- OAuth tokens (google_token.json)
- Runtime state files

### Restore
```bash
git clone git@github.com:Maloha-Coast/hermes-profile-backup.git
cd hermes-profile-backup
./setup.sh
```
Then edit ~/.hermes/config.yaml and ~/.hermes/.env with credentials.

### Known gap
- .env.template is missing from the current backup (README references it but file was not generated). Needs to be added on next backup run.

---

[[HumanLayer]] has launched publicly. It's an Agentic IDE and collaboration platform for engineering teams to design complex systems with AI agents before generating code.

### Key Details

- **Product:** [[HumanLayer]] — Agentic IDE, collaboration platform, and building blocks for the software factory
- **Pricing:** Starter plan free for teams of fewer than three
- **URL:** [humanlayer.com](https://humanlayer.com)
- **Launch post (blurb):** [humanlayer.com/launch/blurb.md](https://humanlayer.com/launch/blurb.md)
- **X post:** [@dexhorthy launch thread](https://x.com/dexhorthy/status/2067286892786454855)
- **LinkedIn:** [[Dex Horthy]] launch post

### Core Thesis

> The year of no more slop.

[[HumanLayer]]'s core idea: **don't outsource the thinking.** Great teams have run design reviews for decades because there's enormous leverage in collaborating on system design *before* writing the code. [[HumanLayer]] brings that missing piece of the software factory into one place.

### What It Does

- Lets engineers move 2-3x faster across the entire SDLC (not just coding)
- Maintains rigorous standards for system architecture and program design
- Engineers design complex systems with AI agents **before** generating a single line of code
- **BYO subscription** — plug in Claude, Codex, and other AI subscriptions/API keys; no separate per-token bill

### Background

[[Dex Horthy]] and [[Kyle]] (the founders) previously built the open-source Research, Plan, Implement (RPI) framework, now running inside Fortune 500s like Block and Uber. [[HumanLayer]] is built on top of all that work.

pages.title: Karpathy Llm Wiki Pattern 2026 06 20
pages.body:
# Karpathy's LLM Wiki Pattern

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee3343b6e5b9e9
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T12:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Shared by sayf on 2026-06-20, gist by Andrej Karpathy

## Link

https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f

## Summary

Andrej Karpathy's "LLM Wiki" describes a pattern for building personal knowledge bases using LLMs. Instead of using RAG (where an LLM re-discovers knowledge from raw documents on every query), the LLM incrementally builds and maintains a persistent wiki — a structured, interlinked collection of markdown files that sits between the user and the raw sources.

## Core Idea

Most people's experience with LLMs and documents looks like RAG: upload files, LLM retrieves chunks at query time. The LLM is rediscovering knowledge from scratch on every question. No accumulation.

Instead, the LLM builds a **persistent wiki** — a structured, interlinked collection of markdown files. When you add a new source, the LLM reads it, extracts key information, and integrates it into the existing wiki — updating entity pages, revising topic summaries, noting where new data contradicts old claims. The knowledge is compiled once and kept current, not re-derived on every query.

This mirrors the gbrain architecture used in this very ecosystem. Key insight: *the wiki is a persistent, compounding artifact.*

## Architecture — Three Layers

1. **Raw sources** — immutable source documents (articles, papers, etc.)
2. **The wiki** — LLM-generated markdown files (summaries, entity pages, concept pages)
3. **The schema** — config document (like CLAUDE.md) telling the LLM how the wiki is structured

## Operations

- **Ingest** — Drop a new source, LLM reads it, writes summaries, updates index and entity pages
- **Query** — Ask questions against the wiki; good answers can be filed back as new pages
- **Lint** — Periodically health-check: contradictions, stale claims, orphans, missing cross-references

## Relevance to Maloha Coast

This is highly relevant — the gbrain knowledge base at Maloha Coast follows essentially this same pattern. Karpathy's framing validates the architecture: persistent wiki (gbrain pages), LLM-maintained cross-references (wikilinks), source provenance tracking. The key difference: gbrain uses embedding-based search for query while Karpathy suggests an index.md file approach at moderate scale.

## Related

- gbrain — similar persistent wiki architecture
- workspace-intake — ingestion pipeline that mirrors Karpathy's "ingest" operation
- second-brain concept — overlaps with the Claude second-brain levels from the AI Automation Society newsletter

pages.title: Knowledge Graph As Moat
pages.body:
# Knowledge Graph as Moat

**Synthesized:** 2026-06-09
**Sources:** Graphify video (Jack, YouTube), email-to-gbrain pipeline benchmarks, Company Memory OS PRD

## The Pattern

The same architectural pattern works across two different domains:

| Layer | Code Domain (Graphify) | Business Domain (Company Memory OS / gbrain) |
|---|---|---|
| **Ingest** | Clone GitHub repo | Forward email, upload transcript |
| **Parse** | Read code, cluster into modules | Extract entities, detect relationships |
| **Graph** | Files → modules → god nodes | People → companies → deals → decisions |
| **Query** | "What depends on X?" | "What was decided about Project X?" |
| **Dashboard** | Interactive circle graph | gbrain dashboard + architecture diagram |
| **Agent** | Claude Code + Hermes | Hermes + gbrain |
| **Compounding** | Every session builds context | Every email ingested enriches memory |

## Core Insight

Both systems solve the same fundamental problem: **"rereading is the tax."**

- In code: re-reading the repo every conversation costs tokens and produces shallower answers
- In business: re-searching emails/meetings every time you need context costs time and lets decisions fall through cracks

The knowledge graph IS the moat. Once a codebase or a business has been mapped:
- Answers come from summaries, not re-parsing
- Dependencies and blast radius are visible before making changes  
- Context compounds across sessions
- Switching costs increase dramatically — the graph IS the switching cost

## Why This Validates Company Memory OS

Jack's video pitches Graphify as worth paying for because it saves Claude Code users money and produces better results. The same value proposition applies to Company Memory OS:

- For a consultant with 50 clients: "What commitments did I make to Client Y?" answered in <500ms
- For an agency owner: "Show me all discussions about the Q2 budget" without digging through email
- For a founder: "Who did I meet at the June conference and what did we discuss?"

The architecture is proven. The pattern is validated. The question is execution and positioning.

## Key References

- [[media/videos/graphify-solves-claude-code-problem]] — Full video analysis with chapter index, quotes, and architecture breakdown
- [[concepts/company-memory-os-ph1-prd]] — Phase 1 PRD for the Company Memory OS productized service
- The email-to-gbrain pipeline (cron job `776c7bf32c77`) is the working MVP of Phase 1

## Client Pitch Analogy

"What Graphify does for code — instant understanding of any codebase without reading through it — we do for your business communications. Forward an email. Get a searchable memory. Never lose a decision again."

pages.title: Graphify Solves Claude Code Problem
pages.body:
# Graphify Solves Claude Code's Problem

**Source:** YouTube video by Jack, AI automation channel
**URL:** https://youtu.be/Owv503rTqYY
**Ingested:** 2026-06-09
**Type:** tutorial + product pitch

## Executive Summary

Graphify is a tool that creates a knowledge graph of any codebase — mapping files, modules, "god nodes" (load-bearing files), and their interdependencies. This gives Claude (or any AI agent) a persistent, queryable map so it doesn't re-read the entire repo every conversation. The core insight: "rereading is the tax we're saving" — the graph lets the AI answer from summaries instead of re-parsing flat code. When connected to an agentic operating system (Hermes + Claude Code + a visual dashboard), it becomes a shared brain that compounds across sessions and devices.

## The Core Idea

Graphify treats every codebase as a foreign country. Without it, you're translating a language you don't speak. With it, you have a map — you understand relationships, dependencies, and what affects what before editing anything.

### What the Map Does
- **Instant orientation** — understand any repo immediately without reading through it
- **Grounded answers** — queries pull from the graph, not guesses from skimming
- **Blast radius awareness** — see every dependency before editing a file
- **Token savings** — avoid re-reading the repo each conversation; answer from summaries
- **Always fresh** — the graph is regenerated, not stale

### How It Works
1. Reads code (does not summarize)
2. Clusters things into modules
3. Ranks "god nodes" — load-bearing files
4. Labels facts vs. guesses (extracted vs. inferred)

## Technical Architecture

- **Input:** Any codebase (Claude Code, Hermes, GitHub repos)
- **Output:** Knowledge graph with interactive visualization (community clusters)
- **Integration:** Works as a Claude Code skill; connects to Hermes Agent
- **Dashboard:** Visual interface showing files, links, clusters, and cost savings per conversation

## The Agentic Operating System Layer

The video's key argument is that Graphify alone is useful, but Graphify + an agentic OS is the 10x unlock. The OS provides:

- **One shared brain:** Graphify, Claude Code, Hermes, and the dashboard all read from one registry
- **Cross-device continuity:** Work on laptop with Claude Code, then query from mobile via Hermes — same graph
- **Any repo ingestion:** Paste a GitHub URL → "graphify it" → $0 cost → queryable immediately
- **Overnight reviews:** The OS runs dream cycles, searches for improvements, processes chat history
- **Hermes Telegram integration:** Hermes can reach out proactively with questions

## Key Quotes

- "Rereading is the tax that we're saving here" (3:04)
- "Without the map, it skims a whole repo every single conversation" (3:10)
- "With the map, we can actually answer from summaries — every session compounds" (3:13)
- "Graphify makes the map and the OS makes it always on, shared, and conversational" (7:02)
- "If you're not using an agentic operating system, you're leaving so much value on the table" (10:04)

## People Mentioned

- **Jack** — Video creator, AI automation YouTuber. Previously built and sold a tech startup "with a gazillion customers." Now building AI businesses. Runs a community with Claude Code + Hermes operating system templates.

## Tools/Technologies Mentioned

- **Graphify** — Knowledge graph generator for codebases (GitHub repo, open source)
- **Claude Code** — Primary coding agent that Graphify enhances
- **Hermes Agent** — Agent framework connected to the operating system
- **OpenCor** — Mentioned as part of the cross-device connectivity stack
- **Anti-Gravity** — Referenced as part of the agent ecosystem
- **Codex** — OpenAI Codex, also integrated into the OS

## Relevance to Maloha Coast

The Graphify pattern directly parallels the email-to-gbrain / Company Memory OS architecture:

| Graphify Pattern | gbrain/Company Memory OS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Knowledge graph of code files | Knowledge graph of people, companies, decisions |
| "God nodes" = key files | "God nodes" = key relationships/deals |
| Query: "what depends on X?" | Query: "what was decided about Project X?" |
| Claude Code integration | Hermes Agent + gbrain integration |
| Visual dashboard | gbrain dashboard + architecture diagram |
| Compound context across sessions | Compound institutional memory across emails |

The video validates the architecture sayf has already built: knowledge graph + agentic layer + visual dashboard + autonomous pipeline. It's a compelling analogy for client pitches — "what Graphify does for code, Company Memory OS does for your business."

## Chapter Index

- 00:00 — Chapter 1: Graphify Solves Claude's Problem (hook + promise)
- 00:46 — Chapter 2: What Graphify Actually Does (the map metaphor)
- 01:21 — Chapter 3: How The Map Works (clustering, god nodes, facts vs guesses)
- 02:10 — Chapter 4: Why Maps Save Tokens (orientation, blast radius, re-reading tax)
- 03:17 — Chapter 5: Install And Set Up (clone repo, Claude Code integration)
- 04:38 — Chapter 6: See The Knowledge Graph (interactive visualization)
- 05:04 — Chapter 7: Query Any Repo Live (prompt example, cost comparison)
- 05:43 — Chapter 8: Take It Further (agentic operating system intro)
- 06:07 — Chapter 9: Inside The Operating System (dashboard, Hermes integration)
- 07:32 — Chapter 10: Import Any GitHub Repo (one-click ingestion)
- 08:07 — Chapter 11: Chat To Your Projects (conversational interface)
- 09:04 — Chapter 12: Connect Everything Together (shared brain architecture)
- 10:04 — Chapter 13: Visual Interface Wins (closing argument)

---

*Raw transcript extracted from YouTube timestamps provided by user (VPS IP blocked by YouTube, transcript retrieved via user-supplied text).*

pages.title: OpenClaw
pages.body:
# OpenClaw

> Open-source autonomous AI agent framework that bridges LLM reasoning (thinking layer) with real-world execution (act layer). Developed as an always-on daemon with multi-channel messaging support, plugin architecture, and layered memory system.

## Architecture Layers

### Gateway
Always-on Node.js server on port 18789. Coordinates messaging apps, LLM, tools,
and memory. Intercepts destructive commands for approval. Serves Control UI at
localhost:18789. Supports hot config reloads. Accessible via Tailscale from any
device.

### Channel Layer
Plugin-based messaging abstraction — each platform (Telegram, WhatsApp, Slack,
Discord) gets a translation adapter. Standardised internal format makes everything
after this boundary platform-agnostic.

### LLM Layer
Model-agnostic ("brain socket"). System prompt assembled from agent personality +
skills + memory + conversation history. Tool call loop with context compaction at
limit.

### Plugin System
Almost everything non-core is a plugin. Hooks into lifecycle events
(before/after tool calls, messages, session start/end). Enables audit logging,
rate limiting, custom approval flows without touching core.

### Memory System
Three-layer architecture: short-term (session files on disk), long-term (indexed
local DB searched before every answer), standing notes (permanent briefing
Markdown files).

### Long-Running Tasks
Long tasks acknowledged immediately, worked on asynchronously, state persisted to
disk. Cron jobs in isolated session mode for recurring background work.

## Key Differentiators

- Always-on daemon, not browser-tab-reliant
- 5,400+ modular skills on [[ClawHub]]
- Multi-channel with shared context
- Proactive via Heartbeat + cron
- Model-agnostic (any LLM provider)

## Related

- [[The Anatomy of OpenClaw]] — deep-dive newsletter article describing the architecture
- [[ClawHub]] — skill marketplace with 5,400+ modular skills
- [[Neo Kim]] — author of the System Design Newsletter issue profiling OpenClaw
- [[System Design One]] — Substack publication that published the architecture deep-dive

## Timeline

- **2026-06-08** | Profiled in System Design Newsletter #151 "The Anatomy of OpenClaw" by Neo Kim. [Source: gmail://msg_19ea8014fa960cb7]

pages.title: Openrouter Auto Model Routing 2026 06 10
pages.body:
# openrouter/auto: per-request model routing

**Source:** Email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eb1db6efeaffa6
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T21:04:57Z
**Provenance:** Email from OpenRouter Team <welcome@openrouter.ai> to info@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-10

One model name. The router picks per request based on task complexity. No routing fee.

OpenRouter
OpenRouter ( https://openrouter.ai/?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A3+Auto+Router&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )

Hi there,

Picking the right model for every request is a job. openrouter/auto is the model name you use when you want OpenRouter to do that job for you. Pass it as the model on any request. The router classifies the request, picks a model from the auto pool based on task complexity, and runs it. You pay the standard rate for whichever model serves the response. There is no routing fee.

See the auto pool ( https://openrouter.ai/models/openrouter/auto?utm_source=email&utm_medium=lifecycle&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=a2_auto_routing&utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A3+Auto+Router&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )

response = client.chat.completions.create
(   model="openrouter/auto",
messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarize this document"}], )

print(response.model)  # which model actually served the request

A few things worth knowing:

A simple classification does not need a frontier model. A nuanced analysis does not belong on a small one. The router handles the call without you writing any branching logic.

Roughly 63 percent of auto traffic routes to models priced under $1 per million tokens. That is how simple prompts stop hitting expensive models without any routing code on your side, and it is the reason most teams see lower per-request cost on auto than on a single pinned frontier model.

You can still pass route="fallback" alongside auto if you want a named backstop on top of automatic selection. If the auto pick fails, the fallback list runs in order until one returns a response.

The auto pool changes as new models launch. You do not have to update your code when it does. Read response.model in the response if you want to see what the router picked on any given call, which is useful for logging and for spotting cost drift.

When not to use it. If you already know the exact model you want for a given call, pin the model string directly. Auto is for the 80 percent of traffic where the choice does not matter, not the 20 percent where it does. Most apps end up using both.

The next email covers what changes when you add credits to your account.

pages.title: Openrouter Chain Models 2026 06 16
pages.body:
# Chain models for complex tasks

**Source:** Email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ed0c0b31514494
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T21:04:51Z
**Provenance:** Email from OpenRouter Team <welcome@openrouter.ai> to info@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-16

Use a fast model for classification, a strong model for generation. One endpoint handles both.

OpenRouter
OpenRouter ( https://openrouter.ai/?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A7+Model+Chaining&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )

Hi there,

Use a fast model for classification, a strong model for generation. Same endpoint, same auth, different model parameter. That is the point of having 300+ models on one API: you mix them per step instead of committing to one for the whole workload.

Browse models ( https://openrouter.ai/models?utm_source=email&utm_medium=lifecycle&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=a11_multi_model&utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A7+Model+Chaining&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )

PATTERN 1: CLASSIFY, THEN GENERATE

# Step 1: classify with a fast, cheap model
classify = client.chat.completions.create
(   model="openai/gpt-4o-mini",
messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Classify: {query}"}], )

# Step 2: generate with a strong model
result = client.chat.completions.create
(   model="anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6",
messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"{classify.choices[0].message.content}: {query}"}], )

GPT-4o-mini runs around $0.15 per million input tokens. Claude Sonnet 4.6 runs around $3 per million. Using the cheap model for routing and the expensive one only where it earns its cost can cut total spend 5 to 10x on mixed workloads.

PATTERN 2: EXTRACT, THEN WRITE

# Step 1: structured extraction
extracted = client.chat.completions.create
(   model="openai/gpt-4o-mini",
response_format={"type": "json_object"},
messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Extract fields as JSON: {doc}"}], )

# Step 2: long-form writing from the structured fields
draft = client.chat.completions.create
(   model="anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6",
messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Write a report from: {extracted.choices[0].message.content}"}], )

Structured outputs are cheap and fast on small models. Long-form generation is where the frontier models actually earn their per-token rate. Splitting the work keeps the expensive calls short.

Why this works on OpenRouter specifically. One endpoint means no separate SDK per provider, no second billing account, no new authentication. The model parameter is the only thing that changes between steps, and the activity dashboard shows both calls side by side so you can see exactly where the cost went.

openrouter.ai ( https://openrouter.ai?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A7+Model+Chaining&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )  ·  Discord ( https://discord.com/invite/8TpuzNXQYr?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A7+Model+Chaining&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )  ·  X ( https://x.com/openrouter?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A7+Model+Chaining&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )  ·  Docs ( https://openrouter.ai/docs?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A7+Model+Chaining&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )

pages.body:
# Your coding agent + 300 models

**Source:** Email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ecb973b2a360e4
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T21:04:54Z
**Provenance:** Email from OpenRouter Team <welcome@openrouter.ai> to info@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-15

Cursor, Cline, Continue, Claude Code. All work with one OpenRouter key.

OpenRouter
OpenRouter ( https://openrouter.ai/?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A6+Coding+Agents&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )

Hi there,

Coding agents are the fastest-growing use case on OpenRouter. One API key gives your agent access to every model, with automatic provider fallback if one goes down. You stop maintaining four provider accounts and one consolidated usage view replaces four separate dashboards.

See integrations ( https://openrouter.ai/docs/frameworks?utm_source=email&utm_medium=lifecycle&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=b9_agents&utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A6+Coding+Agents&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )

The major agents already speak OpenRouter.

Cursor. Drop your OpenRouter key into Settings and pick any model from the dropdown. Switch between Claude, GPT, Gemini, and DeepSeek per task without leaving the editor.
Cline. Autonomous coding with auto-routing. The agent picks the right model per step, so planning runs on a thinking model and execution runs on something faster and cheaper.
Continue. Open-source, configurable. Point the base URL at OpenRouter and every model is available from the model picker.
Claude Code. Use OpenRouter as the backend to track team usage, set per-key rate limits, and control spend across models without touching Anthropic billing directly.

Why the multi-model swap matters for agents. Most coding agents are cheaper and faster when you route structured steps like file reads and diff writes to a small model, and reserve a frontier model for the part that actually writes code. The cost gap between a $0.15/M token small model and a $15/M token frontier model stacks up fast when the agent makes 50 calls per task.

Usage visibility. The activity dashboard shows every request with its model, cost, latency, and token counts. If an agent runs away, you see it inside minutes, not at the end of a billing cycle. Per-key rate limits stop the bleeding without disabling the whole integration.

pages.title: Openrouter Data Policies 2026 06 19
pages.body:
# How provider data policies work on OpenRouter

**Source:** Email (OpenRouter Team newsletter)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee03492356403f
**Ingested:** 2026-06-19T22:14:00Z
**Provenance:** OpenRouter onboarding email, received 2026-06-19

Every provider behind OpenRouter has its own retention and training policy. OpenRouter reflects those policies as structured data on each endpoint, publishes the full table in the docs, and gives users controls to filter routing on them.

## What you control

1. **Block routing to providers that train.** An account-wide toggle for whether routing may use providers that train on prompts, with separate switches for paid and free models. Opting out means OpenRouter will not route to training providers.

2. **Require a specific data policy per request.** Override account default on any single call via data policy filtering in the provider routing docs.

3. **Pick only zero-retention providers.** Many providers on OpenRouter are zero retention: Amazon Bedrock, Azure, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, Together, and others.

## EU in-region routing

For enterprise accounts, OpenRouter supports EU in-region routing. When enabled, prompts and completions are processed inside the European Union via `https://eu.openrouter.ai` base URL.

# Three features worth trying next

**Source:** Email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19edb0e0abd83fb0
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T21:04:44Z
**Provenance:** Email from OpenRouter Team <welcome@openrouter.ai> to info@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-18

Streaming, structured outputs, and provider preferences. Three parameters that change what OpenRouter can do for you.

OpenRouter
OpenRouter ( https://openrouter.ai/?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )

Hi there,

Three features worth trying once your first requests are landing. Each one is a single parameter change. Each one earns its place in production code and none of them require a new integration or a different endpoint.

See the feature docs ( https://openrouter.ai/docs/quickstart?utm_source=email&utm_medium=lifecycle&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=a13_close&utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )

Streaming ( https://openrouter.ai/docs/api-reference/streaming?utm_source=email&utm_medium=lifecycle&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=a13_close&utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io ). Set stream=True and tokens arrive as they generate. Same endpoint, same auth, one parameter. Perceived latency drops from seconds to the first token, which is the difference between a chat UI that feels alive and one that feels broken. Agents that stream partial results to a frontend get a noticeable UX lift for zero code cost.

Structured outputs ( https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/structured-outputs?utm_source=email&utm_medium=lifecycle&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=a13_close&utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io ). Pass a JSON schema via response_format and OpenRouter enforces it at the API level. No regex parsing, no retry loops when the model returns malformed JSON, no "please respond in valid JSON" prompt engineering. Works on every model that supports the format, and the router respects it when you use openrouter/auto.

Provider preferences ( https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/provider-routing?utm_source=email&utm_medium=lifecycle&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=a13_close&utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io ). Pin specific providers, exclude others, set latency floors and throughput minimums. When a model has multiple providers behind it, you decide which backend serves each request. Set {"sort": "price"} to always pick the cheapest, or an allowlist to lock routing to providers you trust.

ALL THREE AT ONCE

response = client.chat.completions.create
(   model="openrouter/auto",
stream=True,
response_format={"type": "json_schema", "json_schema": schema},
extra_body={"provider": {"sort": "price"}},
messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "..."}], )

Pages worth bookmarking: openrouter.ai/models ( https://openrouter.ai/models?utm_source=email&utm_medium=lifecycle&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=a13_close&utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io ) for browsing and pricing, openrouter.ai/rankings ( https://openrouter.ai/rankings?utm_source=email&utm_medium=lifecycle&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=a13_close&utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io ) for real usage volume data, openrouter.ai/activity ( https://openrouter.ai/activity?utm_source=email&utm_medium=lifecycle&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=a13_close&utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io ) for your usage and costs, and discord.gg/openrouter
( https://discord.gg/openrouter?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io ) if you get stuck.

pages.title: Openrouter Three Levers Cost 2026 06 17
pages.body:
# Three levers that lower your per-token cost

**Source:** Email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ed5e7b3a86e916
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T21:04:48Z
**Provenance:** Email from OpenRouter Team <welcome@openrouter.ai> to info@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-17

Tiered pricing, provider preferences, and prompt caching. All per-request, no extra wiring.

OpenRouter
OpenRouter ( https://openrouter.ai/?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A8+Pricing+Tiers&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )

Hi there,

Three things quietly cut your per-token cost on OpenRouter. They stack, and none of them require changing models or rewriting your app.

Compare model pricing ( https://openrouter.ai/models?utm_source=email&utm_medium=lifecycle&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=a10_pricing&utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A8+Pricing+Tiers&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )

1. TIERED PRICING

Most models run on tiered pricing set by the provider: the more tokens you send through, the less each token costs. You do not opt in. The discount applies automatically once you cross the threshold. Open any model page on openrouter.ai/models ( https://openrouter.ai/models?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A8+Pricing+Tiers&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io ) and click the Effective Pricing tab to see the live per-provider rate, including any tiers you have already hit. Consolidating traffic onto one or two models unlocks the next tier faster than scattering tokens across five.

2. PROVIDER PREFERENCES

The same model is served by multiple providers at different rates. provider in the request body lets you prefer cheaper providers, sort by price, or set a price ceiling. It is a per-request setting, so you can apply it selectively to traffic where latency matters less than cost. Docs: Provider Routing ( https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/provider-routing?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A8+Pricing+Tiers&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io ).

3. PROMPT CACHING

On supported models, OpenRouter reuses cached prompt prefixes across requests, so repeated context costs a fraction of the first call. Useful for long system prompts, RAG, agents that replay history, and anything that ships the same context every turn. Per-request, no extra wiring. Docs: Prompt Caching ( https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/prompt-caching?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A8+Pricing+Tiers&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io ).

pages.title: Openrouter Welcome 2026 06 03
pages.body:
# Welcome to OpenRouter

**Source:** email from OpenRouter Team, 2026-06-03
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19e8b97df0c0f20a
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T12:45:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded to info@malohacoast.com

[[OpenRouter]] provides a single API key that reaches 300+ models across OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Meta, DeepSeek, xAI, Mistral, and every other major provider. It is drop-in compatible with the OpenAI SDK.

## Key Features

- **One key, one billing surface, one rate limit pool, one place to see usage.** No need to maintain separate accounts and SDKs for each provider.
- **Automatic failover:** When a provider has an outage, OpenRouter falls over to the next provider serving the same model, preventing cascading failures.
- **Easy model switching:** New models are a string change, not an integration project.
- **openrouter/auto** — auto-routing that reads the prompt and picks a model per request. Roughly 63% of auto traffic routes to models under $1 per million tokens, keeping costs down for simple prompts.

## Getting Started

1. Create an API key at openrouter.ai/settings/keys
2. Point the OpenAI SDK at openrouter.ai/api/v1
3. Make the first request

Switching models: change one string from `openai/gpt-4o-mini` to `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6` to `openrouter/auto`.

**Source:** email forward

**The Maloha Coast Navigator**  
*Your weekly guide to AI strategy for SMBs*

---

## This Week: Why Most AI Pilots Fail

80% of AI proofs-of-concept never reach production. Here's the pattern:

1. **Scope creep** — POCs that try to boil the ocean
2. **Data rot** — Models trained on stale data that's 6 months old
3. **No owner** — AI sits in IT without a business sponsor

### The Fix

Start with a **single workflow**, not a platform. Pick the one manual process your team hates most. Automate that. Measure the time saved. Then expand.

---

## Client Spotlight

A 12-person accounting firm deployed an email triage agent. Result: 14 hours/week saved. ROI in week one.

---

- [[Second Brain Architecture]] — How we structure knowledge for SMBs

Next week: Pricing models that work for SMB AI consulting.

*— Sayf, Maloha Coast*

pages.title: Three Folder System Head Hands Heart
pages.body:
# The 3-Folder System: Atlas → Projects → End Products (Head, Hands, Heart)

**Source:** YouTube — "Your file structure is a map of your cognitive architecture"
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Rl7rvHbDqGk
**Format:** Video transcript
**Ingested:** 2026-06-05

## Summary

The speaker discovered that disorganized AI outputs were a reflection of disorganized thinking. After months of building AI agents and burning API tokens, he stopped and realized the real problem wasn't the tools — it was his cognitive architecture. His core insight: **your file structure is a map of your thinking**. Most people's maps are a junk drawer with a search bar.

The solution is embarrassingly simple: a three-folder system that maps to Head, Hands, and Heart.

## The Three-Folder Framework

### 1. Atlas (Head / Thinking)
Raw materials, ideas, strategy, capture. Where you put ideas when you get them — the thinking layer. Everything that isn't yet a project.

### 2. Projects (Hands / Building)
Active work. Content being created, things being built. This is the doing layer.

### 3. End Products (Heart / Serving)
Completed work. Finished deliverables that serve other people. The output layer.

## Key Insights

- **File structure = cognitive architecture.** Your external files reveal your internal thinking patterns.
- **Organization is speed.** [Bob Proctor](people/bob-proctor): "Organization is heaven's first law." Clear structure makes AI faster and cheaper (fewer tokens).
- **The sophistication trap.** Smart people overcomplicate things. The answer is usually simpler than you think.
- **AI reveals you to yourself.** AI doesn't just do thinking FOR you — it shows you HOW you think by externalizing your patterns.
- **Agents mirror human growth.** An AI agent needs identity, context, and skills — the same three things a human needs to change their life.
- **Stop building, start organizing.** Before chasing the next tool or agent, fix the foundation.

## Claude Mirrors the Same Structure

| Mode | Purpose | Maps To |
|------|---------|---------|
| Chat | Thinking, exploring ideas | Atlas (Head) |
| Co-work | Building artifacts, plans | Projects (Hands) |
| Claude Code | Finished products, shipped work | End Products (Heart) |

## The Three Communities Pattern

The speaker's Skool communities also follow this structure:
- **Free community** (Lifestyle Founders Group) → Thinking, sharing ideas (Atlas)
- **Paid community** (Claude for Coaches) → Active building, workshops (Projects)
- **VIP community** → End products, completed work (Heart)

## Notable Quotes

- "Your file structure is a map of your cognitive architecture. Most people's maps are a junk drawer with a search bar."
- "Organization is heaven's first law." — [Bob Proctor](people/bob-proctor)
- "The bottleneck isn't the tool, it's the clarity of your thinking. Your cognitive architecture is now your competitive advantage."
- "Fix the inside, the outside follows. As above, so below."
- "I need a second brain sounds nice, but actually my first brain needs to get organized first."

## People/Resources Mentioned

- [Bob Proctor](people/bob-proctor) — "Organization is heaven's first law"
- [Jake Van Cleef](people/jake-van-cleef) — academic paper on stopping agent building, fixing file structure first
- [Nick Milo](people/nick-milo) — Obsidian/second brain advocate, three-folder patterns
- Head, Hands & Heart Notes: https://imported-abacus-8dd.notion.site/
- Free Skool Community: https://skool.com/lfg
- Claude For Coaches: https://www.skool.com/claudeforcoaches/about
- Lifestyle Founder Mastermind: https://www.skool.com/quantum100/about

[Source: YouTube video, ingested 2026-06-05]

pages.title: Virtual Machines Deep Dive
pages.body:
# How Virtual Machines Work - A Deep Dive (#148: Part 1 - DevOps Mastermind)

**From:** [[Neo Kim]] (via systemdesignone@substack.com, forwarded by Sayf Codes)
**Date:** Mon, 25 May 2026
**Source:** email forward

---

**DevOps Mastermind** — A newsletter series to help engineers master DevOps fundamentals instead of just memorizing commands. Architecture breakdowns of core DevOps systems, deep dives into Docker, Kubernetes, Terraform, and practical insights into scalability, automation, and reliability.

---

## Emulation vs Virtualization

Emulation is when software pretends to be completely different hardware (e.g., retro gaming emulators). Each instruction from the old system is translated for the modern chip. It works, but it's slow.

Virtualization creates a version of the same hardware the machine already has. Most instructions run directly on the real processor. Result: near-native speed.

- Emulation = hiring a translator for every sentence
- Virtualization = both people already speak the same language

This speed is what made virtualization practical at scale — you cannot run thousands of emulated systems on a single server. With virtualization, you can.

## What a Virtual Machine Actually Is

A VM is a complete computer abstraction built on top of a physical machine. It's presented with CPU, memory, storage, and network — just like a physical system. The OS inside has no awareness it's sharing the machine.

**The hypervisor** is the layer that makes the illusion possible. Every request for CPU, memory, disk I/O, or network goes through it. Three key capabilities:

1. **Isolation** — each VM runs independently; a failure in one doesn't affect others
2. **Portability** — VMs can move between physical systems with minimal changes
3. **Efficient resource use** — multiple workloads share the same hardware

## Hypervisor: Brain Behind the Illusion

### Type 1 vs Type 2

| Type | Description | Examples |
|------|-------------|----------|
| **Type 1 (bare-metal)** | Runs directly on physical hardware, no host OS in between | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM |
| **Type 2 (hosted)** | Runs on top of an existing OS like a regular app | VirtualBox, VMware Workstation |

### Full Virtualization vs Paravirtualization

- **Full virtualization**: Guest OS runs unmodified; hypervisor handles all abstraction work
- **Paravirtualization**: Guest OS is aware it's virtualized and cooperates with the hypervisor to reduce overhead
- Trade-off: compatibility (full) vs performance (para)

### Hardware-Assisted Virtualization

Intel VT-x and AMD-V let the CPU itself differentiate between VMs and the hypervisor. Sensitive operations pass control to the hypervisor automatically. This made virtualization practical at scale.

### Nested Virtualization

Running a VM inside another VM. Useful for testing and local Kubernetes setups but unsuitable for production due to overhead.

## CPU Virtualization

### The Privilege Model

In a normal system, the OS sits at the highest privilege level. In a virtual environment, the hypervisor needs to sit above the OS. The guest OS gets moved down a level — but never knows it was demoted.

When the guest tries a privileged operation, the processor hands control to the hypervisor.

### vCPU and Scheduling

When you create a VM, you assign vCPUs (virtual CPUs). The hypervisor schedules vCPUs onto physical cores via **time-slicing** — the same way an OS runs multiple apps.

**CPU overcommit**: Assigning more vCPUs than physical cores (e.g., 16 cores running 32 vCPUs). Works if all VMs aren't busy simultaneously. When demand rises, contention reduces performance.

### VM-exit and VM-entry

- **VM-exit**: VM tries a privileged instruction → processor stops it → control transfers to hypervisor
- **VM-entry**: Hypervisor handles the request → hands control back to the VM exactly where it left off
- Handoff takes microseconds. But frequent exits hurt performance.

### NUMA Awareness (Non-Uniform Memory Access)

Modern servers have multiple processors, each with local memory. Accessing memory on a different processor is slower. The hypervisor tries to keep a VM's vCPUs and memory on the same NUMA node.

For most small deployments this doesn't matter. For high-performance databases on large servers, it matters a lot.

---

|*This was a free preview of a subscriber-only post from [[Neo Kim]]'s "The System Design Newsletter". Guest author: [[Ayaan]] (self-taught DevOps engineer focused on Kubernetes & cloud infrastructure).*

## Key Takeaways

- Virtualization enabled cloud computing by splitting one powerful machine into many isolated ones
- The hypervisor is the core abstraction layer — handles CPU, memory, storage, and networking
- Hardware support (VT-x/AMD-V) made virtualization practical at scale
- Containers are a different approach (OS-level virtualization, shared kernel) — covered in Part 1's comparison

## People Mentioned

- **[[Neo Kim]]** — Author of The System Design Newsletter (systemdesignone.substack.com). Wrote this deep dive on virtualization architecture as part of the DevOps Mastermind series.
- **[[Ayaan]]** — Self-taught DevOps engineer, guest author. Focused on Kubernetes & cloud infrastructure. Builds in public on Twitter/X and GitHub.

pages.title: Vps Hardening
pages.body:
# VPS Hardening

## Overview
Security hardening of the Maloha Coast production VPS (Ubuntu, 6.8.0-124-generic). All changes applied and verified post-reboot.

## Changes Applied

### SSH Hardening
| Setting | Before | After |
|---------|--------|-------|
| PermitRootLogin | yes | prohibit-password |
| X11Forwarding | yes | no |
| MaxAuthTries | 6 (default) | 3 |
| ClientAliveInterval | none | 300s (5 min) |
| ClientAliveCountMax | 3 (default) | 2 |
| AllowUsers | any local user | ubuntu only |
| DSA host keys | present | removed |
| PasswordAuthentication | yes | no |

### Firewall (UFW)
- **Default**: deny all incoming, allow all outgoing
- **Open ports**: 22/tcp (SSH) only
- Status: active

### fail2ban
- **Jail**: sshd (aggressive mode)
- **Max retries**: 3
- **Ban time**: 1 hour
- **Post-reboot**: banned 4 IPs within 2 minutes of coming online
- See [[2026-06-03#VPS Hardening]] for timeline

### System Updates
- 78 packages upgraded
- Kernel updated to 6.8.0-124 (was pending reboot, now active)

## Verification
- [x] SSH config validated via `sshd -T`
- [x] UFW active with correct rules
- [x] fail2ban running with active bans
- [x] Hermes gateway auto-starts on boot
- [x] Ubuntu NOPASSWD sudo preserved (Hermes requirement)

## Related
- [[Google Workspace Setup]]
- [[2026-06-03]]
- [[Gateway Infrastructure]] — gateway health monitoring and watchdog

pages.title: Why 2026 Career Goals Dead Zaim
pages.body:
# Why Your 2026 Career Goals Are Already Dead

**Source:** Nation Building School newsletter (Zaim Mohzani)
**Date:** 2026-06-12
**Forwarded by:** Zaim Mohzani (info+zaim@malohacoast.com)

It's June. How's that career goal from January going?
If you're being honest, probably: forgotten by March, barely remembered (and feeling guilty) in June and quietly dropped by the end of the year.

It's a timeframe problem, not a discipline problem.

## The Annual Goal Trap

A year is too long to create urgency and too vague to act on. "Get a new job this year" feels fine in January. There's time. So nothing happens. By June, the goal is still technically alive but you haven't done anything that moves it forward.

The author references Brian Moran and Michael Lennington's book "The 12 Week Year" which argues most people don't actually operate in years — they operate in the last few weeks before a deadline. The fix is to shrink the deadline.

Personal example: Lost 6kg in 90 days after rewriting the goal from "lose 6kg by December" to "lose 6kg in 90 days, by 31 March." Hit it in 90 days and the habits persisted.

## Why This Works for Career Too

"Get promoted this year" doesn't tell you what to do on Monday. "By 30 September, I will have had 3 conversations with people in my target department, applied to 5 internal roles, and presented one project to leadership" tells you exactly what to do on Monday.

One is a wish. The other is a plan.

## How to Set Yours

Pick ONE career goal for this quarter. Just one.

Format:
- My 90-day career goal is...
- This matters to me because...
- To complete this, I commit that by 30 September 2026, I will have... (make this checkable)
- To make sure I complete it, I'm going to... (weekly actions, not the outcome)

P.S. Free workshop on setting this 90-day goal for Q3 2026 — https://t.ly/getclearoncareer6

pages.title: Zaim 15 Minute Gap Method 2026 06 14
pages.body:
# Zaim's 15-Minute Gap Method

**Source:** email from Zaim Mohzani re: coaching insight, 2026-06-14
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eca04daacc7a09
**Ingested:** 2026-06-23T10:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Zaim's coaching reflection note, sent to info+zaim@malohacoast.com

## The Method

Between KSI coaching sessions, Zaim started using 15-minute gaps to jot down one thing he noticed about each student — what they're stuck on, what lit them up. After a month of this, patterns emerged that he'd never have spotted otherwise.

**The 15-minute rule:** Never let a gap go empty. Even one sentence per student compounds.

## Three Student Buckets

1. **Career pivoters** — stuck on "what if I fail" not "what do I want"
2. **Fresh grads** — over-indexing on salary, under-indexing on growth trajectory
3. **Mid-career** — all variations of "is this it?"

## Relevance

This is a meta-coaching insight — it's not about any single student but about how Zaim discovered a pattern-recognition system through micro-observation. The 15-minute gap is the mechanism; the three buckets are the output.

## Related

- [[Zaim Mohzani]] — Coach and originator of this method
- [[KSI Workshop]] — Context for the coaching sessions

pages.body:
## The 3-Month Career Pivot Framework

Zaim shared this during a coaching session with a KSI senior analyst looking to move into product.

### Phase 1: Audit (Month 1)
- [[Skills Inventory]] — map current skills to target role requirements
- Identify the 20% skill gap that blocks 80% of interviews
- Don't learn everything — learn the gatekeepers

### Phase 2: Signal (Month 2)
- [[Side Project Strategy]] — build one public artifact, not ten private ones
- Write 2 LinkedIn posts/week documenting the pivot journey
- Engage with 3 target-company hiring managers

### Phase 3: Convert (Month 3)
- [[Salary Negotiation Playbook]] — comp bands for product roles in KL
- Reference calls with 2 people who made the same pivot
- First offer within 90 days

### Key Insight
> 'The career pivot gap isn't a skills problem — it's a positioning problem. You're not underqualified, you're framed wrong.' — Zaim

͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏
͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏
͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏
͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­


It's June. How's that career goal from January going?

If you're being honest, probably: forgotten by March, barely remembered
(and feeling guilty) in June and quietly dropped by...the end of the year.

It's a timeframe problem, not a discipline problem.
The annual goal trap

A year is too long to create urgency and too vague to act on.

"Get a new job this year" feels fine in January. There's time. So nothing
happens.

By June, the goal is still technically alive, but you haven't done anything
that moves it forward. By December, you're setting the same goal again for
next year.

I went through this myself but with weight, not career. In January 2025, I
set an annual goal: lose 6kg by December. Standard SMART goal. Looked fine
on paper.

Then I came across Brian Moran and Michael Lennington's book "The 12 Week
Year," which argues most people don't actually operate in years. They
operate in the last few weeks before a deadline, however long that deadline
is. So the fix is to shrink the deadline.

Btw, this book is available on Shopee!

I rewrote the goal: lose 6kg in 90 days, by 31 March.

I hit it!!

The best part was that I never had to set that goal again. The habits I
built in those 90 days kept running on their own for the rest of the year.
Why this works for your career too

The same applies to a job search, a promotion case or a pivot.

"Get promoted this year" doesn't tell you what to do on Monday.

"By 30 September, I will have had 3 conversations with people in my target
department, applied to 5 internal roles, and presented one project to
leadership" tells you exactly what to do on Monday.

One of these is a wish. The other is a plan.

I've worked with clients on exactly this. The ones who set a 90-day goal
like "land 3 offers from MNCs by end of Q3" with weekly actions attached,
mostly hit it or get close. The ones with "find a better job this year"
mostly don't, because there's no Monday task attached to it.
How to set yours

Pick ONE career goal for this quarter. Just one.

Follow this format and write down:

   - *My 90-day career goal is...*
   - *This matters to me because...*
   - *To complete this, I commit that by 30 September 2026, I will have...*
   (make this checkable. "3 offers" is checkable. "More clarity" is not.)
   - *To make sure I complete it, I'm going to...* (weekly actions, not the
   outcome)

Your homework this weekend

Write it down. One goal. One sentence you can check off or not, no grey
area.

Then build it into your week, not your year.
------------------------------

P.S. I'm running a free workshop exactly on setting this 90-day goal for Q3
2026. If you're interested to join, it's happening early next month in
June. For more info or to sign up, head over to
https://t.ly/getclearoncareer6
<https://preview.kit-mail3.com/click/dpheh0hzhmh4/aHR0cHM6Ly90Lmx5L2dldGNsZWFyb25jYXJlZXI2>

Unsubscribe <https://preview.kit-mail3.com/unsubscribe> | Update your
profile <https://preview.kit-mail3.com/preferences> | I Write on LinkedIn
Every Day - Follow Me For Career, AI and Productivity Insights
<https://preview.kit-mail3.com/click/dpheh0hzhmh4/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubGlua2VkaW4uY29tL2luL3phaW1tb2h6YW5pLw==>

# Zaim's KSI 15-Minute Rule - Student Observation Patterns

Zaim here. Wanted to capture this before I forget.

Between KSI sessions I have these 15-minute gaps. Started using them to jot down one thing I noticed about each student — what they're stuck on, what lit them up. After a month of this, patterns emerged that I'd never have spotted otherwise.

Three buckets emerged:
1. Career pivoters — stuck on 'what if I fail' not 'what do I want'
2. Fresh grads — over-indexing on salary, under-indexing on growth trajectory  
3. Mid-career — all variations of 'is this it?'

The 15-minute rule: never let a gap go empty. Even one sentence per student compounds.

— Zaim

# End-to-End Pipeline Test (Post-Fix Verification)

**Source:** Pipeline test — verifying Bug 1 (venv Python) and Bug 2 (YAML tags) fixes.

## Context

This page tests the full email-to-gbrain pipeline after applying two critical fixes discovered on June 15, 2026:

1. **Bug 1 — Cron Python PATH**: Pipeline now uses Hermes venv Python at `/home/ubuntu/.hermes/hermes-agent/venv/bin/python` instead of bare `python`/`python3`.

2. **Bug 2 — Tags Lost on Export**: Workspace files now embed `tags:` in YAML frontmatter so the `zaim` tag survives `gbrain export` and reaches the enrichment pipeline.

## Verification Steps

- [ ] Workspace file saved with YAML frontmatter
- [ ] `gbrain put --tags zaim --body-stdin` succeeds
- [ ] `gbrain export` includes `tags:` in YAML frontmatter
- [ ] Enrichment pipeline picks up the `zaim` tag
- [ ] Dashboard shows this page under the Zaim filter

**Test timestamp:** $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)

pages.title: Coaching Client Aisyah Rahim
pages.body:
# Coaching Client: Aisyah Rahim

**Source:** email forward
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eca0a8dc4888c3
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T04:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded to Zaim's workspace via self-BCC

Zaim here. Adding a quick profile on a new client.

Name: Aisyah Rahim
Role: Senior Brand Manager at Unilever Malaysia
Context: Referred by Liyana from the KSI workshop last month. First session next Tuesday.

Key points from our intro call:
- 8 years in FMCG brand management, feeling stagnant
- Wants to pivot into tech but unsure where to start
- Main blocker: 'I don't have a technical background'
- Has actually done more data-driven work than she realises — ran consumer analytics for 3 product launches
- Salary anchor: RM 18k/month, worried about resetting to junior level

My read: Classic mid-career pivot case. The real problem isn't skills — it's confidence and narrative. She needs help reframing 'brand manager' as 'growth strategist who uses data.' Tech companies would kill for someone who can bridge consumer insights and product.

Plan for session 1: Audit her last 2 years of work through a tech lens. Find 3-5 specific examples where she already did 'product thinking' without calling it that.

— Zaim

pages.body:
# Your Business Brain — Week of June 3–10, 2026

📥 **This week's intake: 53 items** (first full week of brain population)

- **3 meetings** — with [[Audrey Loh]] (Posh Corporate), [[Bjarte]] (Global AI Village), and [[TJ van der Molen]] (Passend)
- **1 dataset** — [[Dynamics Orchard Farm Management Accounts FYE 2025]], 24 transactions, MYR 196.5K total revenue across two orchards
- **1 video** — [[Graphify Solves Claude Code Problem]], knowledge graph tool for codebases
- **16 people** added as entity pages (Audrey Loh, Yaw Hog Ng, Yassif N.M., Aisyah Humaira, Ayaan, Sayf Codes, Neo Kim, Carson, Nate Herk, Zaim Mohzani, Nabil Jalil, Bjarte, Nick Milo, Jake Van Cleef, Bob Proctor, TJ van der Molen)
- **12 companies** mapped (Posh Corporate, Dynamics Orchard Farm, Passend, Global AI Village, AI Automation Society, OpenAI, Anthropic, KSI Strategic Institute, Inclusive AI Association, Nation Building School, Blackgrid Digital, System Design One)
- **Key concepts** — [[Business Brain]], [[Company Memory OS Ph1 PRD]], [[Knowledge Graph as Moat]], [[OpenClaw Anatomy]], [[Claude Code 10x Skill Newsletter]], [[Three Folder System Head Hands Heart]]

---

🔗 **New connections found**

- [[Audrey Loh]] → [[Posh Corporate]] → [[Dynamics Orchard Farm Sdn Bhd]] — three verified graph edges forming a complete client-accountant-firm chain, with the FYE 2025 management accounts dataset as supporting evidence
- [[Posh Corporate]] connects to 4 people ([[Audrey Loh]], [[Yaw Hog Ng]], [[Yassif N.M.]], [[Aisyah Humaira Wan Aziz]]) and 1 company ([[Dynamics Orchard Farm Sdn Bhd]]) — a dense business relationship cluster with real financial data flowing through it
- [[meetings/2026-06-08-bjarte-global-ai-village]] → [[Global AI Village]] → [[Business Brain]] — the Business Brain concept was pitched to a real prospect with a follow-up test scheduled for June 12
- [[Knowledge Graph as Moat]] backlinks to [[Company Memory OS Ph1 PRD]], which links to [[Sayf Codes]] — the product vision and competitive strategy are connected through the same author
- [[meetings/2026-06-05-tj-vandermolen]] → [[Passend]] — prospective client with 3 development projects scoped, including a Requirements Engineering Workshop proposal at MYR 800/full day

---

📈 **Data updates**

- **Dynamics Orchard Farm — Management Accounts FYE 2025**: 24 transactions across Jan–Dec 2025. Orchard A revenue MYR 89,500; Orchard B revenue MYR 107,000; total net profit MYR 102,000. Orchard B consistently outperforms with 15% higher margins. Orchard expansion feasibility study (MYR 7,500, Sept 2025) and new planting materials (MYR 10,500, Oct 2025) indicate expansion plans are already underway. Source: email from [[Audrey Loh]] at [[Posh Corporate]].

---

💡 **Insight**

The [[Business Brain]] concept — pitched to [[Bjarte]] at [[Global AI Village]] on June 8 — sits at the intersection of two verified product development threads. The [[Company Memory OS Ph1 PRD]] (authored by [[Sayf Codes]]) defines the technical vision for transforming fragmented communication into searchable institutional memory, while [[Knowledge Graph as Moat]] articulates the competitive defensibility of graph-based data relationships. The [[Graphify Solves Claude Code Problem]] video, ingested June 9, independently validates the same core pattern — a knowledge graph that compounds value as more data enters the system. With the [[Dynamics Orchard Farm Management Accounts FYE 2025]] dataset demonstrating real financial data flowing through the brain ([[Posh Corporate]] → [[Audrey Loh]] → [[Dynamics Orchard Farm Sdn Bhd]]), the Business Brain concept has both a product architecture and a live data proof point for the June 12 test.

pages.title: Gateway Infrastructure
pages.body:
# Gateway Infrastructure

## Hardware
2GB VPS (Tencent Cloud, Ubuntu, 6.8.0-124). 2GB swap. ~50GB storage.

## Hermes Gateway

### Service
Systemd user service at `~/.config/systemd/user/hermes-gateway.service`. Auto-starts on boot. Linger enabled so it survives logout.

### Exit Code 75 (EX_TEMPFAIL)
The gateway uses exit code 75 as a "please restart me" signal to systemd. Triggered by `/restart`, `/update`, or SIGUSR1. The systemd config has `RestartForceExitStatus=75` so this always triggers a restart. See [[gateway-watchdog]] for crash-loop detection.

### Memory Profile
- Baseline: ~97MB RSS at startup
- After 14h moderate use: ~600MB (growth ~36MB/h)
- Peak observed: 1.0G
- Gradual leak under active use — tracked by watchdog growth-rate alert

### Manual Recovery
When stuck in a crash loop: `sudo -u ubuntu /home/ubuntu/.local/bin/hermes gateway start`

## Gateway Health Watchdog

Created Jun 4, 2026. A Python script at `~/.hermes/scripts/gateway-watchdog.py` that runs every 10 minutes via cron (`no_agent=True`, zero LLM tokens). Silent when healthy; alerts Discord #Home when thresholds are breached.

### Thresholds
- Gateway RSS >= 1.4GB (warning) / >= 1.6GB (critical)
- System free memory <= 300MB (warning) / <= 150MB (critical)
- Swap usage > 200MB
- >= 1 exit code 75 crash in last hour
- >= 1 SIGTERM kill in last hour
- Gateway uptime < 30 min
- Memory growth rate > 50MB/hour

### Related
- [[VPS Hardening]] — SSH, firewall, fail2ban baseline
- [[2026-06-04]] — watchdog setup and gateway crash investigation

pages.title: Hermes Profile Backup
pages.body:
## Hermes Profile Backup

The Hermes profile backup pushes all agent profiles to a private GitHub repo with secrets stripped.

### Repository
- **Repo:** `github.com/Maloha-Coast/hermes-profile-backup` (private)
- **Contents:** 1,186 files across 2 profiles (default + MC)
- **Includes:** SOUL.md, skills, cron, memories, config.yaml (stripped), setup.sh, .gitignore

### What gets backed up
- SOUL.md (agent identity)
- skills/ (all skill files)
- cron/ (scheduled jobs)
- memories/ (USER.md + MEMORY.md)
- config.yaml with API keys replaced by <YOUR_KEY_HERE>

### What gets EXCLUDED
- API keys, tokens, secrets (replaced with placeholders)
- Session logs and databases
- OAuth tokens (google_token.json)
- Runtime state files

### Restore
```bash
git clone git@github.com:Maloha-Coast/hermes-profile-backup.git
cd hermes-profile-backup
./setup.sh
```
Then edit ~/.hermes/config.yaml and ~/.hermes/.env with credentials.

### Known gap
- .env.template is missing from the current backup (README references it but file was not generated). Needs to be added on next backup run.

pages.title: Msg 19ee03492356403f
pages.body:
# How provider data policies work on [[OpenRouter]]

**Source:** OpenRouter Team (newsletter)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee03492356403f
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20
**Provenance:** Email to info@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-19

Every provider behind [[OpenRouter]] has its own retention and training policy. [[OpenRouter]] reflects those policies as structured data on each endpoint, publishes the full table in the docs, and gives you controls to filter routing on them.

[Provider policy table](https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/privacy-and-logging)

## What you control

1. **Block routing to providers that train.** Account-wide toggle for whether routing may use providers that train on prompts, with separate switches for paid and free models.
2. **Require a specific data policy per request.** Override account default on any single call via [data policy filtering](https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/provider-routing#requiring-providers-to-comply-with-data-policies).
3. **Pick only zero-retention providers.** Many providers — including Amazon Bedrock, Azure, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, and Together — are zero retention. Filter to them the same way as training policy.

## If you need stricter guarantees

[[OpenRouter]] supports EU in-region routing for enterprise accounts. Prompts and completions processed inside the EU via `https://eu.openrouter.ai`. [Contact enterprise team](https://openrouter.ai/enterprise/form) to enable it.

One API key, the same privacy controls across 300+ models.

pages.title: How provider data policies work on OpenRouter
pages.body:
# How provider data policies work on [[OpenRouter]]

**Source:** email from OpenRouter Team, 2026-06-19
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee03492356403f

## Summary

[[OpenRouter]] provides structured data about each provider's retention and training policies, publishes a full table in their docs, and gives users three layers of control over which providers can process their prompts.

## Key Content

Every provider behind [[OpenRouter]] has its own retention and training policy. [[OpenRouter]] reflects those policies as structured data on each endpoint and publishes the full table in the docs.

### What You Control

1. **Block routing to providers that train.** An account-wide toggle for whether routing may use providers that train on prompts, with separate switches for paid and free models. If you opt out, [[OpenRouter]] will not route to training providers.

2. **Require a specific data policy per request.** Override your account default on any single call via data policy filtering in the provider routing docs.

3. **Pick only zero-retention providers.** Many providers on [[OpenRouter]] are zero retention: Amazon Bedrock, Azure, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, Together, and others.

### Enterprise Options

[[OpenRouter]] supports EU in-region routing for enterprise accounts. When enabled, prompts and completions are processed inside the European Union via `https://eu.openrouter.ai`.

## Links

- Provider policy table: https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/privacy-and-logging
- Privacy settings: https://openrouter.ai/settings/privacy
- Data policy filtering docs: https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/provider-routing
- Enterprise: https://openrouter.ai/enterprise/form
