pages.title: Openrouter Three Features 2026 06 18
pages.body:
# Three features worth trying next on OpenRouter

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19edb0e0abd83fb0
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T03:30:00Z

OpenRouter onboarding email covering three features that are single-parameter changes with production value.

## Key Points

1. **Streaming** (`stream=True`): Tokens arrive as generated. Perceived latency drops from seconds to first-token time. Agents that stream partial results to a frontend get a noticeable UX lift for zero code cost.

2. **Structured outputs** (`response_format` with JSON schema): OpenRouter enforces JSON schema at the API level. No regex parsing, no retry loops for malformed JSON, no "please respond in valid JSON" prompt engineering. Works on every model that supports the format; router respects it with `openrouter/auto`.

3. **Provider preferences** (`provider` param in `extra_body`): Pin specific providers, exclude others, set latency floors and throughput minimums. Sort by `"price"` to always pick the cheapest, or use an allowlist to lock routing to trusted providers.

## Combined Example

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="openrouter/auto",
    stream=True,
    response_format={"type": "json_schema", "json_schema": schema},
    extra_body={"provider": {"sort": "price"}},
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "..."}],
)
```

## Useful Pages

- [Models & pricing](https://openrouter.ai/models)
- [Rankings (real usage volume)](https://openrouter.ai/rankings)
- [Usage & costs](https://openrouter.ai/activity)

pages.title: Three features worth trying next on OpenRouter
pages.body:
# Three features worth trying next on [[OpenRouter]]

**Source:** email from OpenRouter Team, 2026-06-18
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19edb0e0abd83fb0

## Summary

Three [[OpenRouter]] features that are each a single parameter change, don't require a new integration or endpoint, and each earns its place in production code.

## Key Features

### 1. Streaming

Set `stream=True` and tokens arrive as they generate. Same endpoint, same auth, one parameter. Perceived latency drops from seconds to first-token time — the difference between a chat UI that feels alive and one that feels broken. Agents that stream partial results to a frontend get a noticeable UX lift for zero code cost.

### 2. Structured Outputs

Pass a JSON schema via `response_format` and [[OpenRouter]] enforces it at the API level. No regex parsing, no retry loops when the model returns malformed JSON, no "please respond in valid JSON" prompt engineering. Works on every model that supports the parameter.

### 3. Provider Preferences

Use the `provider` parameter to control which providers handle a request: set `ignore=`` to skip specific providers, set `order` to prioritize by latency or cost, or set `allow_fallbacks` to keep the request reliable even when preferred providers are down.

pages.title: Call with Sarah Chen - AI Initiative Discussion
pages.body:
**Date:** 2026-06-15
**Type:** Discovery Call

pages.title: Graphify Solves Claude Code Problem
pages.body:
# Graphify Solves Claude Code's Problem

**Source:** YouTube video by Jack, AI automation channel
**URL:** https://youtu.be/Owv503rTqYY
**Ingested:** 2026-06-09
**Type:** tutorial + product pitch

## Executive Summary

Graphify is a tool that creates a knowledge graph of any codebase — mapping files, modules, "god nodes" (load-bearing files), and their interdependencies. This gives Claude (or any AI agent) a persistent, queryable map so it doesn't re-read the entire repo every conversation. The core insight: "rereading is the tax we're saving" — the graph lets the AI answer from summaries instead of re-parsing flat code. When connected to an agentic operating system (Hermes + Claude Code + a visual dashboard), it becomes a shared brain that compounds across sessions and devices.

## The Core Idea

Graphify treats every codebase as a foreign country. Without it, you're translating a language you don't speak. With it, you have a map — you understand relationships, dependencies, and what affects what before editing anything.

### What the Map Does
- **Instant orientation** — understand any repo immediately without reading through it
- **Grounded answers** — queries pull from the graph, not guesses from skimming
- **Blast radius awareness** — see every dependency before editing a file
- **Token savings** — avoid re-reading the repo each conversation; answer from summaries
- **Always fresh** — the graph is regenerated, not stale

### How It Works
1. Reads code (does not summarize)
2. Clusters things into modules
3. Ranks "god nodes" — load-bearing files
4. Labels facts vs. guesses (extracted vs. inferred)

## Technical Architecture

- **Input:** Any codebase (Claude Code, Hermes, GitHub repos)
- **Output:** Knowledge graph with interactive visualization (community clusters)
- **Integration:** Works as a Claude Code skill; connects to Hermes Agent
- **Dashboard:** Visual interface showing files, links, clusters, and cost savings per conversation

## The Agentic Operating System Layer

The video's key argument is that Graphify alone is useful, but Graphify + an agentic OS is the 10x unlock. The OS provides:

- **One shared brain:** Graphify, Claude Code, Hermes, and the dashboard all read from one registry
- **Cross-device continuity:** Work on laptop with Claude Code, then query from mobile via Hermes — same graph
- **Any repo ingestion:** Paste a GitHub URL → "graphify it" → $0 cost → queryable immediately
- **Overnight reviews:** The OS runs dream cycles, searches for improvements, processes chat history
- **Hermes Telegram integration:** Hermes can reach out proactively with questions

## Key Quotes

- "Rereading is the tax that we're saving here" (3:04)
- "Without the map, it skims a whole repo every single conversation" (3:10)
- "With the map, we can actually answer from summaries — every session compounds" (3:13)
- "Graphify makes the map and the OS makes it always on, shared, and conversational" (7:02)
- "If you're not using an agentic operating system, you're leaving so much value on the table" (10:04)

## People Mentioned

- **Jack** — Video creator, AI automation YouTuber. Previously built and sold a tech startup "with a gazillion customers." Now building AI businesses. Runs a community with Claude Code + Hermes operating system templates.

## Tools/Technologies Mentioned

- **Graphify** — Knowledge graph generator for codebases (GitHub repo, open source)
- **Claude Code** — Primary coding agent that Graphify enhances
- **Hermes Agent** — Agent framework connected to the operating system
- **OpenCor** — Mentioned as part of the cross-device connectivity stack
- **Anti-Gravity** — Referenced as part of the agent ecosystem
- **Codex** — OpenAI Codex, also integrated into the OS

## Relevance to Maloha Coast

The Graphify pattern directly parallels the email-to-gbrain / Company Memory OS architecture:

| Graphify Pattern | gbrain/Company Memory OS Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Knowledge graph of code files | Knowledge graph of people, companies, decisions |
| "God nodes" = key files | "God nodes" = key relationships/deals |
| Query: "what depends on X?" | Query: "what was decided about Project X?" |
| Claude Code integration | Hermes Agent + gbrain integration |
| Visual dashboard | gbrain dashboard + architecture diagram |
| Compound context across sessions | Compound institutional memory across emails |

The video validates the architecture sayf has already built: knowledge graph + agentic layer + visual dashboard + autonomous pipeline. It's a compelling analogy for client pitches — "what Graphify does for code, Company Memory OS does for your business."

## Chapter Index

- 00:00 — Chapter 1: Graphify Solves Claude's Problem (hook + promise)
- 00:46 — Chapter 2: What Graphify Actually Does (the map metaphor)
- 01:21 — Chapter 3: How The Map Works (clustering, god nodes, facts vs guesses)
- 02:10 — Chapter 4: Why Maps Save Tokens (orientation, blast radius, re-reading tax)
- 03:17 — Chapter 5: Install And Set Up (clone repo, Claude Code integration)
- 04:38 — Chapter 6: See The Knowledge Graph (interactive visualization)
- 05:04 — Chapter 7: Query Any Repo Live (prompt example, cost comparison)
- 05:43 — Chapter 8: Take It Further (agentic operating system intro)
- 06:07 — Chapter 9: Inside The Operating System (dashboard, Hermes integration)
- 07:32 — Chapter 10: Import Any GitHub Repo (one-click ingestion)
- 08:07 — Chapter 11: Chat To Your Projects (conversational interface)
- 09:04 — Chapter 12: Connect Everything Together (shared brain architecture)
- 10:04 — Chapter 13: Visual Interface Wins (closing argument)

---

*Raw transcript extracted from YouTube timestamps provided by user (VPS IP blocked by YouTube, transcript retrieved via user-supplied text).*

pages.title: 2026 06 05 Tj Vandermolen
pages.body:
# Meeting with TJ van der Molen

**Date:** 2026-06-05
**Attendees:** [TJ van der Molen](people/tj-vandermolen), sayf

## About TJ

- **Role:** CEO & ECHA Specialist
- **Company:** [Passend](companies/passend)
- **Email:** t.vandermolen@passend.my
- **Website:** www.passend.my
- **Phone:** +6016 434 8614

## Summary

Met with [TJ van der Molen](people/tj-vandermolen), CEO and ECHA Specialist at [Passend](companies/passend). Discussed his business progress, partnerships, and growth plans. Explored how Maloha Coast can support Passend's development needs.

## Three Projects Discussed

### 1. Database + Frontend - Project-Based Learning Showcase

- **Primary users:** Admin & staff
- **Purpose:** Maintain chronological records of student projects & learning outcomes
- **Functionality:** Distribute updates to students, parents, and community via portal/newsletters/social media
- **Feature consideration:** Facial recognition/computer vision for obtaining parental consent before publishing student content

### 2. Internal Tool - Reporting Automation

- **Primary users:** Admin & staff recording behavioral observations for assessment
- **Challenge:** Excessive paperwork distracts teachers/instructors from effective student engagement
- **Solution:** Implement rapid data input tools with database storage and report generation
- **Quality control:** Staff verification system to validate AI/OCR data imports

### 3. SaaS Platform - Counselor Teaching & Learning

- **Primary users:** Counselors/teaching staff accessing Passend materials
- **Business model:** Freemium to subscription conversion
- **Tier structure:**
  - Level 1: Course access
  - Level 2: Curriculum sharing with colleagues
  - Level 3: Passend-hosted events
  - Certification available at all levels

## Next Steps

- Recommended a Requirements Engineering Workshop to develop detailed user stories and interaction requirements
- Workshop would produce a comprehensive task list usable by any development team
- Duration: 1 hour to full day depending on system complexity
- TJ to prioritize which of the three systems to pursue first

## Commercial

- Services offered through Maloha Coast Sdn Bhd
- sayf's calendar relatively open until May, after which contract work begins
- Requirements Engineering Workshop rates: MYR 100/hour or MYR 800/full day

[Source: User, 2026-06-05]

## Draft Email

```
Hi TJ,

Thank you for meeting with me today. I thoroughly enjoyed learning about your business progress and partnerships.

I'm excited about your growth plans and exploring how I can support Passend. Below is a summary of the projects we discussed:

1. Database + Frontend - Project-Based Learning Showcase
   - Primary users: Admin & staff
   - Purpose: Maintain chronological records of student projects & learning outcomes
   - Functionality: Distribute updates to students, parents, and community via portal/newsletters/social media
   - Feature consideration: Facial recognition/computer vision for obtaining parental consent before publishing student content

2. Internal Tool - Reporting Automation
   - Primary users: Admin & staff recording behavioral observations for assessment
   - Challenge: Excessive paperwork distracts teachers/instructors from effective student engagement
   - Solution: Implement rapid data input tools with database storage and report generation
   - Quality control: Staff verification system to validate AI/OCR data imports

3. SaaS Platform - Counselor Teaching & Learning
   - Primary users: Counselors/teaching staff accessing Passend materials
   - Business model: Freemium to subscription conversion
   - Tier structure:
     - Level 1: Course access
     - Level 2: Curriculum sharing with colleagues
     - Level 3: Passend-hosted events
     - Certification available at all levels

Please feel free to correct any misunderstandings or add details I may have missed.

For next steps, I recommend a Requirements Engineering Workshop to develop detailed user stories and specific interaction requirements. This workshop would produce a comprehensive task list that any development team, including ours, could use to build your MVP. The documentation would cover all necessary frontend and backend components.

Workshop duration typically ranges from one hour to a full day depending on system complexity. You might consider prioritizing which of the three systems you'd like to pursue first.

These services are offered through my company, Maloha Coast Sdn Bhd. My calendar is relatively open until May, after which I'll begin contract work for several months. For transparency, our Requirements Engineering Workshop rates are MYR 100 per hour or MYR 800 for a full day.

Looking forward to potentially working together.

Best regards,
[Your Name]
```

**Provenance:** Meeting notes from sayf on 2026-06-10

## Summary

Met with [[Audrey Loh]], Senior Executive at [[Posh Corporate]], to discuss the Dynamics Orchard Farm account and expansion plans. Audrey provided an update on the management accounts preparation and flagged that the client is exploring orchard expansion into new acreage.

## Key Decisions

- Management accounts FYE 2025 are complete (see [[datasets/dynamics-orchard-management-accounts-fye-2025]])
- [[Posh Corporate]] will present the orchard expansion budget proposal at the next quarterly review
- Orchard B's consistent outperformance suggests expansion should prioritise B-varietal planting

## Action Items

- [ ] Sayf: Share MyKAP/KKKP compliance checklist for new agricultural land acquisition
- [ ] Audrey: Circulate orchard expansion feasibility study findings by next week
- [ ] Both: Schedule follow-up meeting mid-July to review budget proposal

## Discussion Notes

Audrey mentioned that the orchard expansion feasibility study (MYR 7,500, captured in the September 2025 management accounts) identified two potential expansion zones. The preferred zone could increase total orchard output by approximately 40%. The land acquisition cost is estimated at MYR 180,000-220,000, with planting and infrastructure adding another MYR 60,000-80,000. Revenue projections suggest the expanded orchard would break even within 18 months.

Audrey specifically noted that Orchard B's margins are 15% higher than Orchard A's, attributed to better soil conditions and more mature trees. She recommended that any expansion should use B-varietal planting stock to maximise ROI.

Discussion explored AI skepticism and market strategy alongside technical collaboration opportunities for business growth.

# HumanLayer — We're live, come build with us

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19edfd1054c17600
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T04:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by Sayf Codes to info@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-19

[[HumanLayer]] is opening up to everyone. Previously waitlist-only, the Agentic IDE and collaboration platform is now publicly available.

From [[Dex Horthy]] (dexter@hey.humanlayer.dev) and Kyle — the team behind the open-source Research, Plan, Implement (RPI) framework now running inside Fortune 500s (Block, Uber).

## What HumanLayer Is

An Agentic IDE, collaboration platform, and building blocks for your software factory — where engineering teams design complex systems with their AI agents before generating a single line of code. It lets engineers solving hard problems in complex codebases:

- Move 2-3x faster across the entire SDLC (not just the coding part)
- Maintain rigorous standards for system architecture and program design

## Core Idea

Don't outsource the thinking. Great teams have run design reviews for decades because there's enormous leverage in collaborating on system design before writing code. HumanLayer brings that missing piece of the software factory into one place.

Bring your own subscription — plug in Claude, Codex, and other AI subscriptions or API keys. No separate per-token bill.

## Links

- HumanLayer: https://humanlayer.com
- Launch post (blurb.md): https://humanlayer.com/launch/blurb.md
- X launch post: https://x.com/dexhorthy/status/2067286892786454855
- LinkedIn launch post: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/dexterihorthy_at-humanlayer-were-on-a-mission-to-solve-ugcPost-7473052840899407872-Zb5U

pages.title: Maloha Coast Navigator 2026 06 15
pages.body:
# The Maloha Coast Navigator

*Your weekly guide to AI strategy for SMBs*

---

## This Week: Why Most AI Pilots Fail

80% of AI proofs-of-concept never reach production. Here's the pattern:

1. **Scope creep** — POCs that try to boil the ocean
2. **Data rot** — Models trained on stale data that's 6 months old
3. **No owner** — AI sits in IT without a business sponsor

### The Fix

Start with a **single workflow**, not a platform. Pick the one manual process your team hates most. Automate that. Measure the time saved. Then expand.

---

## Client Spotlight

A 12-person accounting firm deployed an email triage agent. Result: 14 hours/week saved. ROI in week one.

---

## What We're Reading

- [[Claude Code 10x Skill]] — The skill pattern that changed everything
- [[Second Brain Architecture]] — How we structure knowledge for SMBs

---

## Upcoming

Next week: Pricing models that work for SMB AI consulting.

*— Sayf, Maloha Coast*

**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eca4c3e7e0cefc

pages.title: How provider data policies work on OpenRouter
pages.body:
# How provider data policies work on [[OpenRouter]]

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee03492356403f
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T04:00:00Z
**Provenance:** OpenRouter newsletter to info@malohacoast.com

Every provider behind [[OpenRouter]] has its own retention and training policy. [[OpenRouter]] reflects those policies as structured data on each endpoint, publishes the full table in the docs, and gives you controls to filter routing on them.

## What You Control

1. **Block routing to providers that train** — Account-wide toggle in privacy settings for whether routing may use providers that train on prompts, with separate switches for paid and free models.
2. **Require a specific data policy per request** — Override account default on any single call via data policy filtering in provider routing docs.
3. **Pick only zero-retention providers** — Many providers on [[OpenRouter]] are zero retention: Amazon Bedrock, Azure, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, Together, and others.

## EU Routing

[[OpenRouter]] supports EU in-region routing for enterprise accounts. When enabled, prompts and completions are processed inside the European Union via the https://eu.openrouter.ai base URL.

pages.body:
# 30 Core Agentic Engineering Concepts, Explained Simply

**Source:** email from Sayf Codes, forwarded from Neo Kim (System Design One / Substack)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee2580f5fe7903
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T12:30:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded systemdesignone@substack.com → sayfcodes@gmail.com → info@malohacoast.com

Originally from: The System Design Newsletter by Neo Kim

## Summary

A comprehensive breakdown of 30 core concepts in agentic engineering. Covers the evolution of LLMs, agent architecture patterns, memory systems, reasoning techniques, and infrastructure. The article is gated (requires subscription for full access) but the open portions cover the conceptual landscape.

## Key Concepts (From Open Portion)

(Article content gated — the open portion previewed the following concepts as covered topics:)

### Part 1: Introduction to Agentic Engineering
- Evolution from LLMs to agents — agents perceive, reason, and act
- Framing: agentic engineering > prompt engineering

### Part 2: Agent Architectures
- ReAct (Reasoning + Acting): interleaving reasoning traces with actions
- Reflection: agents examining their own outputs for self-correction
- Tool use: function calling as the bridge between LLMs and external systems

### Part 3: Memory Systems
- Short-term vs long-term memory in agent architectures
- RAG and retrieval patterns

### Part 4: Planning & Reasoning
- Chain-of-thought, tree-of-thought
- Multi-agent collaboration patterns

### Part 5: Infrastructure & Evaluation
- Agent evaluation frameworks
- Production deployment considerations

## Relevance for Maloha Coast

- Every concept maps to Hermes Agent capabilities
- Directly informs how to structure agent prompts, tool use, and memory
- Signals what the broader industry considers "agentic engineering" best practice

pages.title: 30 Core Agentic Engineering Concepts 2026 06 17
pages.body:
# 30 Core Agentic Engineering Concepts, Explained Simply

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee2580f5fe7903
**Ingested:** 2026-06-23T00:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf on 2026-06-20

[[System Design One]] #154 by [[Neo Kim]] and [[Paul Hoekstra]] (guest author).

A comprehensive breakdown of agentic engineering into 30 fundamental concepts across 6 layers: Foundations, Configuration, Capability, Orchestration, Guardrails, and Observability. The thesis: learn the ideas behind the tools, and let the tools come and go.

## Key Concepts

### Foundations (1-5)

1. **What is an agent?** — An LLM that runs in a loop, has access to tools, and decides what to do next. Coding is the most natural use case because of fast feedback from tests and errors. Use agents for open-ended tasks, not simple scripts.

2. **Execution model** — Think → Act → Observe (ReAct pattern). The loop replaces upfront prediction with feedback. This is why agents can recover from their own mistakes.

3. **Agent state** — Everything the agent knows at any moment. Two halves: context window (conversation history + tool results) and persistent state (files, databases, long-term memory).

4. **Tools** — Functions the agent can call: file read/write, shell commands, MCP requests, API calls. The tool interface is the agent's interface to the world.

5. **Tool-use permissions** — Not all tools should be available at all times. Permission boundaries control what the agent can reach.

### Configuration (6-10)

6. **System prompt** — The base instructions that shape agent behavior before it writes any code. Like a constitution for the agent.
7. **Configuration files** — Declarative settings (`.claude.json`, `rules/`) that encode workflow patterns without code changes.
8. **Reusable workflows** — Pre-defined agent processes that can be invoked with different parameters.
9. **Prompt caching** — Keeping recent context in fast memory to reduce latency and cost on repeated patterns.
10. **Context rot** — The degradation of agent performance as the context window fills with irrelevant or stale information.

### Capability (11-15)

11. **Live documentation** — Agents that read docs at runtime rather than at training time, enabling them to work with tools they've never seen before.
12. **AI-native search** — Semantic search as a tool the agent can call during execution.
13. **Long-term memory** — Persistent storage that survives between agent sessions.
14. **Knowledge retrieval** — Querying external knowledge bases without storing the data locally.
15. **Trust boundaries** — What data the agent can read vs. what requires human approval.

### Orchestration (16-20)

16. **Multi-agent coordination** — Multiple agents working on different parts of the same problem.
17. **Subagents** — Child agents spawned by a parent agent to handle specific subtasks. Subagents solve the state-isolation problem that parallel tool calls create.
18. **Task decomposition** — Breaking a complex task into smaller, independent subtasks that can be parallelized.
19. **Handoff protocols** — How one agent transfers context to another.
20. **Merge strategies** — How to combine outputs from parallel agents (git worktrees, structured merge, manual review).

### Guardrails (21-25)

21. **Sandboxing** — Running agents in isolated environments so they can't affect the host system.
22. **Permission hooks** — Interceptors that gate every agent action against a policy before it executes.
23. **Commit gates** — Checks that block problematic changes before they're persisted.
24. **Rate limiting** — Preventing an agent from exhausting API quotas or compute resources.
25. **Audit trails** — Every action logged for review.

### Observability (26-30)

26. **Tracing** — Following the agent's decision chain through each loop iteration.
27. **Logging** — Structured logs of every tool call, result, and decision.
28. **Replay** — Re-running an agent's session to debug or audit its behavior.
29. **Metrics** — Cost per task, success rate, latency, loops per completion.
30. **Human-in-the-loop** — Checkpoints where the agent pauses and waits for approval before proceeding.

## Guest Author

[[Paul Hoekstra]] — data engineer at [[Digital Power]] (data and AI consultancy). Writes Paul's Pipeline newsletter on AI, data, and side projects. Has a four-part series on the layers covered here.

## Relevance

This directly maps to how Hermes agents (falconhermes, MC, Sammy) work. The subagent pattern (concept 17) is how delegate_task works. The tool-use permissions (concept 5) map to toolset configuration. Context rot (concept 10) is the fundamental limitation of long-running agents.

pages.title: 30 Core Agentic Engineering Concepts 2026 06 20
pages.body:
# 30 Core Agentic Engineering Concepts, Explained Simply

**Source:** System Design Newsletter (Neo Kim & Paul Hoekstra)
**Published:** Jun 17, 2026
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T08:04:29+08:00

#154: What an agent is, how to configure one, when to use multiple, and how to keep them from doing damage.

*Guest author: Paul Hoekstra — data engineer at Digital Power, data and AI consultancy; writes Paul's Pipeline.*

## Foundations

### 1. What is an agent?
An agent is an LLM that runs in a loop, has access to tools, and decides what to do next. Instead of a single answer to a prompt, the model produces a chain of actions, each building on the last.

Use an agent when: task is open-ended, the next step depends on the last, you cannot script the path in advance. Skip the agent when a single prompt or plain script will do.

### 2. Execution model (Think-Act-Observe / ReAct)
Three steps, repeated: (1) model thinks — reads current state and decides next action; (2) acts — emits a tool call, harness validates and runs it; (3) observes — tool result joins conversation as context. The loop replaces upfront prediction with feedback, allowing the agent to recover from its own mistakes.

Two variations: parallelism (multiple tool calls in one act step) and blocking vs non-blocking (sync vs async).

### 3. Agent state
Two senses: (1) workflow progress — which step is the agent on; (2) everything the agent knows at any moment. Breaks into: context window (everything the model sees, hard ceiling) and everything outside it (files, DB, memory it fetches on demand). Files are the right default — diffable, versionable, easy to edit. Subagents get a fresh context window, parent passes only what's needed.

### 4. Common agent patterns
- **Planner/executor**: one plans, another executes. Keeps each agent in a suitable context shape.
- **Router/specialist**: router dispatches to specialists with tighter prompts and smaller toolsets. Cheaper, easier to reason about.
- **Map-reduce parallelism**: fan-out across subagents, merge results. Cost bounded by longest subagent.
- These combine in real workflows: planner spawns router → router fans out map-reduce → merged result comes back.

## Configuration Layer

### 5. Agent config files
Project-level Markdown loaded at session start (CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md). The agent reads hard rules before writing code. Useful configs are short and specific: hard limits, actual tooling stack, behavioral rules. Keep under 100 lines.

### 6. Reusable workflow files (skills)
Markdown with YAML frontmatter — name, description, optional globs. Loaded on demand. SkillsBench showed Claude Haiku at 27.7% with human-curated skills beats Opus at 22.0% without them. Model-written skills saw gains disappear — generic AI-generated boilerplate makes things worse.

### 7. Workflow frameworks
Chains of skills, hooks, and slash commands wired into repeatable processes. Examples: Superpowers, Get Shit Done, Compound Engineering. Shared thread: understand what you're building, tell it how to work, check the result.

### 8. Prompt caching
Stores the stable prefix so the model doesn't re-read it every turn. First call pays a small premium to write the cache; later turns read at a discount. Cache TTL ranges from minutes to an hour depending on provider. Without caching you'd think carefully about config file size; with it, the cost is a rounding error.

### 9. Context rot
Model performance drops as context fills up. GPT-5.5: 98.1% on short documents → 74.0% at 1M tokens. Claude Opus 4.7: 59.2% at 256K → 32.2% at 1M. The model has to look past each token to focus on what matters.

## Key Insights
- Learn the ideas behind the tools, not the tools themselves — the pace of change won't slow down.
- Skills/benchmark data proves that good instructions beat a better model.
- Context rot is the hard constraint that all agentic workflows must design around.

READ IN APP
<https://open.substack.com/pub/systemdesignone/p/agentic-engineering?utm_source=email&redirect=app-store&utm_campaign=email-read-in-app>
-
*Share this post
& I'll send you some rewards for the referrals.*
------------------------------
How do you keep up with agentic engineering when a new tool ships every
week?
You do not…
You learn the ideas behind the tools, and let the tools come and go.
The pace will not slow down. New models, new frameworks, a new *“this
changes everything*” launch every few days. Chase all of it, and you spend
more time switching tools than using them.
But underneath the noise, it is the same few ideas every time.
One calls it a skill, the next calls it a rule; it's the same job
underneath.
Learn the idea, and it stops mattering which tool you pick.
By the end of this newsletter, you can read any agent post or news drop and
see which of these ideas it actually covers, instead of feeling behind
every time something new ships.
Onward.
------------------------------
”How to adopt externalized authorization: step-by-step roadmap” eBook by
------------------------------
I want to introduce *Paul Hoekstra
as the guest author.
Paul is a data engineer at Digital Power, a data and AI consultancy, and
writes *Paul’s Pipeline
a free newsletter on AI, data, and side projects.
This newsletter pulls out the ideas that keep mattering, whichever tool you
pick. If it leaves you wanting more, he has a four-part series on the
layers covered in this newsletter: configuration, capability,
orchestration, and guardrails.
------------------------------
*Here’s what you’ll find inside this newsletter:*
-
*Foundations.* What an agent is, how it runs, where its state lives, and
the patterns you build once you have more than one.
-
*Configuration.* How you shape the agent’s behavior before it writes a
line of code: config files, reusable workflows, prompt caching, and the
context rot that limits all of it.
-
*Capability.* What the agent can reach for once it is running: live
documentation, AI-native search, long-term memory, and knowledge it queries
but never owns.
-
*Orchestration.* How you coordinate multiple agents without letting them
step on each other.
-
*Guardrails.* What stops the agents from doing damage: sandboxing,
permissions, hooks, and the commit gates that catch the rest.
-
*Observability.* How you see what an agent actually did after it stopped
typing: tracing, logging, replay, and metrics.
------------------------------
*Foundations*
Let’s dive in!
*1. What is an agent?*
“Agent” gets used for almost anything these days.
Let us pin it down:
An agent is a large language model (*LLM*) that runs in a loop, has access
to tools¹, and decides what to do next. Instead of a single answer to a
prompt, the model produces a chain of actions. And each action builds on
the result of the last.
Coding is the most obvious use case so far.
The loop fits naturally there. Small building blocks. Fast feedback from
tests and errors. Files the agent can edit directly. The same shape works
for research agents, ops automation, customer support, and content
workflows.
Use an agent when:
-
Task is open-ended (”debug this failing test”, not “format this date”)
-
The next step depends on the result of the last one
-
You cannot script the path in advance
But skip the agent when a single prompt or a plain script will do.
Loops are not free.
Every time round the loop costs time and money, and they add up fast. The
agent gets less predictable. And it gets harder to debug: the runs are
longer, and the agent does not always make the same choices twice.
You do not have to pick one mode for everything.
Use a single prompt or a plain script for easy problems. Reserve the agent
for tasks where its flexibility earns the cost.
------------------------------
*2. Execution model*
An agent loop has a recognizable shape.
Three steps, repeated until something says we are done:
-
First, the model *thinks*. It reads the current state of the
conversation and decides what to do next.
-
The model then *acts*. It emits a tool call. The harness² around the
model receives the call, validates it, and runs it: file read, shell
command, MCP request, whatever fits.
-
Finally, the model *observes*. The tool result joins the conversation as
new context. The next iteration starts with that result in scope.
This pattern goes by a few names.
ReAct (from the original paper
Think-act-observe, agent loop.
The name changes, but the loop does not.
A single model call has to predict the entire path up front and commit to
it. The loop replaces that prediction with feedback. The model takes one
step, observes what actually happened, and decides the next step based on
the actual result rather than a guess.
This is why an agent can recover from its own mistakes.
A failing test returns a stack trace. A blocked command returns an error.
Each one is just the next observation, and the next iteration plans against
it. The loop catches a wrong first step on the next pass, instead of
letting it poison everything after it.
Two variations sit on top of this basic loop:
-
*Parallelism*. Modern agents can fire several tool calls inside a single
act step. That cuts wall-clock time on read-heavy work. It also causes
conflicts when two parallel calls try to edit the same thing. We will come
back to ways of handling that when we get to subagents (concept 17).
-
*Blocking vs non-blocking*. Most agents run synchronously: conversation
waits while a tool runs, then the result comes back as the observation.
Some run asynchronously and fire off long-running jobs in the background.
Async unlocks fan-out, at the cost of more complexity.
------------------------------
*3. Agent state*
“State” gets used in two senses.
One is *workflow progress*: which step the agent is on and what is left. *Agent
loops* carry that sense across iterations (concept 17). This concept is the
other sense: everything the agent knows at any moment.
It breaks into two halves with very different properties:
-
*Context window*: everything the model can see right now. Your latest
message, the system prompt, the agent’s previous tool calls, and the
results of those calls. It has a hard ceiling (the token cap) and a soft
one (concept 9, context rot). When a session ends, the context window is
gone.
-
*Everything outside it*: what the model cannot see until it fetches it.
Files on disk, database entries. Memory that survives across sessions
(concept 14). Knowledge the agent only queries and never owns (concept 15).
The model cannot reason over any of it directly, but the agent can pull
pieces into context on demand.
So, where should your state actually live?
Files are usually the right default. They are diffable, versionable, and
easy for both you and the agent to edit. Memory works well for facts that
survive across sessions but do not need git history. Databases earn their
place when the state needs to be queried by structure or shared across
processes.
Multiple agents make the state harder.
Two agents reading the same file is fine. Two agents writing to it is the
canonical race condition. Git worktrees (concept 16) give each agent its
own working copy.
Subagents are friendlier: each starts with a fresh context window, and the
parent passes in only what it needs. If the parent has to pass more than a
few paragraphs, the split between the two is probably wrong.
------------------------------
*4. Common agent patterns*
Once you have multiple agents to work with, the question is how to compose
them.
There are a handful of useful patterns to know:
The simplest is *planner/executor*.
One agent plans, another executes. The split keeps each agent in a context
shape that suits it: open-ended thinking on one side, tight execution on
the other. Useful for long tasks where you want planning quality separate
from how the implementation goes.
*Router/specialist* sits one level above.
A router agent takes the incoming request and dispatches it to one of
several specialists. Each specialist has a tighter system prompt and a
smaller toolset: a security reviewer, a debug specialist, a docs writer, a
test author. Cheaper, because most calls hit a smaller model. Easier to
reason about, because each specialist’s behavior is bounded.
*Map-reduce parallelism* is the workhorse for read-heavy fan-out.
Send a single task out across many subagents and merge their results. *“Review
this pull request” *splits into one subagent per file, and the results
merge into a single summary. Cost is bounded by the longest individual
subagent. Quality is bounded by how well the aggregator merges.
These patterns are not exclusive.
Real workflows combine them: a planner spawns a router, the router fans out
work map-reduce style, and the merged result comes back for review.
Every pattern relies on handoffs between agents.
Each handoff carries a compressed summary, so the next agent does not start
from scratch. That is what makes the patterns useful: they define where one
agent’s job ends and the next one’s begins. Those seams are where work
tends to break.
That’s the foundation done.
Next come the four practical layers, starting with how you shape the
agent’s behavior before it touches any code.
------------------------------
*Get 1+ referral & I’ll send you my Leetcode Master Template!*
Share
<https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1511845&post_id=200995493&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&action=share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=2k46ui&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjoxNTQ3MjAxNzAsInBvc3RfaWQiOjIwMDk5NTQ5MywiaWF0IjoxNzgxNjg5MTE2LCJleHAiOjE3ODQyODExMTYsImlzcyI6InB1Yi0xNTExODQ1Iiwic3ViIjoicG9zdC1yZWFjdGlvbiJ9.CZcfri6GGTQ8GfxA0BTPLT6si-x5-T-85JKeMZi7ABE>
------------------------------
*Configuration Layer*
Let’s dive in!
*5. Agent config files*
Every agent starts with a system prompt.
The harness builder bakes in a block of instructions that covers tool use
and the harness’s own conventions. The system prompt does not know anything
about your project.
Agent config files are the layer above.
The agent loads a project-level Markdown file at the start of every
session, and the file remains in the context window for each subsequent
step. The file tells the agent your standards, limits, conventions, and
behavioral rules.
Claude Code calls this file CLAUDE.md.
Most other tools have settled on AGENTS.md. A bit like Apple and USB-C: the
industry wants a standard, but not everyone follows it.
It matters because agents default to whatever looked most plausible in
their training data. Without a config file, you get pip install when your
project uses uv, black when you use ruff, and defensive sludge wrapped
around every change.
With one, the agent reads a set of hard rules before it writes a line of
code.
A useful config is short and specific:
-
Hard limits on function length
-
The package manager and tooling stack you actually use
-
A few behavioral rules like “never commit secrets” or “always read a
file before editing it.”
Anything generic or AI-generated dilutes the file and makes the rest harder
for the model to attend to.
Keep it under 100 lines. Cut anything that is not consistently improving
outcomes. Treat it the way you would treat a Makefile: as code, not
documentation, reviewed when it changes.
------------------------------
*6. Reusable workflow files*
If config files are always on, reusable workflow files are on demand.
They are still just Markdown, but with YAML frontmatter that controls when
the agent loads them. Examples are Claude Code skills (in .claude/skills/)
and Cursor rules.
The frontmatter³ does most of the work. A name and description tell the
agent when the skill is relevant. An optional globs field narrows it
further by file type or path pattern. Whether the skill actually helps
depends on the quality of the instructions inside it.
Researchers built SkillsBench
a benchmark of 86 tasks across 11 domains, and provided models with brief
written workflows for approaching each task.
Claude Haiku scored 27.7% with human-curated skills. That beat the 22.0%
Opus scored without them. The cheapest model with good instructions
outperformed the flagship without.
There is a second lesson in the data.
When the researchers let the model write its own skills, the gains
disappeared. Given everything we know about context rot, that should
surprise no one. Generic AI-generated boilerplate makes things worse, not
better.
The rough division of labor is simple: *config files* for always-relevant
project rules, *skills* for reusable task-specific procedures.
The live prompt for what is unique about the current task.
------------------------------
*7. Workflow frameworks*
If you are using an agent for coding, a framework on top helps.
The agent follows a documented process for planning, test-driven
development (*TDD*), debugging, and code review. No more falling back on
whatever it remembers from training. The mechanism is a chain of skills,
hooks, and slash commands⁴ wired together into a repeatable process.
*Here are a few options worth knowing:*
-
Superpowers
ships a curated library of skills for common workflows: brainstorming, TDD,
debugging, and code review. It also adds HARD-GATE directives and
anti-rationalization tables. These push the agent to use the skills rather
than skip them.
-
Get Shit Done
does something similar, but through slash commands, hooks, and
meta-prompting instead of skills.
-
Compound Engineering
splits work into Plan, Work, Review, and Compound phases. The last phase
captures patterns and solutions into a searchable knowledge base, so each
feature makes the next one easier.
They differ in shape but share a thread: get the agent to understand what
it is building, tell it how to go to work, and check the result against
what you actually wanted.
------------------------------
*8. Prompt caching*
Prompt caching stores the *stable prefix* of the conversation so the model
does not pay the full price to re-read it every turn.
Most agentic tools handle this in the background, but it is worth
understanding because it changes the economics of long config files.
The mechanism is the same across providers:
The first call sends the full prefix, including the config file and any
reusable workflows the agent has loaded, and pays a small premium to write
that prefix into the cache. Later turns re-read it at a steep discount.
Latency improves, too, because the model does not reprocess the same tokens.
The catch is the cache time to live (*TTL*).
Caches expire after a period of inactivity, somewhere between a few minutes
and an hour, depending on the provider. An active session keeps the cache
warm. A long pause (coffee, Slack, a spec to read) lets it expire and
forces a rewrite on the next call.
If your tooling lets you configure the TTL, longer windows cost more to
write but stay warm through bigger gaps in activity. The choice depends on
how active you expect to be. Longer TTL when you will keep coming back.
Shorter TTL when you might not.
Without prompt caching, you would think carefully about the size of your
config file. With it, the cost is closer to a rounding error.
------------------------------
*9. Context rot*
Model performance drops as the context window fills up.
Prompt caching saves your wallet, but those tokens still sit there, and the
model has to look past each one to get to what you want it to focus on.
*Even the latest models show this:*
-
Drop a few specific facts into a long document and ask a model to find
them. GPT-5.5
scores 98.1% on short documents and 74.0% on documents stretching to a
million tokens.
-
Claude Opus 4.7
drops from 59.2% at 256K to 32.2% at 1M.
A

# 30 Core Agentic Engineering Concepts, Explained Simply

**Source:** email forward
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee2580f5fe7903
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T08:45:11Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf via Gmail

> **Forwarded from:** Neo Kim (System Design One) via Sayf Codes  
> **Original date:** Wed, 17 Jun 2026  
> **Original subject:** 30 Core Agentic Engineering Concepts, Explained Simply

## Entities Mentioned

- **[[Neo Kim]]** — author, founder of [[System Design One]] newsletter
- **[[Paul Hoekstra]]** — guest author, data engineer at [[Digital Power]]
- **[[Paul's Pipeline]]** — Paul Hoekstra's newsletter on AI, data, and side projects

## Summary

A comprehensive breakdown of 30 core agentic engineering concepts organized into six layers: Foundations (what an agent is, execution model, state, patterns), Configuration (config files, reusable workflows, workflow frameworks, prompt caching, context rot), Capability (documentation, AI-native search, long-term memory, query-only knowledge), Orchestration (coordination, handoffs, subagents), Guardrails (sandboxing, permissions, hooks, commit gates), and Observability (tracing, logging, replay, metrics).

The key thesis: learn the ideas behind the tools, not the tools themselves. The same few concepts keep appearing across frameworks — naming them makes every new tool easier to understand.

## Key Concepts Covered

- **Agent loop** — think-act-observe cycle (ReAct pattern)
- **Planner/executor** — one agent plans, another executes
- **Router/specialist** — router dispatches to specialized agents
- **Map-reduce parallelism** — fan-out across subagents, merge results
- **Context rot** — model performance drops as context fills up
- **Prompt caching** — stable prefix caching changes economics of long configs
- **SkillsBench** — benchmark showing Haiku + skills beats Opus without
- **Superpowers, Get Shit Done, Compound Engineering** — workflow frameworks

## People Mentioned

- **[[Neo Kim]]** — Author of System Design One
- **[[Paul Hoekstra]]** — Guest author, data engineer

## Companies Mentioned

- **[[System Design One]]** — Substack publication
- **[[Digital Power]]** — Data and AI consultancy where Paul works

## Related

- [[pauls-pipeline]] — Paul's own newsletter

pages.title: 30 Core Agentic Engineering Concepts, Explained Simply
pages.body:
# 30 Core Agentic Engineering Concepts, Explained Simply

**Source:** email (sayf forward)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee2580f5fe7903
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T08:15:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf on 2026-06-20

**From:** Neo Kim, System Design One (#154)
**Original Date:** Wed, 17 Jun 2026
**Forwarded by:** Sayf → info@malohacoast.com

👋 Hey, it's Neo — This is a special free edition of this newsletter.

## Overview

A guide to 30 core agentic engineering concepts, organized into six layers: Foundations, Configuration, Capability, Orchestration, Guardrails, and Observability. Guest author is Paul Hoekstra, data engineer at Digital Power.

The core thesis: learn the ideas behind the tools, not the tools themselves. Underneath the noise, it's the same few ideas every time.

## Foundations

**1. What is an agent?** An LLM that runs in a loop, has access to tools, and decides what to do next. Use when tasks are open-ended, the next step depends on the last, or you cannot script the path in advance. Skip when a single prompt or script will do — loops are not free.

**2. Execution model** — Three steps (think → act → observe) repeated. Variations: parallelism (multiple tool calls per step) and blocking vs non-blocking (sync vs async).

**3. Agent state** — Context window (everything the model sees, has hard and soft limits) vs everything outside it (files, databases, memory). Files are the right default — diffable, versionable, editable.

**4. Common agent patterns** — Planner/executor, router/specialist, map-reduce parallelism. Real workflows combine them.

## Configuration Layer

**5. Agent config files** — Project-level Markdown (CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md). Short, specific, under 100 lines.

**6. Reusable workflow files** — On-demand Markdown with YAML frontmatter. SkillsBench showed Claude Haiku at 27.7% with human-curated skills beat Opus at 22.0% without them.

**7. Workflow frameworks** — Superpowers, Get Shit Done, Compound Engineering provide documented processes.

**8. Prompt caching** — Stores stable prefix. Cache TTL matters: longer windows stay warm through bigger activity gaps.

**9. Context rot** — Model performance drops as context fills. GPT-5.5: 98.1% at short vs 74.0% at 1M tokens. Claude Opus 4.7: 59.2% at 256K to 32.2% at 1M.

## Key People

- **Neo Kim** — Author of System Design One newsletter
- **Paul Hoekstra** — Guest author, data engineer at Digital Power, writes Paul's Pipeline

## Related

- [[System Design One]] — Publication
- [[Digital Power]] — Paul's employer

pages.title: The Skill That 10x'd My Claude Code Projects
pages.body:
# The Skill That 10x'd My Claude Code Projects

Sharper Claude context, Carson's first automation win, and Anthropic's $965B IPO filing

**June 05, 2026**

## In This Issue

- The single technique that makes every Claude project sound less like everyone else and more like you
- Carson landed his first automation client with a free pilot — here's the exact move
- Anthropic just filed for IPO at $965 billion — what it means if Claude is your primary tool

---

## New Video: The Skill That 10x'd My Claude Code Projects

If your Claude outputs feel generic — technically correct but missing your voice — the problem is not the model. Everyone using Claude Opus 4.8 starts from the same foundation. The difference between outputs that sound like you and ones that sound like everyone else is context.

The **Grill Me skill** fixes that. Instead of a five-minute brain dump before you start building, it interviews you until it actually understands how you think. It asks one question at a time, checkpoints your answers to a markdown file in your project, and keeps going until there are no gaps. By the end, you have reusable context your AI pulls from every time you touch that skill or project.

**The mistake most people make:** Starting with a brain dump and calling it good enough. Five minutes of info-dumping gets your output quality to about 70% on iteration one. The Grill Me approach gets you to 90% from the start — fewer revision cycles, faster time to a deliverable clients actually like.

**How this helps you:** When Claude knows your voice, your decisions, and your process, it stops generating generic output. Your projects feel like yours. Clients notice. You spend less time fixing what the AI got wrong.

---

## Winning Is All We Do: Off the sidelines — Carson landed his first automation client

**What happened:** Carson built a lead automation for a local aesthetics clinic as a free pilot. The workflow routes contact data and handles follow-ups automatically. He's waiting on Facebook credentials to go live, but the clinic owner is already highly receptive. His plan: deliver the pilot, then position for a paid monthly retainer.

**The opportunity for you:** Local service businesses (clinics, salons, studios) are some of the easiest first clients to land. They have real lead problems, a low technology bar, and if you deliver, they talk to other business owners. A free pilot with a clear upsell path is one of the cleanest ways to get your first paying automation client.

Stories like this happen every week inside AIS+. AIS Live (July 11-12) is where you see exactly how: practitioners walking through real projects on stage, from first outreach to signed invoice. Early bird GA tickets are $97 through June 7.

---

## In the News: Anthropic filed for IPO at $965 billion

**The news:** On June 1, Anthropic — the company behind Claude — confidentially submitted an S-1 to the SEC for a proposed initial public offering. Valuation: $965 billion, putting it ahead of OpenAI ($852 billion after their last funding round). Anthropic joins SpaceX and OpenAI in what analysts are already calling a race to go public — all three expected to list this year.

**How it affects automation:** When your primary AI tool's parent company goes public, two things typically follow: more capital to build better models (good for you), and quarterly earnings pressure that tends to push enterprise-first roadmaps and price adjustments upward. This is not a reason to panic. It is a reason to build your skills and client base now, before the landscape shifts again. The practitioners who get in early always have the advantage.

---

## AIS Live — July 11-12, 2026

**Who it's for:** You're building AI skills but haven't made the leap to income yet. You want to see the full picture: what practitioners sell, what clients pay, and how the deals close.

**How it helps:** AIS Live is a two-day event where real practitioners walk through real client engagements on stage. Day 1, you see the exact projects being sold right now and build one live. Day 2 covers exactly how to get clients, even if you're a beginner with no credibility yet. General Admission starts at $97 (early bird ends June 7, then the price goes up).

pages.title: Ai Data Center Architecture 2026 06 19
pages.body:
# The Secret Architecture Behind AI Data Centers

When people talk about Artificial Intelligence (*AI*), they often refer to
models such as Generative Pre-trained Transformers (*GPTs*), Large Language
Models (*LLMs*), and neural networks.

It sounds like magic, software that *thinks*…

But here’s the uncomfortable truth:

The biggest breakthroughs in AI are not coming from better models; they’re
coming from better infrastructure.

If AI were only about the models, there would be no need for huge,
warehouse-sized data centers packed with specialized hardware and powered
by massive amounts of electricity.

So what’s actually going on inside those buildings?

To make sense of everything that follows, we need a simple mental model.

Let’s find out one layer at a time…

Onward.
------------------------------
*---------------------------

I’d like to introduce *Ashish Prajapati
<https://substack.com/redirect/681e4821-11cc-4a93-9af5-80bc26a7e017?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>*
.
<https://substack.com/redirect/681e4821-11cc-4a93-9af5-80bc26a7e017?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

He is an AI infrastructure specialist and technology educator who helps
engineers understand the technologies powering modern AI.

With expertise spanning data centers, cloud computing, GPU infrastructure,
Generative AI, and Agentic AI, he focuses on turning complex concepts into
practical, real-world knowledge.

His training programs have reached over 39,000 learners worldwide, and
through his free mentoring initiative, Become a Solutions Architect (BeSA)
<https://substack.com/redirect/30f898b6-b928-47a1-9d3b-b30d12b24f91?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>,
he has helped over 23,000 professionals accelerate their careers.

As AI adoption continues to grow, understanding the infrastructure behind
AI systems is becoming a valuable skill for architects, engineers, and IT
professionals.

For a limited time, this newsletter's readers can use code *SYSTEM-DESIGN
to get 25% off Ashish’s live NCA-AIIO bootcamp
<https://substack.com/redirect/681e4821-11cc-4a93-9af5-80bc26a7e017?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>*
.

This intensive one-day workshop covers the fundamentals of NVIDIA AI
infrastructure, GPU networking, accelerated computing, and AI operations,
and prepares you for the NVIDIA-Certified Associate: AI Infrastructure and
Operations (NCA-AIIO) certification.

*Learn, Prepare, and Pass NCA-AIIO in a Day
<https://substack.com/redirect/681e4821-11cc-4a93-9af5-80bc26a7e017?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>*.

------------------------------

*Here’s what you’ll find inside this newsletter:*

-

*The hidden architecture behind AI.* Why models are only the visible tip
of a much larger infrastructure stack.
-

*The physics that limit AI growth.* How power, cooling, and space shape
every AI data center.
-

*Why GPUs became the backbone of AI.* The architectural advantages that
made modern AI training possible.
-

*The real bottlenecks in modern AI.* Why memory, networking, and
coordination determine performance at scale.
-

*How thousands of GPUs work together as one system. *The software,
networking, and orchestration layers that enable large-scale AI.

Golden members get all posts like these!…
Upgrade to paid
<https://substack..TPBY7LfeDQSNxd8lMq_bZ2a3zPdKO8SDT2VH0PpWhMk?&utm_medium=email&utm_source=subscribe-widget-preamble&utm_content=200923722>
------------------------------
Think of an AI Data Center as a Factory

Before we go deep, build this mental model; it will explain almost
everything that follows:

-

AI Data Center = Factory
-

Data = Raw material
-

Graphics Processing Units (*GPUs*) = Machines on the factory floor
-

Network = Internal transport system
-

Storage = Warehouse holding raw materials
-

Software = Assembly line logic
-

Output = Trained intelligence

<https://substack.com/redirect/5e45622c-2bb8-41c9-8918-1ecab585affc?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

Now imagine a car factory.

It doesn’t just need skilled workers; instead, it needs conveyors,
warehouses, and power. Remove any of those, and production stops.

An AI data center works the same way…

AI infrastructure processes vast amounts of data using mathematical
operations, similar to how a factory transforms raw materials into finished
goods.

Remove any one ingredient, and the entire system stalls…

Once you see AI as a factory, the next question becomes obvious: *what
limits how fast this factory can run?*

Share
<https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1511845&post_id=200923722&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&action=share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=2k46ui&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjoxNTQ3MjAxNzAsInBvc3RfaWQiOjIwMDkyMzcyMiwiaWF0IjoxNzgxODYwNDAzLCJleHAiOjE3ODQ0NTI0MDMsImlzcyI6InB1Yi0xNTExODQ1Iiwic3ViIjoicG9zdC1yZWFjdGlvbiJ9.YdmtNfofj9kNOvfhe0cEAH64YdVJ2yKsV0IJWCJV8Dw>
------------------------------
First Problem: Physics

*Before AI is a software problem, it’s a physics problem…*
<https://substack.com/redirect/1e36bdff-cf91-4ab9-b759-4f5cd80e9915?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

Here are three hard physical constraints that shape every design decision
in an AI data center:
1. Power

Modern AI workloads rely on high-density GPUs that consume enormous amounts
of electricity.

A single server rack of GPUs can draw 80-120 kilowatts. i.e., roughly
equivalent to 40 homes running at full load simultaneously. Yet you can’t
just add more GPUs forever.

You’re limited by how much electricity your facility can physically receive
and distribute.
2. Cooling

More power means more heat, and heat is the enemy…

Here’s what happens if you don’t remove it fast enough:

-

Hardware fails prematurely,
-

Performance throttles back to avoid damage,
-

In extreme cases, systems shut down entirely.

To manage the intense heat generated by AI workloads, modern data centers
are increasingly adopting *liquid cooling*, which is far more effective
than conventional air cooling.

Coolant is circulated through cold plates attached directly to GPUs and
CPUs, absorbing heat much more efficiently than air. The warmed liquid is
then pumped to heat exchangers, where the heat is removed before the
coolant is recirculated.

This approach enables higher rack densities, lower cooling power
consumption, and more consistent performance for large-scale AI training
and inference¹ workloads.
<https://substack.com/redirect/e06be2b6-153f-4c1b-961f-e2b90cb22b2e?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>
3. Space

Even if you had unlimited chips and unlimited power, you still need racks
to mount them and floor space to put those racks.

Scaling AI is not just software--it’s real estate, energy infrastructure,
and thermodynamics combined.
Takeaway

A data center is, at its core, a power plant combined with a massive,
precisely controlled air-conditioning system. Every engineering decision
flows from managing this reality.

After dealing with the physical challenges, the next question is: *how we
perform the computation?*
------------------------------
Compute: Why GPUs Took Over

To understand why GPUs dominate AI, picture a very simple task…

Imagine you have 1,000 fence posts to paint:

-

Option A: One expert painter, working post by post. Slow, but the
painter can handle any unexpected situation--a broken post, a curved
section.
-

Option B: 1,000 workers, each painting exactly one post simultaneously.
Extremely fast, but each worker knows only one simple task.

That’s the difference between a CPU and a GPU.
CPU (Central Processing Unit)

-

A few very powerful cores
-

Handles complex, varied, sequential tasks
-

Acts as the “manager” making decisions and coordinating work

GPU (Graphics Processing Unit)

-

Thousands of smaller, simpler cores
-

Handles same simple operation repeated millions of times, all at once
-

Acts as the “factory workers” doing mass production

AI training repeatedly performs matrix multiplications billions of times,
making minor adjustments after each step. GPUs are specifically designed
for this type of work.
DPU (Data Processing Unit)

-

As AI systems grow, a third processor becomes important.
-

DPU handles networking, storage I/O, and security operations--tasks that
would otherwise steal cycles from the GPU and CPU.

*Here’s the system view:*

-

CPU = manager (decision-making)
-

GPU = factory workers (mass production)
-

DPU = logistics and operations (keeping materials moving and systems
secure)

<https://substack.com/redirect/b68e2ff4-8896-4f5d-bfe6-b818215dea42?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

But even with powerful GPUs, computation alone isn’t the limiting factor;
the real challenge is keeping them fed with data…

Share
<https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1511845&post_id=200923722&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&action=share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=2k46ui&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjoxNTQ3MjAxNzAsInBvc3RfaWQiOjIwMDkyMzcyMiwiaWF0IjoxNzgxODYwNDAzLCJleHAiOjE3ODQ0NTI0MDMsImlzcyI6InB1Yi0xNTExODQ1Iiwic3ViIjoicG9zdC1yZWFjdGlvbiJ9.YdmtNfofj9kNOvfhe0cEAH64YdVJ2yKsV0IJWCJV8Dw>
------------------------------
Hidden Bottleneck: Memory and Storage

Here’s something that surprises most people:

GPUs don’t fail because they’re slow. They fail because they’re
hungry—constantly starved for data.

<https://substack.com/redirect/e20c200f-be89-420c-bba9-c3fd72bb261e?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>
Memory Hierarchy

Data doesn’t live in one place. It moves through a layered system, trading
speed for capacity:

-

GPU Memory (HBM²): Blazingly fast but tiny; usually tens of gigabytes.
This is the GPU’s working memory.
-

CPU RAM: Larger (hundreds of gigabytes), but slower than HBM.
-

SSD/NVMe³ Storage: Enormous capacity at a lower cost, but still slower.
-

Network/Object Storage: Nearly unlimited, but the slowest link in the
chain.

Every time the GPU needs data that isn’t already in its fast local memory,
it must wait... And a GPU sitting idle costs just as much money as one
doing useful work.

*Takeaway*

AI performance is not determined solely by how fast the GPU computes.
Instead, by how fast data can be fed into the GPU.

A faster chip with a slow data pipeline is like a faster car stuck in
traffic.
Tiered storage in practice

In real AI data centers, storage is organized into tiers that roughly map
to hot, warm, and cold data:

-

*Hot*: NVMe SSDs holding active training datasets and model checkpoints.
-

*Warm*: Parallel file systems⁴, such as Lustre, General Parallel File
System (*GPFS*), provide high-speed shared access across many GPUs.
-

*Cold*: Object storage, such as Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon
S3), for storing raw datasets and older checkpoints.

As models grow larger, a single GPU is no longer enough…

Techniques such as model sharding⁵ and distributed training⁶ distribute the
model across multiple GPUs and memory layers.

And once data starts moving across machines, a new bottleneck appears: *how
those machines communicate with each other.*

*Reminder: this is a teaser of the subscriber-only newsletter series,
exclusive to my golden members.*

When you upgrade, you’ll get:

-

*High-level architecture of real-world systems.*
-

Deep dive into how popular real-world systems actually work.
-

*How real-world systems handle scale, reliability, and performance.*

Unlock Full Access
<https://substack.com/redirect/bc198849-e83f-4dfb-9489-9752289ffd6f?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>
------------------------------
Networking: Bottleneck Nobody Talks About

When training a large AI model, you typically use thousands of GPUs working
together rather than a single GPU…

And those thousands of GPUs must constantly communicate with each other,
sharing what they’ve learned at every step…
<https://substack.com/redirect/416747cc-b41c-43c4-b795-c8e297cddfcd?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

Every second a GPU waits for data or synchronization is a second of lost
productivity and wasted investment.
Cluster topology and fabrics

Modern AI clusters typically use leaf-spine network architectures⁷: *a
design built for high throughput and low latency at scale*.

Every GPU server can reach every other GPU server in *at most ‘two’ hops*.

Within a node, GPUs talk over NVLink (intra-node fabric). Between nodes,
traffic flows over InfiniBand or RDMA over Converged Ethernet (*RoCE*), the
inter-node fabric⁸.

There’s also a clear speed hierarchy:

-

NVLink⁹: fastest path, a direct, private highway connecting GPUs within
the same server.
-

InfiniBand/RoCE¹⁰: very high-speed interconnect between separate
servers. Imagine it as the “bullet train” of the data center.
-

Ethernet: general-purpose, flexible, and ubiquitous, but not designed
for AI’s most extreme bandwidth and latency demands.

Amdahl’s Law: Why More GPUs Aren’t Always Better

There’s a fundamental principle in distributed computing called *Amdahl’s
Law*.

In plain English: if parts of your system must wait for other parts, adding
more workers eventually stops helping.

In AI workloads, adding more GPUs increases computing power but also
increases communication overhead. As GPUs spend more time sharing and
synchronizing data, the bottleneck gradually shifts from computation to
communication.
Smarter data movement

Modern AI systems address this with several techniques:

-

Remote Direct Memory Access (*RDMA*): Provides low-latency,
high-throughput communication by enabling direct memory-to-memory data
transfers between servers.
-

RDMA kernel¹¹ bypass: Lets the network adapter access application memory
directly from user space¹², eliminating unnecessary operating system
overhead.
-

GPUDirect: GPUs can read from and write to storage or network cards
directly, bypassing the CPU entirely.
-

Zero-copy pipelines: Data takes the shortest path from source to GPU,
with no redundant copying along the way.

The goal of AI networking is to make thousands of GPUs behave like one
giant, unified processor, as if the data never had to travel at all.

*Once communication is in place, the next challenge is dividing the work
efficiently across all those GPUs…...*

pages.title: The Secret Architecture Behind AI Data Centers
pages.body:
# The Secret Architecture Behind AI Data Centers

**Source:** email from Sayf Codes, forwarded from Neo Kim (System Design One / Substack)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee23d6478fc08e
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T12:30:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded systemdesignone@substack.com → sayfcodes@gmail.com → info@malohacoast.com

Originally from: The System Design Newsletter by Neo Kim. Featuring guest author Ashish Prajapati (AI infrastructure specialist).

## Summary

A deep dive into the physical and architectural reality of AI data centers. Covers why AI infrastructure is as much a physics problem as a software one — power, cooling, compute, memory hierarchy, and networking at scale.

## Key Ideas

### Mental Model: AI Data Center as a Factory
- AI Data Center = Factory
- Data = Raw material
- GPUs = Machines on the factory floor
- Network = Internal transport system
- Storage = Warehouse holding raw materials
- Software = Assembly line logic
- Output = Trained intelligence

### Three Physical Constraints
1. **Power**: Single server rack of GPUs draws 80-120 kW (~40 homes at full load). Limited by what the facility can physically receive and distribute.
2. **Cooling**: More power = more heat. Liquid cooling is replacing air cooling — coolant circulated through cold plates attached directly to GPUs/CPUs. Enables higher rack densities, lower cooling power consumption.
3. **Space**: Unlimited chips + unlimited power still needs racks and floor space. AI scaling is real estate, energy infrastructure, and thermodynamics combined.

### Compute Architecture
- **CPU** = manager (complex, varied, sequential tasks; coordinates)
- **GPU** = factory workers (thousands of simple cores for parallel matrix multiplication)
- **DPU** = logistics (handles networking, storage I/O, security — frees GPU/CPU)

AI training is repeatedly performing matrix multiplications billions of times — GPUs are designed for this.

### Memory Hierarchy
- **GPU Memory (HBM)**: Blazingly fast, tiny (tens of GB) — GPU's working memory
- **CPU RAM**: Hundreds of GB, slower than HBM
- **SSD/NVMe**: Enormous capacity, slower still
- **Network/Object Storage**: Nearly unlimited, slowest link

The bottleneck: GPUs don't fail because they're slow — they fail because they're starved for data. AI performance = how fast data can be fed into the GPU, not how fast the GPU computes.

### Networking at Scale
- Modern AI clusters use leaf-spine architecture: every GPU server reaches every other in at most 2 hops
- **NVLink**: fastest path, intra-node GPU communication
- **InfiniBand/RoCE**: inter-node, "bullet train" of the data center
- **Ethernet**: general-purpose but not designed for AI's extreme demands
- **Amdahl's Law**: adding more GPUs eventually stops helping because communication overhead grows

### Smarter Data Movement
- **RDMA**: direct memory-to-memory data transfers between servers
- **GPUDirect**: GPUs read/write to storage/network directly, bypassing CPU
- Zero-copy pipelines: data takes shortest path with no redundant copying

### About the Guest Author
- **Ashish Prajapati**: AI infrastructure specialist and technology educator
- Training programs reached 39,000+ learners worldwide
- Runs BeSA (Become a Solutions Architect) mentoring initiative — helped 23,000+ professionals
- Offers NCA-AIIO bootcamp (NVIDIA-Certified Associate: AI Infrastructure and Operations)

## Relevance for Maloha Coast

- Understanding AI infrastructure informs deployment decisions for Hermes Agent hosting
- GPU topology knowledge relevant if running local models
- Ashish Prajapati's BeSA program may be a useful resource for the team
- The factory mental model is useful for explaining AI infrastructure to clients

pages.body:
# AI Garage Weekly — June 19, 2026

**Source:** AI Garage Weekly (Tonbi, Beehiiv), forwarded by Sayf
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee2c24d5819622
**Ingested:** 2026-06-24T04:30:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf on 2026-06-20

Excel, Fable Ban, GLM-5.2, and DR-DCI

Between the Fable ban and the GLM-5.2 release, it was a pretty big week for the local LLM community.

## 🎬 Video of the Week: Hermes Agent in Excel

Tonbi's wife was talking about using Claude in Excel for her work, and he wondered: Could he do that with Hermes Agent? He researched a way to do it, and after some iteration, found a method that works. You get a chat window right in Excel, plus you can create custom functions with your Hermes.

- [How I Put Hermes Agent Inside Excel (And Made It a Custom Function)](https://link.mail.beehiiv.com/ss/c/u001.maopPdr3OQftpzkXs5OR4-tDkO_ZYupC2QTCbId7IkHkiJxZzzIEuleA0Zxl07q_mIlFIjX1d-qo0fTV8ktpwzR6AzV7NOC9JaoERjTgGRKKpgQr8t9M6Ax6eRdxc2VS1KSZt9LH0au-x505YOJCIHODQTRnFuG4RF0Fca33iwrdB49JxH-Wpzn-QIBG9tCOiNcW0-lZMJIx8ziuwfUR4WLoyIC_B8CQQdw3BksEQ74jMfWREhLH2w5wWjK9POqQ/4rm/2_NJKj_3TB2RDv6Z_iUFxg/h2/h001.Uo1z3BXiQOSpNs0ZoYyWZCZTVSRrzmTQWvnIe6dPxT8)
- [Open source GitHub code](https://link.mail.beehiiv.com/ss/c/u001.qJ5Rzv1y5w9Ogi8ibfVtWgZcfXE2VG8Vw54KSfDWY6h70LFeL-Q7Hx42sHfgOTOHMfMA8Tvh0i2lXuut8kk0h-HXg9zZbozz-7x3AickIscmvoz47v_X2unfOY-IPfm5OqSPCBDoJ7W6GB2UFlen122VfcEGkssl7vh2JO8LNdhT5quXBuoVWvc_bqV3T5MZ0ZZSS0xTlNNgdAuBmngLXc6nngpFV3y34Goi5ri2vNdx-hdK2WRft7oI-HEsR61LRab8ztti1FMpsJDIdZ8pXA/4rm/2_NJKj_3TB2RDv6Z_iUFxg/h3/h001.0XFq9A22xcSOelVpwn2BUywwViPilIFy83_G8h69g1U)

## 📡 AI News & Model Releases

**Claude Fable 5 banned.** Anthropic removed Fable 5 access due to a new directive from the US government. Many people were blown away by Fable and disappointed by this move. Tonbi thinks this specific case is some weird Anthropic/administration negotiation happening in public and will get cleared up eventually, but it highlights the need for local and decentralized AI moving forward.

**GLM-5.2 released.** New open weights model release. It feels only slightly behind Opus 4.8/GPT-5.5 — maybe one generation behind. An incredible feat for an open source model, showing we'll get genuine frontier-level open source models sooner than expected.

## 📄 Research & Experiments

**DR-DCI: Scaling Direct Corpus Interaction via Dynamic Workspace Expansion** (arxiv.org/abs/2606.14885)

A retrieval/workspace pattern that goes beyond RAG. With normal RAG, the agent asks a retriever, gets snippets, then answers. With earlier DCI, the agent gets direct shell/file access to the corpus. The problem: it breaks when the corpus is huge. DR-DCI hides the huge corpus behind a retriever, lets the agent call `pull(query, topK)` to bring relevant docs into a local workspace, then use normal file tools on that smaller workspace. Increases accuracy while improving wall time and costs.

Relevant to Agent Wikis and any agent handling large corpora.

## 💭 Random Brainstorming & Project Progress

**Agent Wikis:** First release day got nearly 10k visitors. Maintaining 5k-7k daily since. Tonbi created a loop so missed topics get researched and autonomously added to the wikis. Exploring premium wikis with Stripe integration, possibly small ads after that — an advertising pipeline where the agent develops, releases, and analyzes ad material autonomously.

# AI Garage Weekly - June 19, 2026

**Source:** email from Sayf (forwarded)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee2c24d5819622
