> Excel, Fable Ban, GLM-5.2, and DR-DCI

Between the Fable ban and the GLM-5.2 release, it was a pretty big week for the local LLM community. One showed why local AI is so important, the other showed that open weights models can approach the top closed frontier models. We just need to lower the hardware barrier.

## Video of the Week: Hermes Agent in Excel

[[Tonbi]]'s wife was using Claude in Excel, and he wondered if he could do it with [[Hermes Agent]]. After a false start with an overly complicated method, he found a simpler approach. The end result: a chat window inside Excel plus custom functions with Hermes. The video didn't go viral but got nice comments. He plans more integrations like this.

[How I Put Hermes Agent Inside Excel](https://link.mail.beehiiv.com/ss/c/u001.maopPdr3OQftpzkXs5OR4-tDkO_ZYupC2QTCbId7IkHkiJxZzzIEuleA0Zxl07q_mIlFIjX1d-qo0fTV8ktpwzR6AzV7NOC9JaoERjTgGRKKpgQr8t9M6Ax6eRdxc2VS1KSZt9LH0au-x505YOJCIHODQTRnFuG4RF0Fca33iwrdB49JxH-Wpzn-QIBG9tCOiNcW0-lZMJIx8ziuwfUR4WLoyIC_B8CQQdw3BksEQ74jMfWREhLH2w5wWjK9POqQ/4rm/2_NJKj_3TB2RDv6Z_iUFxg/h2/h001.Uo1z3BXiQOSpNs0ZoYyWZCZTVSRrzmTQWvnIe6dPxT8)

## AI News & Model Releases

**Claude Fable 5 banned.** [[Anthropic]] removed Fable 5 access due to a new US government directive. [[Tonbi]] wasn't surprised — predicted this direction. Thinks it's some weird Anthropic/admin negotiating in public and will get cleared up eventually, but highlights the need for local and decentralized AI.

**GLM-5.2 released.** Open weights model. [[Tonbi]] gave it tasks in a first look video and has been using it. Feels "only slightly behind Opus 4.8/GPT5.5, maybe one generation behind." Incredible for an open source model, though most people won't be able to run it locally without serious hardware.

## Research & Experiments

**DR-DCI (Dynamic Retrieval-Direct Corpus Interaction).** Paper at arxiv.org/abs/2606.14885. Describes an agent retrieval/workspace pattern combining RAG recall with filesystem agency. Key insight: hide huge corpus behind a retriever, let agent call `pull(query, topK)` to bring relevant docs into a local workspace, then use normal file tools. Increases accuracy while improving wall time and costs. [[Tonbi]] sees clear applications for [[Agent Wikis]].

## Random Brainstorming & Project Progress

**[[Agent Wikis]]:** First release day got nearly 10k visitors (humans and agents), now doing 5k-7k daily. [[Tonbi]] created a loop for missed topics to be autonomously researched and added to wikis. Considering premium wikis with Stripe integration and an autonomous ad pipeline where the agent develops, releases, and optimizes ads.

pages.title: Ai Garage Weekly June 19 2026
pages.body:
# AI Garage Weekly - June 19, 2026

**Source:** email forward from Sayf Codes to Maloha Coast
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee2c24d5819622
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T02:10:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by Sayf Codes on 2026-06-20

*Excel, Fable Ban, GLM-5.2, and DR-DCI*

Between the Fable ban and the GLM-5.2 release, it was a pretty big week for the local LLM community. One showed why local AI is so important, the other showed that it was actually possible to have open weights models that approach the top closed frontier models. We just need to lower the hardware barrier somehow and I think local AI will really explode.

---

## 🎬 Video of the Week: Hermes Agent in Excel

So my wife was talking about using Claude in Excel for her work, and I had the thought: Could I do that with Hermes Agent? I hadn't heard of that type of integration, I wasn't even sure if it was possible, but I started to research a way to do it. The first attempt I actually recorded a full video using a much more complicated, much more frustrating technique. I got to the end of it and asked my agent: Isn't there an easier way to do this? And after some extra research, they said yes! "YOU SHOULD'VE TOLD ME THAT TO BEGIN WITH" I screamed in my head, not wanting to upset my agent too much (lol). In the end that was the method I ended up recording for this video, it does require some prep work to start up, but the end result is pretty cool. You get a chat window right in Excel, plus you can create custom functions with your Hermes. I was excited for this video and while I got a lot of nice comments on it, it didn't go super viral as I had hoped (lol).

Video: [How I Put Hermes Agent Inside Excel (And Made It a Custom Function)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=...)

---

## 📡 AI News & Model Releases

**Claude Fable 5 banned.** Last Friday the big news came out that Anthropic was removing Fable 5 access due to a new directive from the US government. It was really surprising news, a lot of people were really blown away by Fable and disappointed by this move. I was disappointed too, but I've been convinced this was the direction we were headed anyway. These models are so powerful, the government isn't going to let private companies and individuals have power like that. They'll want to control it. I think this specific case is just some weird Anthropic and the current administration negotiating in public and it will get cleared up eventually, but it still highlights the need for local and decentralized AI moving forward. You can't let all that power be handed to only the current administration, or 1-2 companies, people should have access to these tools.

**GLM-5.2 released.** I've been impressed with GLM models before, so when I heard about this new open weights model release, I was excited to try. It was really impressive! I gave it some tasks in my first look video and I've been using it a bit since then, and it does feel like only slightly behind Opus 4.8/GPT5.5, maybe one generation behind. This is still an incredible feat for an open source model, and it does make me think that we will get genuine frontier-level open source models sooner than we think. Now of course most people won't be able to run them locally, it would take some serious hardware to be able to run it that isn't super slow and quality-degraded, but it just shows what is possible for the future.

---

## 📄 Research & Experiments

**Dr-DCI: Scaling Direct Corpus Interaction via Dynamic Workspace Expansion** (arXiv:2606.14885)

With Agent Wikis, one of the big things I've been researching is retrieval, how agents are able to collect and process information. I wanted to see if I could find a way to go beyond RAG, or enhance RAG in some way. This paper caught my eye. It describes an agent retrieval/workspace pattern that could be effective for Agent Wikis, or having any agent handle any large corpus.

With normal RAG, you have the agent ask a retriever, get snippets, and then answer from those snippets. With DCI, an earlier version of this method, the agent gets direct shell/file access to the corpus, and can use tools like find, read, compare files, inspect evidence. The problem with this is that it breaks when the corpus is huge. Search millions of files directly is slow and noisy. So with DR-DCI, that huge corpus is hidden behind a retriever, but the agent can call pull(query, topK), to bring relevant documents into a local workspace, then use normal file tools on that smaller workspace. So it actually gives you RAG-like recall with filesystem agency from DCI. They found that using this method increased accuracy as well as significantly improved wall time and costs.

The applications for Agent Wikis is pretty clear. I experimented with RAG over the wikis and found that to be one the best methods, but some of these wikis are quite large, so it could become challenging. Since these are specifically agent focused, I think having this smaller workspace to pull relevant chunks out could be useful, because I imagine people are using this knowledge not for just one-off questions, but for ongoing project work.

---

## 💭 Random Brainstorming & Project Progress

**Agent Wikis:** Nice release for Agent Wikis, people seemed to really like it and the first release day got nearly 10k visitors on the site (humans and agents), and we've been doing 5k-7k since then. Very large improvement from the last time I released a project before my YouTube channel (I was happy with 10 visitors, lol). I've been refining the pipeline to maintaining these wikis, trying to make it as autonomous as possible without ruining everything. The one thing I did was to create a loop so that missed topics will get researched and then autonomously added to the wikis. This will ensure that the wikis actually answer the questions that people have on these topics (without any extra work from me).

I've been exploring other ways to allow agents to maintain and enhance the product. I think once I'm ready to do premium wikis with the stripe integration, that would be a good place for it. I may also explore doing some small ads after that to see how those can drive traffic. I would love to do an advertising pipeline where the agent develops all the ad material (so I can be lazy), releases the ads, then analyzes the results and hones the ads based on that. I've heard of people doing this, but that would be an amazing loop to have. The ads themselves would cost money though, so I'll need the site to be profitable before I try that.

pages.title: Anatomy Of Openclaw 2026 06 08
pages.body:
# The Anatomy of OpenClaw

**Source:** Email ([[System Design One]] / [[Neo Kim]] newsletter forwarded by Sayf)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ea8014fa960cb7
**Ingested:** 2026-06-24T12:30:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by Sayf on 2026-06-09

## Summary

Deep dive into [[OpenClaw]], an open-source autonomous AI agent that bridges the gap between
LLM reasoning (thinking layer) and real-world execution (act layer). Published in The
System Design Newsletter #151 by [[Neo Kim]].

## Key Architecture Layers

### Gateway
Always-on Node.js server on port 18789. Coordinates messaging apps, LLM, tools, and memory.
Intercepts destructive commands for approval. Serves Control UI at localhost:18789. Supports
hot config reloads. Accessible via Tailscale from any device.

### Channel Layer
Plugin-based abstraction: each messaging platform (Telegram, WhatsApp, Slack, Discord) gets
a translation adapter. Standardised internal format (identity key, content, metadata,
attachments) makes everything after this boundary platform-agnostic.

### LLM Layer
Model-agnostic ("brain socket"). System prompt assembled from agent personality + skills +
memory + conversation history. Tool call loop: model signals action → [[OpenClaw]] executes →
result fed back → model decides next step. Compaction auto-triggers at context limit.

### Plugin System
Almost everything non-core is a plugin. Hooks into lifecycle events (before/after tool calls,
messages, session start/end). Enables audit logging, rate limiting, custom approval flows
without touching core.

### Memory System
Three-layer architecture: short-term (session files on disk), long-term (indexed local DB
searched before every answer), standing notes (permanent briefing Markdown files).

### Long-Running Tasks
Long tasks (scraping, test suites) acknowledged immediately, worked on asynchronously, result
delivered when done. State persisted to disk, not held in memory. Cron jobs in isolated
session mode for recurring background work.

## What Makes It Different
- Always-on daemon, not browser-tab-reliant
- 5,400+ modular skills on [[ClawHub]]
- Multi-channel with shared context
- Proactive via Heartbeat + cron
- Model-agnostic (any LLM provider)

pages.title: Claude Second Brain Explained 2026 06 20
pages.body:
# Every Level of a Claude Second Brain Explained

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee2dfa87f86548
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T12:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf on 2026-06-20, originally from Nate Herk (AI Automation Society)

## Summary

A breakdown of five levels of building a Claude second brain, a client email pipeline automation case study, and Codex hitting 5M weekly users with banked rate limit resets.

## In This Issue

- Five levels of building a Claude second brain, from basic file search to fully autonomous recall.
- Robert automated a client's outbound email pipeline and got their call center actually following up.
- OpenAI's Codex just crossed 5 million weekly users, and there's a free bonus available through June 24.

## New Video: Every Level of a Claude Second Brain Explained

There are five distinct levels to building a Claude second brain, and most people are stuck at level one.

Level one is just finding a file by searching an exact word. Level five is a fully autonomous system that knows your business, your client relationships, and your IP better than you do. The difference between them isn't the tool. It's how the data is organized.

*The insight that changes how you build:* Start from the end. Before you organize a single file, ask how you'll access this data later. The shape of the basketball determines the size of the hoop. Jon shows his actual setup (a Herku project, just markdown files and folders) and walks through what separates each level, including semantic search (finding by meaning, not exact match) and full relationship mapping.

*How this helps you:* Your moat is your data. This video shows you how to actually organize it so your agents can use it, not just find it.

## Winning Is All We Do

Robert automated a client's outbound email pipeline from scratch.

*What happened:* Robert's client had a call center calling leads, but nothing was following up by email. Sales managers emailed Robert their daily lead lists as Excel files. He built an automation that handles the rest: when the file lands in his inbox, it extracts the email, address, and name data, normalizes it, and uploads every contact directly into Instantly as a ready-to-run campaign. No manual cleanup. No copy-paste.

*The opportunity for you:* Businesses running call centers almost always have messy lead data and no email follow-up. An automation that converts a spreadsheet into a live campaign solves a problem they didn't know anyone could fix.

## In the News

Codex just hit 5 million weekly users. If you use it, there's a free bonus through June 24.

*The news:* OpenAI Codex has crossed 5 million weekly active users and launched two features alongside the milestone. One is permanent: banked rate limit resets. Instead of your weekly limit resetting on a fixed timer, you can now save resets and use them when you actually need them. The other runs through June 24: Plus and Pro users can invite up to three friends to try Codex. When a friend sends their first Codex message, you both get a bonus reset.

*How it affects you:* If you've been hitting Codex rate limits at the wrong moment mid-build, the banked reset system fixes that. You decide when to use your reset, not the clock. If you're not hitting limits yet, the referral is easy headroom to bank before the window closes. The bigger signal here: Codex is now growing through referral mechanics at 5 million weekly users.

- [[OpenAI]]'s Codex just crossed 5 million weekly users, with a free bonus available through June 24.

---

## New Video: Every Level of a Claude Second Brain Explained

There are five distinct levels to building a Claude second brain, and most people are stuck at level one.

Level one is just finding a file by searching an exact word. Level five is a fully autonomous system that knows your business, your client relationships, and your IP better than you do. The difference between them isn't the tool. It's how the data is organized.

**The insight:** Start from the end. Before you organize a single file, ask how you'll access this data later. The shape of the basketball determines the size of the hoop. Jon shows his actual setup (a Herku project, just markdown files and folders) and walks through what separates each level, including semantic search (finding by meaning, not exact match) and full relationship mapping.

**How this helps you:** Your moat is your data. This video shows you how to actually organize it so your agents can use it, not just find it.

---

## Winning Is All We Do: Robert Automated a Client's Outbound Email Pipeline

Robert's client had a call center calling leads, but nothing was following up by email. Sales managers emailed Robert their daily lead lists as Excel files. He built an automation that handles the rest: when the file lands in his inbox, it extracts the email, address, and name data, normalizes it, and uploads every contact directly into Instantly as a ready-to-run campaign.

**The opportunity:** Businesses running call centers almost always have messy lead data and no email follow-up. An automation that converts a spreadsheet into a live campaign solves a problem they didn't know anyone could fix.

---

## In the News: Codex Hits 5M Weekly Users

[[OpenAI]] Codex has crossed 5 million weekly active users and launched two features alongside the milestone:

1. **Banked rate limit resets (permanent):** Save resets and use them when you actually need them, instead of resetting on a fixed timer.
2. **Referral bonus (through June 24):** Plus and Pro users can invite up to three friends to try Codex. When a friend sends their first message, you both get a bonus reset.

**How it affects you:** If you've been hitting Codex rate limits mid-build, the banked reset system fixes that. The referral is easy headroom to bank before the window closes. Codex growing through referral mechanics at 5M weekly users is a signal of a tool people are actively building with and recommending.

---

*Source: AI Automation Society newsletter, June 19, 2026. Forwarded by Sayf Codes.*

How Virtual Machines Work - A Deep Dive
<https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1511845&post_id=198581056&utm_source=post-email-title&utm_campaign=email-post-title&isFreemail=true&r=2k46ui&token=eyJ1c2...PBso>#148:
Part 1 - DevOps Mastermind

Neo Kim <https://substack.com/@systemdesignone> and Ayaan
<https://substack.com/@ayaan49>
May 25
∙
Preview
<https://substack.com/@systemdesignone> <https://substack.com/@ayaan49>

<https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1511845&post_id=198581056&utm_source=substack&isFreemail=true&submitLike=true&token=eyJ1c2...PeHI&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=email-reaction&r=2k46ui>
<https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1511845&post_id=198581056&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&isFreemail=true&comments=true&token=eyJ1c2...PBso&r=2k46ui&utm_campaign=email-half-magic-comments&action=post-comment&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email>

READ IN APP
<https://open.substack.com/pub/systemdesignone/p/virtualization-architecture?utm_source=email&redirect=app-store&utm_campaign=email-read-in-app>


Get my system design playbook for FREE on newsletter signup:
Upgrade to paid
<https://substack.com/redirect/2/eyJlIj...ItTY?&utm_medium=email&utm_source=subscribe-widget-preamble&utm_content=198581056>

   -

   *Share this post
   <https://substack.com/redirect/349d5210-3a29-4e4e-9043-ba97b535bcff?j=eyJ1Ij...Z-GI>


I have a huge huge huge announcement for everyone…

INTRODUCING: *DevOps Mastermind*

This newsletter series will help you become a fast-growing and high-paying
engineer.

If you’ve ever thought:

*“I use Docker & Kubernetes, but I still don’t fully understand how they
work.”*

*“I want to master DevOps fundamentals instead of just memorizing
commands.”*

*“I should learn how modern infrastructure & real-world systems actually
work.”*

Then this is for you.

Here’s what you’ll get inside DevOps Mastermind:

   -

   *Architecture breakdown of core DevOps systems.*
   -

   Deep dives into Docker, Kubernetes, Terraform, and so on.
   -

   *Practical insights into how real-world systems achieve scalability,
   automation, and reliability.*

Onward.
------------------------------

Your laptop is already running an operating system.

Now imagine starting another one on the same machine. A completely separate
system with its own files, memory, and processes. You can run Linux inside
Windows, or Windows inside a Mac. It boots independently, runs programs,
and behaves as if it has full control over the hardware.

But it doesn’t.

Underneath, there is still only one physical machine. One CPU, one pool of
memory, one disk. Yet multiple such systems can run at the same time, each
isolated from the others and each functioning as if it were the only system
present.

This idea changed computing…

Instead of buying a new server each time, you could split one powerful
machine into many smaller ones. Each workload gets its own space. Nothing
interferes with anything else.

That shift is what made cloud computing possible…

When you run an app on Amazon Web Services, Microsoft Azure, or Google
Cloud Platform, it runs inside a virtual machine. The entire cloud
infrastructure is built on this foundation.

*But how does it actually work? How can one machine present itself as many?
How are resources shared without breaking isolation?*
------------------------------
Build strong data foundations for agentic AI at scale (Partner)
<https://substack.com/redirect/6375839c-b2b1-4c34-9404-f2e58eee6f76?j=eyJ1Ij...Z-GI>
<https://substack.com/redirect/6375839c-b2b1-4c34-9404-f2e58eee6f76?j=eyJ1Ij...Z-GI>

AI experiments are easy.

But building trusted AI systems that scale across an enterprise is hard.
That’s exactly what leaders from Mercedes-Benz, Yahoo, Regeneron, and AWS
discuss in this on-demand virtual panel on agentic AI.

*Here’s what you’ll learn inside:*

   -

   *Practical strategies from enterprise leaders:* See how teams move from
   isolated AI experiments to production-scale systems.
   -

   *Real-world guidance on governance and architecture:* Learn how industry
   experts approach analytics, unified governance, and continuous learning.
   -

   *Frameworks for scaling intelligent systems:* Discover how to build AI
   systems that drive business value.
   -

   *Pragmatic infrastructure decisions:* Learn how to select the right
   databases and data foundations for AI-based applications.

Watch the *AWS panel
<https://substack.com/redirect/6375839c-b2b1-4c34-9404-f2e58eee6f76?j=eyJ1Ij...Z-GI>*
and see how enterprise teams are building trusted AI systems with speed,
governance, and control.

Watch the AWS Virtual Panel
<https://substack.com/redirect/6375839c-b2b1-4c34-9404-f2e58eee6f76?j=eyJ1Ij...Z-GI>

(Thanks to AWS
<https://substack.com/redirect/6375839c-b2b1-4c34-9404-f2e58eee6f76?j=eyJ1Ij...Z-GI>
for partnering on this post.)
------------------------------

I want to introduce *Ayaan
<https://substack.com/redirect/e667f9ad-a51f-478c-a0ca-74caad3890cd?j=eyJ1Ij...Z-GI>*
as a guest author.
<https://substack.com/redirect/e667f9ad-a51f-478c-a0ca-74caad3890cd?j=eyJ1Ij...Z-GI>

He’s a self-taught DevOps engineer focused on Kubernetes & cloud
infrastructure. He builds in public and writes about cloud-native systems,
sharing daily technical tips and projects.

Check out his work and socials:

   -

   *Twitter
   <https://substack.com/redirect/e667f9ad-a51f-478c-a0ca-74caad3890cd?j=eyJ1Ij...Z-GI>*
   -

   *Github
   <https://substack.com/redirect/018c5ea2-4296-4d44-855e-0f59b5eab786?j=eyJ1Ij...Z-GI>*

------------------------------

*Here’s what’s inside this newsletter:*

   -

   *The difference between emulation and virtualization, and why one
   simulates hardware while the other shares it.*
   -

   What a virtual machine actually is, how it presents itself as a full
   computer, and why the guest operating system believes it owns the hardware.
   -

   *How the hypervisor works, the different types it comes in, and how it
   controls access to CPU, memory, storage, and networking.*
   -

   What happens under the hood when a virtual machine boots, from resource
   allocation and virtual hardware setup to kernel startup and runtime
   execution.
   -

   *How features like live migration, isolation, and resource controls make
   virtual machines practical for modern infrastructure.*
   -

   Where virtual machines fit today, why cloud platforms depend on them,
   and how they differ from containers.

Golden members get all posts like these!…
Upgrade to paid
<https://substack.com/redirect/2/eyJlIj...ItTY?&utm_medium=email&utm_source=subscribe-widget-preamble&utm_content=198581056>
------------------------------
Emulation vs Virtualization

Before we get into how virtual machines work, there is one distinction
worth getting clear early.
<https://substack.com/redirect/c9e3a68f-5e54-426f-becf-9dec4e757162?j=eyJ1Ij...Z-GI>

Emulation is when software pretends to be completely different hardware.
Retro gaming emulators work this way. Your laptop takes each instruction
from the old console and turns it into something your modern chip
understands.

It works, but it is slow…

Virtualization is different. Instead of pretending to be different
hardware, it creates a version of the same hardware your machine already
has. Most instructions run directly on the real processor with no
translation needed.

The result is near-native speed.

*Think of it this way:*

   -

   Emulation is like hiring a translator for every sentence in a
   conversation.
   -

   Virtualization is like realizing that both people already speak the same
   language.

This distinction matters because speed is what made virtualization
practical at scale. You cannot run thousands of emulated systems on a
single server. With virtualization, you can.

And that is exactly what cloud providers do…
------------------------------
What a Virtual Machine Actually Is

A virtual machine is a complete computer abstraction built on top of a
physical machine.
<https://substack.com/redirect/ff06b8cb-8ef0-485b-9a7f-fa7010a1f1ba?j=eyJ1Ij...Z-GI>

It’s presented with a CPU, memory, storage, and a network interface, just
like a physical system. The operating system inside it interacts with these
components as if they were real hardware. It has no awareness that it is
sharing the underlying machine with anything else.

From its perspective, it owns the system.

This behavior is made possible by a layered design.

At the bottom is the physical hardware, which provides the actual compute,
memory, and storage resources. Above it sits the hypervisor, which controls
how those resources are allocated and accessed. On top of that, each
virtual machine runs its own operating system in isolation.

The hypervisor is the layer that makes the illusion possible.

Because the operating system never interacts with the hardware directly.

Instead, every request for CPU time, memory access, disk I/O, or network
communication is handled through the hypervisor. It decides how resources
are shared and ensures that each virtual machine remains isolated from the
others.

This abstraction enables three key capabilities:

   -

   *Isolation:* Each virtual machine runs independently, so a failure in
   one doesn’t affect others.
   -

   *Portability:* Virtual machines can move between physical systems with
   minimal changes.
   -

   *Efficient resource use:* Multiple workloads share the same hardware
   instead of leaving it idle.

These properties are what made modern infrastructure possible.

Next, let’s look at the hypervisor: what it is, the different types, and
how it powers virtual machines…
------------------------------
Hypervisor: Brain Behind the Illusion

The hypervisor is the software layer that enables virtualization.

It sits between the physical hardware and the virtual machines running
above it. Every request a virtual machine makes, whether for CPU time,
memory, or storage, goes through the hypervisor. It’s responsible for
mapping those requests to the underlying hardware resources.

This layer controls all access to the hardware.
Type 1 vs Type 2

Not all hypervisors follow the same structure. The main difference comes
from where they run.
<https://substack.com/redirect/d59c51d4-eaa5-457c-841f-4183fbbb63ea?j=eyJ1Ij...Z-GI>

   -

   *Type 1 (bare-metal):* It runs on the physical hardware, with no host
   operating system in between. Examples include VMware ESXi, Microsoft
   Hyper-V, and Kernel-based Virtual Machine (*KVM*). Cloud environments
   and data centers use these because they provide direct hardware access and
   minimal overhead.
   -

   *Type 2 (hosted):* Runs on top of an existing operating system, like a
   regular application. Tools such as VirtualBox and VMware Workstation fall
   into this category. They are easier to use but introduce additional
   overhead.

The difference lies in how the hypervisor accesses hardware and the number
of extra layers it adds…
Full Virtualization vs Paravirtualization

There are also different ways a hypervisor presents hardware to a virtual
machine.
<https://substack.com/redirect/93b2f0ef-de9b-46e3-9daa-e84133c62304?j=eyJ1Ij...Z-GI>

In *full virtualization*, guest operating system runs without modification
and behaves as if it were on real hardware. The hypervisor handles all the
work required to maintain that abstraction.

In *paravirtualization*, the guest operating system is aware that it is
running in a virtual environment. Instead of relying only on emulated
hardware, it asks the hypervisor to handle some tasks, which reduces
overhead.

The trade-off is between compatibility and performance.

Full virtualization runs any unmodified operating system.
Paravirtualization is faster because the guest works with the hypervisor.
Hardware-Assisted Virtualization

Early hypervisors relied on software, making virtualization complex and
inefficient. This changed when processor manufacturers introduced hardware
support such as Intel VT-x (Intel Virtualization Technology for x86) and
AMD-V (AMD Virtualization).

With these features, CPU itself can differentiate between virtual machines
and the hypervisor. Sensitive operations pass control to the hypervisor
when needed. It manages access and keeps everything secure.

This made virtualization practical at scale.
Nested Virtualization

It is also possible to run a virtual machine inside another virtual
machine, a setup known as *nested virtualization*. You use this for
testing, development, and running tools like Kubernetes locally in virtual
machines (*VMs*).

But each additional layer introduces overhead and increases complexity. As
you add more virtualization layers, performance drops. This makes the
approach unsuitable for most production workloads.

It is useful in controlled scenarios, but not a general-purpose design.

Now you know what the hypervisor does. The next question is how it shares
the CPU across multiple virtual machines. The answer is CPU virtualization.

Let’s look at how it works…
------------------------------
CPU Virtualization

The CPU is the brain of any computer.

Every instruction, process, and calculation goes through the CPU. So when
you create a virtual machine, the question becomes: *how do different
operating systems share one CPU without getting in each other’s way?*

That is what CPU virtualization solves…
The Privilege Model

Operating systems are designed to be in charge.

When your OS needs to access hardware, manage memory, or control processes,
it does so with full authority. The processor has a concept called *privilege
levels* to enforce this.

Think of it like levels of trust, where the most trusted software gets the
most access.
<https://substack.com/redirect/738da4fc-c00b-41a0-94df-c835ccc83b24?j=eyJ1Ij...Z-GI>

In a normal system, the operating system sits at the highest privilege
level.

But in a virtual environment, the hypervisor needs to sit above the
operating system. It needs to intercept certain instructions and control
what the guest OS can and cannot do. So the guest OS gets moved down to a
lower privilege level, even though it still believes it is fully in charge.

The guest OS never knows the hypervisor demoted it.

This is the fundamental trick behind CPU virtualization. The guest OS
continues to behave as if it owns the processor. But when it tries to do
something that needs high-level access, the processor steps in and hands
control to the hypervisor.

Everything continues smoothly, just one level deeper…
vCPU and Scheduling

When you create a virtual machine, you assign it CPUs, but these are not
physical cores. They’re virtual CPUs (*vCPUs*) that represent processing
capacity managed by the hypervisor.

The hypervisor schedules vCPUs onto physical cores in the same way an
operating system schedules processes.

At any given moment, a physical core can execute only one thread of work.
The hypervisor switches between virtual machines, giving each one a slice
of CPU time. This switching happens fast enough that each virtual machine
appears to have continuous access to the processor.

*Modern CPUs may expose multiple logical threads per core through
technologies like Intel Hyper-Threading or AMD SMT (Simultaneous
Multithreading), but CPU time is still shared and scheduled across
workloads.*

In reality, all virtual machines share it.

This is called *time-slicing*. It’s the same idea your operating system
uses to run multiple applications at once.

Hypervisors can also assign more vCPUs than there are physical cores, a
practice known as *CPU overcommit*. For example, a machine with 16 cores
might run virtual machines totaling 32 or more vCPUs.

This works as long as not all virtual machines are busy at the same time.

When demand rises across multiple VMs, they compete for CPU time. This
causes contention and reduces performance.

Overcommit is a useful tool, but it depends on workload behavior.
VM-exit and VM-entry

Let’s go one level deeper into what actually happens when a guest OS tries
to do something it is not allowed to do on its own.
<https://substack.com/redirect/9a26b39f-2661-498a-8b8f-f69d679d63b4?j=eyJ1Ij...Z-GI>

When a virtual machine tries to run a privileged instruction, the processor
stops it. Control is immediately transferred to the hypervisor. This event
is called a *VM-exit*.

The hypervisor then figures out what the guest was trying to do and handles
it safely.

Once the hypervisor has dealt with the request, it hands control back to
the virtual machine exactly where it left off. This is called a *VM-entry*.
From the guest OS’s perspective, nothing unusual happened. The CPU executed
the instruction and continued running.

The entire handoff takes microseconds.

This mechanism is what makes isolation possible. The guest OS cannot access
hardware it shouldn’t. Every sensitive operation triggers a VM-exit, which
the hypervisor controls. But it also introduces overhead. Every VM-exit and
VM-entry takes time, and if they happen too frequently, performance suffers.

Keeping VM exits to a minimum is one of the core challenges of CPU
virtualization.
NUMA Awareness and CPU Topology

This matters more in large systems and performance-sensitive workloads, but
it’s still worth understanding at a high level…

Modern servers often have more than one physical processor. Each processor
has its own local memory. Accessing memory that belongs to a different
processor is slower than accessing your own. This architecture is called
Non-Uniform Memory Access (*NUMA*).

The name describes the problem exactly. Not all memory accesses are equal.

In a virtual environment, this creates a subtle issue. A virtual machine
might run on cores from one processor but end up accessing memory that
belongs to another. The guest OS does not know this is happening. But the
performance hit is real.

The solution is NUMA-aware scheduling.

The hypervisor tries to keep a virtual machine’s vCPUs and its memory on
the same NUMA node. When it succeeds, the VM runs faster. When workloads
grow and resources get spread across nodes, the performance gap becomes
noticeable.

For most small deployments, this never comes up. For high-performance
databases or real-time systems running on large servers, it matters a lot.

Putting it together, CPU virtualization is not a single mechanism but a
combination of coordinated techniques.

The privilege model restricts direct hardware access. vCPU scheduling
creates the illusion of dedicated processing power. VM-exits and VM-entries
enforce safe boundaries for sensitive operations. NUMA awareness ensures
that physical hardware layout does not introduce hidden performance
penalties.

Each part addresses a specific challenge.

Together, they allow multiple virtual machines to share a single physical
CPU while operating independently and efficiently.

Next, let’s look at how memory is virtualized and how virtual machines
manage their view of system memory…
------------------------------

*Reminder: this is a teaser of the subscriber-only newsletter, exclusive to
my golden members.*

When you upgrade, you’ll get:

   -

   *Architecture breakdown of core DevOps systems.*
   -

   Deep dives into Docker, Kubernetes, Terraform, and so on.
   -

   *Practical insights into how real-world systems achieve scalability,
   automation, and reliability.*

Unlock Full Access
<https://substack.com/redirect/f3966046-8eb3-453e-8c7f-033325d9459a?j=eyJ1Ij...Z-GI>

Let’s keep going!
------------------------------
Memory Virtualization...

# HumanLayer // We're live — come build with us

**Source:** email from dex at humanlayer re: HumanLayer launch, 2026-06-18
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19edfd1054c17600
**Ingested:** 2026-06-23T00:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf on 2026-06-19

HumanLayer has opened up to everyone. An Agentic IDE, collaboration platform, and building blocks for software factories — where engineering teams design complex systems with AI agents before generating code.

## Key Points

- **Core idea:** Don't outsource the thinking. Design reviews have enormous leverage — HumanLayer brings that into one place alongside AI agents.
- **Speed:** Move 2-3x faster across the entire SDLC, not just coding.
- **BYO subscription:** Plug in your Claude, Codex, or other API keys — no separate AI bill.
- **RPI framework:** Built on the open-source Research, Plan, Implement framework now inside Fortune 500s (Block, Uber).
- **Pricing:** Starter plan free for teams of < 3 people at humanlayer.com

## Context

Founded by Dex (dexter@hey.humanlayer.dev) and Kyle. Sayf was on their waitlist. Relevant to Maloha Coast's work on agentic workflows and AI-assisted development.

## Links

- X launch post: https://x.com/dexhorthy/status/2067286892786454855
- LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/dexterihorthy_at-humanlayer-were-on-a-mission-to-solve-ugcPost-7473052840899407872-Zb5U

pages.title: Js Mastery Three Claudes 2026 06 11
pages.body:
# Three Features. Three Claudes. One Afternoon.

I ran three agents in parallel...Then merged everything.

February 5th is in my diary. I'm looking at three terminal windows, all
running Claude Code simultaneously, all working on the same project.

One is building a dark/light mode toggle. One is writing API endpoint
tests. One is adding code quality tooling. None of them knows the others
exist.

I watched all three work at the same time and remember thinking, "This is
genuinely new." This isn't a faster way to do what I was already doing.
This is a different thing entirely.

Here's the setup. Git has a feature called worktrees, you to check out
multiple branches of the same project into separate directories and work on
them independently. Each directory gets its own Claude session, its own
context window, its own isolated set of files to modify.

What this solves is the thing that makes parallel AI development feel
risky: two agents writing to the same file at the same time. With
worktrees, that can't happen.

They're operating in completely separate environments.

When all three were done, I committed each branch with a descriptive
message. Claude wrote those too, which matters more than it sounds when
you're about to merge three sets of changes, and then asked a fourth Claude
session to pull everything together and resolve any conflicts.

It found one. A pyproject.toml that two branches had both modified.
Resolved it in about thirty seconds.

The whole thing, three features built simultaneously, merged, tested, took
an afternoon. Features that would have been sequential, each requiring
their own setup, their own context, their own careful hand-holding through
implementation.

But here's what actually stuck with me. It wasn't the speed. It was that I
spent that afternoon thinking, about architecture, about what each feature
needed to know, about how the pieces would fit together after the merge.
Not implementing. Directing.

That's the shift I keep coming back to.

Have you ever tried running more than one AI session in parallel on the
same project? Did it work, or did it create more problems than it solved?

Talk soon,
Adrian
JSM

P.S. Next email, I want to talk about something I noticed when I spoke to a
business owner with 30 developers on his team.

Almost none of them were using AI seriously. What he said next is the
reason this course needs an enterprise angle, and why the timing right now
is unlike anything we've seen before.
You are receiving this email because you opted in to receive email from the
list AI course waitlist from JS Mastery

pages.body:
# The Maloha Coast Navigator

*Your weekly guide to AI strategy for SMBs*

---

## This Week: Why Most AI Pilots Fail

80% of AI proofs-of-concept never reach production. Here's the pattern:

1. **Scope creep** — POCs that try to boil the ocean
2. **Data rot** — Models trained on stale data that's 6 months old
3. **No owner** — AI sits in IT without a business sponsor

### The Fix

Start with a **single workflow**, not a platform. Pick the one manual process your team hates most. Automate that. Measure the time saved. Then expand.

---

## Client Spotlight

A 12-person accounting firm deployed an email triage agent. Result: 14 hours/week saved. ROI in week one.

---

## What We're Reading

- [[Claude Code 10x Skill]] — The skill pattern that changed everything
- [[Second Brain Architecture]] — How we structure knowledge for SMBs

---

## Upcoming

Next week: Pricing models that work for SMB AI consulting.

*— Sayf, Maloha Coast*

pages.title: Your coding agent + 300 models
pages.body:
Cursor, Cline, Continue, Claude Code. All work with one OpenRouter key.  

OpenRouter
OpenRouter ( https://openrouter.ai/?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A6+Coding+Agents&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )

	

Hi there,

Coding agents are the fastest-growing use case on OpenRouter. One API key gives your agent access to every model, with automatic provider fallback if one goes down. You stop maintaining four provider accounts and one consolidated usage view replaces four separate dashboards.

See integrations ( https://openrouter.ai/docs/frameworks?utm_source=email&utm_medium=lifecycle&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=b9_agents&utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A6+Coding+Agents&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )

	

The major agents already speak OpenRouter.

Cursor. Drop your OpenRouter key into Settings and pick any model from the dropdown. Switch between Claude, GPT, Gemini, and DeepSeek per task without leaving the editor.
Cline. Autonomous coding with auto-routing. The agent picks the right model per step, so planning runs on a thinking model and execution runs on something faster and cheaper.
Continue. Open-source, configurable. Point the base URL at OpenRouter and every model is available from the model picker.
Claude Code. Use OpenRouter as the backend to track team usage, set per-key rate limits, and control spend across models without touching Anthropic billing directly.

Why the multi-model swap matters for agents. Most coding agents are cheaper and faster when you route structured steps like file reads and diff writes to a small model, and reserve a frontier model for the part that actually writes code. The cost gap between a $0.15/M token small model and a $15/M token frontier model stacks up fast when the agent makes 50 calls per task.

Usage visibility. The activity dashboard shows every request with its model, cost, latency, and token counts. If an agent runs away, you see it inside minutes, not at the end of a billing cycle. Per-key rate limits stop the bleeding without disabling the whole integration.

openrouter.ai ( https://openrouter.ai?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A6+Coding+Agents&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )  ·  Discord ( https://discord.com/invite/8TpuzNXQYr?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A6+Coding+Agents&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )  ·  X ( https://x.com/openrouter?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A6+Coding+Agents&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )  ·  Docs ( https://openrouter.ai/docs?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A6+Coding+Agents&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )

Manage preferences ( https://track.customer.io/manage_subscription_preferences/dgSE_QoBALa-kAS1vpAEAZ7Lln4qwIcpkzT5dDLICQ== )

pages.title: Three features worth trying next
pages.body:
# Three features worth trying next

**Source:** Email from OpenRouter Team <welcome@openrouter.ai>
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19edb0e0abd83fb0
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf on 2026-06-20

**Author:** OpenRouter Team
**Type:** OpenRouter newsletter

---

Streaming, structured outputs, and provider preferences. Three parameters that change what OpenRouter can do for you.  

OpenRouter
OpenRouter ( https://openrouter.ai/?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )

 

Hi there,

Three features worth trying once your first requests are landing. Each one is a single parameter change. Each one earns its place in production code and none of them require a new integration or a different endpoint.

See the feature docs ( https://openrouter.ai/docs/quickstart?utm_source=email&utm_medium=lifecycle&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=a13_close&utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )

 

Streaming ( https://openrouter.ai/docs/api-reference/streaming?utm_source=email&utm_medium=lifecycle&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=a13_close&utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io ). Set stream=True and tokens arrive as they generate. Same endpoint, same auth, one parameter. Perceived latency drops from seconds to the first token, which is the difference between a chat UI that feels alive and one that feels broken. Agents that stream partial results to a frontend get a noticeable UX lift for zero code cost.

Structured outputs ( https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/structured-outputs?utm_source=email&utm_medium=lifecycle&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=a13_close&utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io ). Pass a JSON schema via response_format and OpenRouter enforces it at the API level. No regex parsing, no retry loops when the model returns malformed JSON, no "please respond in valid JSON" prompt engineering. Works on every model that supports the format, and the router respects it when you use openrouter/auto.

Provider preferences ( https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/provider-routing?utm_source=email&utm_medium=lifecycle&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=a13_close&utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io ). Pin specific providers, exclude others, set latency floors and throughput minimums. When a model has multiple providers behind it, you decide which backend serves each request. Set {"sort": "price"} to always pick the cheapest, or an allowlist to lock routing to providers you trust.

 

ALL THREE AT ONCE

response = client.chat.completions.create
(   model="openrouter/auto",
  stream=True,
  response_format={"type": "json_schema", "json_schema": schema},
  extra_body={"provider": {"sort": "price"}},
  messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "..."}], )

 

Pages worth bookmarking: openrouter.ai/models ( https://openrouter.ai/models?utm_source=email&utm_medium=lifecycle&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=a13_close&utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io ) for browsing and pricing, openrouter.ai/rankings ( https://openrouter.ai/rankings?utm_source=email&utm_medium=lifecycle&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=a13_close&utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io ) for real usage volume data, openrouter.ai/activity ( https://openrouter.ai/activity?utm_source=email&utm_medium=lifecycle&utm_campaign=onboarding_v2&utm_content=a13_close&utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io ) for your usage and costs, and discord.gg/openrouter 
( https://discord.gg/openrouter?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io ) if you get stuck.

openrouter.ai ( https://openrouter.ai?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )  ·  Discord ( https://discord.com/invite/8TpuzNXQYr?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )  ·  X ( https://x.com/openrouter?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )  ·  Docs ( https://openrouter.ai/docs?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A9+Feature+Trio&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )

  |

pages.title: Msg 19ee03492356403f
pages.body:
# How provider data policies work on [[OpenRouter]]

**Source:** OpenRouter Team (newsletter)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee03492356403f
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20
**Provenance:** Email to info@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-19

Every provider behind [[OpenRouter]] has its own retention and training policy. [[OpenRouter]] reflects those policies as structured data on each endpoint, publishes the full table in the docs, and gives you controls to filter routing on them.

[Provider policy table](https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/privacy-and-logging)

## What you control

1. **Block routing to providers that train.** Account-wide toggle for whether routing may use providers that train on prompts, with separate switches for paid and free models.
2. **Require a specific data policy per request.** Override account default on any single call via [data policy filtering](https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/provider-routing#requiring-providers-to-comply-with-data-policies).
3. **Pick only zero-retention providers.** Many providers — including Amazon Bedrock, Azure, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, and Together — are zero retention. Filter to them the same way as training policy.

## If you need stricter guarantees

[[OpenRouter]] supports EU in-region routing for enterprise accounts. Prompts and completions processed inside the EU via `https://eu.openrouter.ai`. [Contact enterprise team](https://openrouter.ai/enterprise/form) to enable it.

One API key, the same privacy controls across 300+ models.

<https://substack..dy_jdOxm0woT2JKgwC1fhSfla5h_BNRd9gpaZUyJMTU?&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email>

READ IN APP
<https://open.substack.com/pub/systemdesignone/p/what-is-ai-infrastructure?utm_source=email&redirect=app-store&utm_campaign=email-read-in-app>

Get my system design playbook for FREE on newsletter signup:
Upgrade to paid
<https://substack..TPBY7LfeDQSNxd8lMq_bZ2a3zPdKO8SDT2VH0PpWhMk?&utm_medium=email&utm_source=subscribe-widget-preamble&utm_content=200923722>

 -

 *Share this post
 <https://substack.com/redirect/9cf1ec6d-5730-4731-84de-751a79999295?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>
 & I’ll send you some rewards for the referrals.*

------------------------------

When people talk about Artificial Intelligence (*AI*), they often refer to
models such as Generative Pre-trained Transformers (*GPTs*), Large Language
Models (*LLMs*), and neural networks.

It sounds like magic, software that *thinks*…

But here’s the uncomfortable truth:

The biggest breakthroughs in AI are not coming from better models; they’re
coming from better infrastructure.

If AI were only about the models, there would be no need for huge,
warehouse-sized data centers packed with specialized hardware and powered
by massive amounts of electricity.

So what’s actually going on inside those buildings?

To make sense of everything that follows, we need a simple mental model.

Let’s find out one layer at a time…

Onward.
------------------------------
*Review the actual change, not the file list (Partner)
<https://substack.com/redirect/536b6e05-fe44-488b-9e69-cf79bfdc2d2b?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>*
<https://substack.com/redirect/536b6e05-fe44-488b-9e69-cf79bfdc2d2b?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

*AI writes more code than ever. Reviewing it shouldn’t mean scrolling forty
files in alphabetical order.
<https://substack.com/redirect/536b6e05-fe44-488b-9e69-cf79bfdc2d2b?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>*

CodeRabbit Review
<https://substack.com/redirect/536b6e05-fe44-488b-9e69-cf79bfdc2d2b?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>
reorganizes any pull request from a flat file list into a structured,
layer-by-layer walkthrough - the logical reading order of the change, not
the order your platform happens to sort it. Every range gets its own
plain-language summary, with sequence diagrams, state machines, and ERDs
generated inline wherever a visual earns its place.

*Cohorts* group related files and chunks so you review one idea at a time.
*Layers* order them so foundational changes - data shapes, contracts - come
before the code that depends on them. *Code Peek* lets you click any
variable, function, class, or type to see its definition and usages without
leaving the tab, while *Semantic Diff* view cuts past formatting noise to
show what actually changed.

Open it straight from the Review Change Stack button in the PR Walkthrough.
Navigate cohorts and layers from the keyboard, comment against exact line
ranges, and submit native reviews, comments, and approvals post back to
GitHub or GitLab right where your team expects them.

*In the early access, available for free to everyone.*

From the team that pioneered AI code reviews. 2M reviews every week. 6M
repos. 15K customers.The review interface built for the way modern PRs are
actually written.

>>> *Review your next PR with CodeRabbit Review Today
<https://substack.com/redirect/536b6e05-fe44-488b-9e69-cf79bfdc2d2b?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>*

(Thanks to CodeRabbit
<https://substack.com/redirect/536b6e05-fe44-488b-9e69-cf79bfdc2d2b?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>
for partnering on this post.)
------------------------------

I’d like to introduce *Ashish Prajapati
<https://substack.com/redirect/681e4821-11cc-4a93-9af5-80bc26a7e017?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>*
.
<https://substack.com/redirect/681e4821-11cc-4a93-9af5-80bc26a7e017?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

He is an AI infrastructure specialist and technology educator who helps
engineers understand the technologies powering modern AI.

With expertise spanning data centers, cloud computing, GPU infrastructure,
Generative AI, and Agentic AI, he focuses on turning complex concepts into
practical, real-world knowledge.

His training programs have reached over 39,000 learners worldwide, and
through his free mentoring initiative, Become a Solutions Architect (BeSA)
<https://substack.com/redirect/30f898b6-b928-47a1-9d3b-b30d12b24f91?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>,
he has helped over 23,000 professionals accelerate their careers.

As AI adoption continues to grow, understanding the infrastructure behind
AI systems is becoming a valuable skill for architects, engineers, and IT
professionals.

For a limited time, this newsletter's readers can use code *SYSTEM-DESIGN
to get 25% off Ashish’s live NCA-AIIO bootcamp
<https://substack.com/redirect/681e4821-11cc-4a93-9af5-80bc26a7e017?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>*
.

This intensive one-day workshop covers the fundamentals of NVIDIA AI
infrastructure, GPU networking, accelerated computing, and AI operations,
and prepares you for the NVIDIA-Certified Associate: AI Infrastructure and
Operations (NCA-AIIO) certification.

*Learn, Prepare, and Pass NCA-AIIO in a Day
<https://substack.com/redirect/681e4821-11cc-4a93-9af5-80bc26a7e017?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>*.

------------------------------

*Here’s what you’ll find inside this newsletter:*

 -

 *The hidden architecture behind AI.* Why models are only the visible tip
 of a much larger infrastructure stack.
 -

 *The physics that limit AI growth.* How power, cooling, and space shape
 every AI data center.
 -

 *Why GPUs became the backbone of AI.* The architectural advantages that
 made modern AI training possible.
 -

 *The real bottlenecks in modern AI.* Why memory, networking, and
 coordination determine performance at scale.
 -

 *How thousands of GPUs work together as one system. *The software,
 networking, and orchestration layers that enable large-scale AI.

Golden members get all posts like these!…
Upgrade to paid
<https://substack..TPBY7LfeDQSNxd8lMq_bZ2a3zPdKO8SDT2VH0PpWhMk?&utm_medium=email&utm_source=subscribe-widget-preamble&utm_content=200923722>
------------------------------
Think of an AI Data Center as a Factory

Before we go deep, build this mental model; it will explain almost
everything that follows:

 -

 AI Data Center = Factory
 -

 Data = Raw material
 -

 Graphics Processing Units (*GPUs*) = Machines on the factory floor
 -

 Network = Internal transport system
 -

 Storage = Warehouse holding raw materials
 -

 Software = Assembly line logic
 -

 Output = Trained intelligence

<https://substack.com/redirect/5e45622c-2bb8-41c9-8918-1ecab585affc?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

Now imagine a car factory.

It doesn’t just need skilled workers; instead, it needs conveyors,
warehouses, and power. Remove any of those, and production stops.

An AI data center works the same way…

AI infrastructure processes vast amounts of data using mathematical
operations, similar to how a factory transforms raw materials into finished
goods.

Remove any one ingredient, and the entire system stalls…

Once you see AI as a factory, the next question becomes obvious: *what
limits how fast this factory can run?*

Share
<https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1511845&post_id=200923722&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&action=share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=2k46ui&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjoxNTQ3MjAxNzAsInBvc3RfaWQiOjIwMDkyMzcyMiwiaWF0IjoxNzgxODYwNDAzLCJleHAiOjE3ODQ0NTI0MDMsImlzcyI6InB1Yi0xNTExODQ1Iiwic3ViIjoicG9zdC1yZWFjdGlvbiJ9.YdmtNfofj9kNOvfhe0cEAH64YdVJ2yKsV0IJWCJV8Dw>
------------------------------
First Problem: Physics

*Before AI is a software problem, it’s a physics problem…*
<https://substack.com/redirect/1e36bdff-cf91-4ab9-b759-4f5cd80e9915?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

Here are three hard physical constraints that shape every design decision
in an AI data center:
1. Power

Modern AI workloads rely on high-density GPUs that consume enormous amounts
of electricity.

A single server rack of GPUs can draw 80-120 kilowatts. i.e., roughly
equivalent to 40 homes running at full load simultaneously. Yet you can’t
just add more GPUs forever.

You’re limited by how much electricity your facility can physically receive
and distribute.
2. Cooling

More power means more heat, and heat is the enemy…

Here’s what happens if you don’t remove it fast enough:

 -

 Hardware fails prematurely,
 -

 Performance throttles back to avoid damage,
 -

 In extreme cases, systems shut down entirely.

To manage the intense heat generated by AI workloads, modern data centers
are increasingly adopting *liquid cooling*, which is far more effective
than conventional air cooling.

Coolant is circulated through cold plates attached directly to GPUs and
CPUs, absorbing heat much more efficiently than air. The warmed liquid is
then pumped to heat exchangers, where the heat is removed before the
coolant is recirculated.

This approach enables higher rack densities, lower cooling power
consumption, and more consistent performance for large-scale AI training
and inference¹ workloads.
<https://substack.com/redirect/e06be2b6-153f-4c1b-961f-e2b90cb22b2e?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>
3. Space

Even if you had unlimited chips and unlimited power, you still need racks
to mount them and floor space to put those racks.

Scaling AI is not just software--it’s real estate, energy infrastructure,
and thermodynamics combined.
Takeaway

A data center is, at its core, a power plant combined with a massive,
precisely controlled air-conditioning system. Every engineering decision
flows from managing this reality.

After dealing with the physical challenges, the next question is: *how we
perform the computation?*
------------------------------
Compute: Why GPUs Took Over

To understand why GPUs dominate AI, picture a very simple task…

Imagine you have 1,000 fence posts to paint:

 -

 Option A: One expert painter, working post by post. Slow, but the
 painter can handle any unexpected situation--a broken post, a curved
 section.
 -

 Option B: 1,000 workers, each painting exactly one post simultaneously.
 Extremely fast, but each worker knows only one simple task.

That’s the difference between a CPU and a GPU.
CPU (Central Processing Unit)

 -

 A few very powerful cores
 -

 Handles complex, varied, sequential tasks
 -

 Acts as the “manager” making decisions and coordinating work

GPU (Graphics Processing Unit)

 -

 Thousands of smaller, simpler cores
 -

 Handles same simple operation repeated millions of times, all at once
 -

 Acts as the “factory workers” doing mass production

AI training repeatedly performs matrix multiplications billions of times,
making minor adjustments after each step. GPUs are specifically designed
for this type of work.
DPU (Data Processing Unit)

 -

 As AI systems grow, a third processor becomes important.
 -

 DPU handles networking, storage I/O, and security operations--tasks that
 would otherwise steal cycles from the GPU and CPU.

*Here’s the system view:*

 -

 CPU = manager (decision-making)
 -

 GPU = factory workers (mass production)
 -

 DPU = logistics and operations (keeping materials moving and systems
 secure)

<https://substack.com/redirect/b68e2ff4-8896-4f5d-bfe6-b818215dea42?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

But even with powerful GPUs, computation alone isn’t the limiting factor;
the real challenge is keeping them fed with data…

Share
<https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1511845&post_id=200923722&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&action=share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=2k46ui&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjoxNTQ3MjAxNzAsInBvc3RfaWQiOjIwMDkyMzcyMiwiaWF0IjoxNzgxODYwNDAzLCJleHAiOjE3ODQ0NTI0MDMsImlzcyI6InB1Yi0xNTExODQ1Iiwic3ViIjoicG9zdC1yZWFjdGlvbiJ9.YdmtNfofj9kNOvfhe0cEAH64YdVJ2yKsV0IJWCJV8Dw>
------------------------------
Hidden Bottleneck: Memory and Storage

Here’s something that surprises most people:

GPUs don’t fail because they’re slow. They fail because they’re
hungry—constantly starved for data.

<https://substack.com/redirect/e20c200f-be89-420c-bba9-c3fd72bb261e?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>
Memory Hierarchy

Data doesn’t live in one place. It moves through a layered system, trading
speed for capacity:

 -

 GPU Memory (HBM²): Blazingly fast but tiny; usually tens of gigabytes.
 This is the GPU’s working memory.
 -

 CPU RAM: Larger (hundreds of gigabytes), but slower than HBM.
 -

 SSD/NVMe³ Storage: Enormous capacity at a lower cost, but still slower.
 -

 Network/Object Storage: Nearly unlimited, but the slowest link in the
 chain.

Every time the GPU needs data that isn’t already in its fast local memory,
it must wait... And a GPU sitting idle costs just as much money as one
doing useful work.

*Takeaway*

AI performance is not determined solely by how fast the GPU computes.
Instead, by how fast data can be fed into the GPU.

A faster chip with a slow data pipeline is like a faster car stuck in
traffic.
Tiered storage in practice

In real AI data centers, storage is organized into tiers that roughly map
to hot, warm, and cold data:

 -

 *Hot*: NVMe SSDs holding active training datasets and model checkpoints.
 -

 *Warm*: Parallel file systems⁴, such as Lustre, General Parallel File
 System (*GPFS*), provide high-speed shared access across many GPUs.
 -

 *Cold*: Object storage, such as Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon
 S3), for storing raw datasets and older checkpoints.

As models grow larger, a single GPU is no longer enough…

Techniques such as model sharding⁵ and distributed training⁶ distribute the
model across multiple GPUs and memory layers.

And once data starts moving across machines, a new bottleneck appears: *how
those machines communicate with each other.*

*Reminder: this is a teaser of the subscriber-only newsletter series,
exclusive to my golden members.*

When you upgrade, you’ll get:

 -

 *High-level architecture of real-world systems.*
 -

 Deep dive into how popular real-world systems actually work.
 -

 *How real-world systems handle scale, reliability, and performance.*

Unlock Full Access
<https://substack.com/redirect/bc198849-e83f-4dfb-9489-9752289ffd6f?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>
------------------------------
Networking: Bottleneck Nobody Talks About

When training a large AI model, you typically use thousands of GPUs working
together rather than a single GPU…

And those thousands of GPUs must constantly communicate with each other,
sharing what they’ve learned at every step…
<https://substack.com/redirect/416747cc-b41c-43c4-b795-c8e297cddfcd?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

Every second a GPU waits for data or synchronization is a second of lost
productivity and wasted investment.
Cluster topology and fabrics

Modern AI clusters typically use leaf-spine network architectures⁷: *a
design built for high throughput and low latency at scale*.

Every GPU server can reach every other GPU server in *at most ‘two’ hops*.

Within a node, GPUs talk over NVLink (intra-node fabric). Between nodes,
traffic flows over InfiniBand or RDMA over Converged Ethernet (*RoCE*), the
inter-node fabric⁸.

There’s also a clear speed hierarchy:

 -

 NVLink⁹: fastest path, a direct, private highway connecting GPUs within
 the same server.
 -

 InfiniBand/RoCE¹⁰: very high-speed interconnect between separate
 servers. Imagine it as the “bullet train” of the data center.
 -

 Ethernet: general-purpose, flexible, and ubiquitous, but not designed
 for AI’s most extreme bandwidth and latency demands.

Amdahl’s Law: Why More GPUs Aren’t Always Better

There’s a fundamental principle in distributed computing called *Amdahl’s
Law*.

In plain English: if parts of your system must wait for other parts, adding
more workers eventually stops helping.

In AI workloads, adding more GPUs increases computing power but also
increases communication overhead. As GPUs spend more time sharing and
synchronizing data, the bottleneck gradually shifts from computation to
communication.
Smarter data movement

Modern AI systems address this with several techniques:

 -

 Remote Direct Memory Access (*RDMA*): Provides low-latency,
 high-throughput communication by enabling direct memory-to-memory data
 transfers between servers.
 -

 RDMA kernel¹¹ bypass: Lets the network adapter access application memory
 directly from user space¹², eliminating unnecessary operating system
 overhead.
 -

 GPUDirect: GPUs can read from and write to storage or network cards
 directly, bypassing the CPU entirely.
 -

 Zero-copy pipelines: Data takes the shortest path from source to GPU,
 with no redundant copying along the way.

The goal of AI networking is to make thousands of GPUs behave like one
giant, unified processor, as if the data never had to travel at all.

*Once communication is in place, the next challenge is dividing the work
efficiently across all those GPUs…...*
