<https://substack.com/@systemdesignone> <https://substack.com/@paulhoekstra>

<https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1511845&post_id=200995493&utm_source=substack&isFreemail=true&submitLike=true&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjoxNTQ3MjAxNzAsInBvc3RfaWQiOjIwMDk5NTQ5MywicmVhY3Rpb24iOiLinaQiLCJpYXQiOjE3ODE2ODkxMTYsImV4cCI6MTc4NDI4MTExNiwiaXNzIjoicHViLTE1MTE4NDUiLCJzdWIiOiJyZWFjdGlvbiJ9.P-ZPnH12XKKCNIbPo2vVXfIXqCv19kdU37j4G6Xmfk4&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=email-reaction&r=2k46ui>
<https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1511845&post_id=200995493&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&isFreemail=true&comments=true&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjoxNTQ3MjAxNzAsInBvc3RfaWQiOjIwMDk5NTQ5MywiaWF0IjoxNzgxNjg5MTE2LCJleHAiOjE3ODQyODExMTYsImlzcyI6InB1Yi0xNTExODQ1Iiwic3ViIjoicG9zdC1yZWFjdGlvbiJ9.CZcfri6GGTQ8GfxA0BTPLT6si-x5-T-85JKeMZi7ABE&r=2k46ui&utm_campaign=email-half-magic-comments&action=post-comment&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email>
<https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1511845&post_id=200995493&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&action=share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=2k46ui&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjoxNTQ3MjAxNzAsInBvc3RfaWQiOjIwMDk5NTQ5MywiaWF0IjoxNzgxNjg5MTE2LCJleHAiOjE3ODQyODExMTYsImlzcyI6InB1Yi0xNTExODQ1Iiwic3ViIjoicG9zdC1yZWFjdGlvbiJ9.CZcfri6GGTQ8GfxA0BTPLT6si-x5-T-85JKeMZi7ABE>
<https://substack..Exbes_G42oZxe6Prz11CmLROxLDogb648K-OpT9kTGQ?&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email>

READ IN APP
<https://open.substack.com/pub/systemdesignone/p/agentic-engineering?utm_source=email&redirect=app-store&utm_campaign=email-read-in-app>

Get my system design playbook for FREE on newsletter signup:
Upgrade to paid
<https://substack..8ybv1NXMQbPlUHWM1x-Mi90hxyNfKpYFTSgYqxHDqYs?&utm_medium=email&utm_source=subscribe-widget-preamble&utm_content=200995493>

 -

 *Share this post
 <https://substack.com/redirect/231855c1-d697-4e2b-ab27-502cc1346b36?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>
 & I'll send you some rewards for the referrals.*

------------------------------

How do you keep up with agentic engineering when a new tool ships every
week?

You do not…

You learn the ideas behind the tools, and let the tools come and go.

The pace will not slow down. New models, new frameworks, a new *“this
changes everything*” launch every few days. Chase all of it, and you spend
more time switching tools than using them.

But underneath the noise, it is the same few ideas every time.

One calls it a skill, the next calls it a rule; it's the same job
underneath.

Learn the idea, and it stops mattering which tool you pick.

By the end of this newsletter, you can read any agent post or news drop and
see which of these ideas it actually covers, instead of feeling behind
every time something new ships.

Onward.
------------------------------
”How to adopt externalized authorization: step-by-step roadmap” eBook by
Cerbos (Sponsor)
<https://substack.com/redirect/bcb4f70a-c4ee-4ba9-be50-85582e203e48?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>
<https://substack.com/redirect/bcb4f70a-c4ee-4ba9-be50-85582e203e48?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

Hardcoded authorization logic doesn’t scale. As systems grow and
requirements change, those tightly coupled permissions end up slowing down
development, creating security risks, and making compliance a nightmare.

This *80-page eBook
<https://substack.com/redirect/bcb4f70a-c4ee-4ba9-be50-85582e203e48?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>*
breaks down how hundreds of engineering teams have tackled this problem. It
walks through the practical steps for moving from hardcoded permissions to
externalized authorization.

What’s in it:

 -

 Step-by-step adoption strategy from planning to PoC rollout
 -

 Frameworks, policy examples, code samples
 -

 Lessons from teams who’ve made the transition

If you’re dealing with authorization complexity, this might save you some
of the trial and error.

Download free eBook
<https://substack.com/redirect/bcb4f70a-c4ee-4ba9-be50-85582e203e48?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

(Thanks to Cerbos
<https://substack.com/redirect/bcb4f70a-c4ee-4ba9-be50-85582e203e48?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>
for partnering on this post.)
------------------------------

I want to introduce *Paul Hoekstra
<https://substack.com/redirect/5490f1f0-29c6-4301-b914-61d3cacdf278?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>*
as the guest author.
<https://substack.com/redirect/5490f1f0-29c6-4301-b914-61d3cacdf278?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

Paul is a data engineer at Digital Power, a data and AI consultancy, and
writes *Paul’s Pipeline
<https://substack.com/redirect/5490f1f0-29c6-4301-b914-61d3cacdf278?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>*,
a free newsletter on AI, data, and side projects.

This newsletter pulls out the ideas that keep mattering, whichever tool you
pick. If it leaves you wanting more, he has a four-part series on the
layers covered in this newsletter: configuration, capability,
orchestration, and guardrails.
------------------------------

*Here’s what you’ll find inside this newsletter:*
<https://substack.com/redirect/2c82e85a-4ec5-4959-a981-187f9b6921da?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

 -

 *Foundations.* What an agent is, how it runs, where its state lives, and
 the patterns you build once you have more than one.
 -

 *Configuration.* How you shape the agent’s behavior before it writes a
 line of code: config files, reusable workflows, prompt caching, and the
 context rot that limits all of it.
 -

 *Capability.* What the agent can reach for once it is running: live
 documentation, AI-native search, long-term memory, and knowledge it queries
 but never owns.
 -

 *Orchestration.* How you coordinate multiple agents without letting them
 step on each other.
 -

 *Guardrails.* What stops the agents from doing damage: sandboxing,
 permissions, hooks, and the commit gates that catch the rest.
 -

 *Observability.* How you see what an agent actually did after it stopped
 typing: tracing, logging, replay, and metrics.

Upgrade to paid
<https://substack..8ybv1NXMQbPlUHWM1x-Mi90hxyNfKpYFTSgYqxHDqYs?&utm_medium=email&utm_source=subscribe-widget&utm_content=200995493>
------------------------------
*Foundations*

Let’s dive in!
*1. What is an agent?*

“Agent” gets used for almost anything these days.

Let us pin it down:

An agent is a large language model (*LLM*) that runs in a loop, has access
to tools¹, and decides what to do next. Instead of a single answer to a
prompt, the model produces a chain of actions. And each action builds on
the result of the last.

Coding is the most obvious use case so far.

The loop fits naturally there. Small building blocks. Fast feedback from
tests and errors. Files the agent can edit directly. The same shape works
for research agents, ops automation, customer support, and content
workflows.

Use an agent when:

 -

 Task is open-ended (”debug this failing test”, not “format this date”)
 -

 The next step depends on the result of the last one
 -

 You cannot script the path in advance

But skip the agent when a single prompt or a plain script will do.
<https://substack.com/redirect/87357e96-6669-4e2a-818e-ef60d4674e27?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

Loops are not free.

Every time round the loop costs time and money, and they add up fast. The
agent gets less predictable. And it gets harder to debug: the runs are
longer, and the agent does not always make the same choices twice.

You do not have to pick one mode for everything.

Use a single prompt or a plain script for easy problems. Reserve the agent
for tasks where its flexibility earns the cost.
------------------------------
*2. Execution model*

An agent loop has a recognizable shape.

Three steps, repeated until something says we are done:

 -

 First, the model *thinks*. It reads the current state of the
 conversation and decides what to do next.
 -

 The model then *acts*. It emits a tool call. The harness² around the
 model receives the call, validates it, and runs it: file read, shell
 command, MCP request, whatever fits.
 -

 Finally, the model *observes*. The tool result joins the conversation as
 new context. The next iteration starts with that result in scope.

This pattern goes by a few names.

ReAct (from the original paper
<https://substack.com/redirect/ce007234-6160-492c-959c-0ec089fcffaa?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>),
Think-act-observe, agent loop.

The name changes, but the loop does not.

A single model call has to predict the entire path up front and commit to
it. The loop replaces that prediction with feedback. The model takes one
step, observes what actually happened, and decides the next step based on
the actual result rather than a guess.

This is why an agent can recover from its own mistakes.

A failing test returns a stack trace. A blocked command returns an error.
Each one is just the next observation, and the next iteration plans against
it. The loop catches a wrong first step on the next pass, instead of
letting it poison everything after it.

Two variations sit on top of this basic loop:

 -

 *Parallelism*. Modern agents can fire several tool calls inside a single
 act step. That cuts wall-clock time on read-heavy work. It also causes
 conflicts when two parallel calls try to edit the same thing. We will come
 back to ways of handling that when we get to subagents (concept 17).

 -

 *Blocking vs non-blocking*. Most agents run synchronously: conversation
 waits while a tool runs, then the result comes back as the observation.
 Some run asynchronously and fire off long-running jobs in the background.
 Async unlocks fan-out, at the cost of more complexity.

------------------------------
*3. Agent state*

“State” gets used in two senses.

One is *workflow progress*: which step the agent is on and what is left. *Agent
loops* carry that sense across iterations (concept 17). This concept is the
other sense: everything the agent knows at any moment.

It breaks into two halves with very different properties:

 -

 *Context window*: everything the model can see right now. Your latest
 message, the system prompt, the agent’s previous tool calls, and the
 results of those calls. It has a hard ceiling (the token cap) and a soft
 one (concept 9, context rot). When a session ends, the context window is
 gone.

 -

 *Everything outside it*: what the model cannot see until it fetches it.
 Files on disk, database entries. Memory that survives across sessions
 (concept 14). Knowledge the agent only queries and never owns (concept 15).
 The model cannot reason over any of it directly, but the agent can pull
 pieces into context on demand.

<https://substack.com/redirect/8a97c18e-0e5e-4d65-9dec-a3c84c244b37?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

So, where should your state actually live?

Files are usually the right default. They are diffable, versionable, and
easy for both you and the agent to edit. Memory works well for facts that
survive across sessions but do not need git history. Databases earn their
place when the state needs to be queried by structure or shared across
processes.

Multiple agents make the state harder.

Two agents reading the same file is fine. Two agents writing to it is the
canonical race condition. Git worktrees (concept 16) give each agent its
own working copy.

Subagents are friendlier: each starts with a fresh context window, and the
parent passes in only what it needs. If the parent has to pass more than a
few paragraphs, the split between the two is probably wrong.
------------------------------
*4. Common agent patterns*

Once you have multiple agents to work with, the question is how to compose
them.

There are a handful of useful patterns to know:

The simplest is *planner/executor*.

One agent plans, another executes. The split keeps each agent in a context
shape that suits it: open-ended thinking on one side, tight execution on
the other. Useful for long tasks where you want planning quality separate
from how the implementation goes.
<https://substack.com/redirect/fc494e91-db12-4505-a9ba-a97bc7ded321?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

*Router/specialist* sits one level above.

A router agent takes the incoming request and dispatches it to one of
several specialists. Each specialist has a tighter system prompt and a
smaller toolset: a security reviewer, a debug specialist, a docs writer, a
test author. Cheaper, because most calls hit a smaller model. Easier to
reason about, because each specialist’s behavior is bounded.
<https://substack.com/redirect/fda5e8d8-2d79-4fe7-8e9b-f6e63d7575ca?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

*Map-reduce parallelism* is the workhorse for read-heavy fan-out.

Send a single task out across many subagents and merge their results. *“Review
this pull request” *splits into one subagent per file, and the results
merge into a single summary. Cost is bounded by the longest individual
subagent. Quality is bounded by how well the aggregator merges.
<https://substack.com/redirect/2e43c0bd-b53e-46b7-bcd9-fdb141148b56?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

These patterns are not exclusive.

Real workflows combine them: a planner spawns a router, the router fans out
work map-reduce style, and the merged result comes back for review.

Every pattern relies on handoffs between agents.

Each handoff carries a compressed summary, so the next agent does not start
from scratch. That is what makes the patterns useful: they define where one
agent’s job ends and the next one’s begins. Those seams are where work
tends to break.

That’s the foundation done.

Next come the four practical layers, starting with how you shape the
agent’s behavior before it touches any code.
------------------------------

*Get 1+ referral & I’ll send you my Leetcode Master Template!*

Share
<https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1511845&post_id=200995493&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&action=share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=2k46ui&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjoxNTQ3MjAxNzAsInBvc3RfaWQiOjIwMDk5NTQ5MywiaWF0IjoxNzgxNjg5MTE2LCJleHAiOjE3ODQyODExMTYsImlzcyI6InB1Yi0xNTExODQ1Iiwic3ViIjoicG9zdC1yZWFjdGlvbiJ9.CZcfri6GGTQ8GfxA0BTPLT6si-x5-T-85JKeMZi7ABE>
------------------------------
*Configuration Layer*

Let’s dive in!
*5. Agent config files*

Every agent starts with a system prompt.

The harness builder bakes in a block of instructions that covers tool use
and the harness’s own conventions. The system prompt does not know anything
about your project.

Agent config files are the layer above.

The agent loads a project-level Markdown file at the start of every
session, and the file remains in the context window for each subsequent
step. The file tells the agent your standards, limits, conventions, and
behavioral rules.

Claude Code calls this file CLAUDE.md.

Most other tools have settled on AGENTS.md. A bit like Apple and USB-C: the
industry wants a standard, but not everyone follows it.

It matters because agents default to whatever looked most plausible in
their training data. Without a config file, you get pip install when your
project uses uv, black when you use ruff, and defensive sludge wrapped
around every change.

With one, the agent reads a set of hard rules before it writes a line of
code.
<https://substack.com/redirect/8255e820-aae5-4b63-91b6-a12402858367?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

A useful config is short and specific:

 -

 Hard limits on function length
 -

 The package manager and tooling stack you actually use
 -

 A few behavioral rules like “never commit secrets” or “always read a
 file before editing it.”

Anything generic or AI-generated dilutes the file and makes the rest harder
for the model to attend to.

Keep it under 100 lines. Cut anything that is not consistently improving
outcomes. Treat it the way you would treat a Makefile: as code, not
documentation, reviewed when it changes.
------------------------------
*6. Reusable workflow files*

If config files are always on, reusable workflow files are on demand.

They are still just Markdown, but with YAML frontmatter that controls when
the agent loads them. Examples are Claude Code skills (in .claude/skills/)
and Cursor rules.

The frontmatter³ does most of the work. A name and description tell the
agent when the skill is relevant. An optional globs field narrows it
further by file type or path pattern. Whether the skill actually helps
depends on the quality of the instructions inside it.

Researchers built SkillsBench
<https://substack.com/redirect/f6d97bae-f1a9-42e8-8296-3602b6c12bce?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>,
a benchmark of 86 tasks across 11 domains, and provided models with brief
written workflows for approaching each task.

Claude Haiku scored 27.7% with human-curated skills. That beat the 22.0%
Opus scored without them. The cheapest model with good instructions
outperformed the flagship without.
<https://substack.com/redirect/9cad555c-d2f0-4dee-8ecf-60372b3c810b?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

There is a second lesson in the data.

When the researchers let the model write its own skills, the gains
disappeared. Given everything we know about context rot, that should
surprise no one. Generic AI-generated boilerplate makes things worse, not
better.

The rough division of labor is simple: *config files* for always-relevant
project rules, *skills* for reusable task-specific procedures.

The live prompt for what is unique about the current task.
------------------------------
*7. Workflow frameworks*

If you are using an agent for coding, a framework on top helps.

The agent follows a documented process for planning, test-driven
development (*TDD*), debugging, and code review. No more falling back on
whatever it remembers from training. The mechanism is a chain of skills,
hooks, and slash commands⁴ wired together into a repeatable process.

*Here are a few options worth knowing:*

 -

 Superpowers
 <https://substack.com/redirect/905f5176-8765-42b7-bbd7-e2c985c635c1?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>
 ships a curated library of skills for common workflows: brainstorming, TDD,
 debugging, and code review. It also adds HARD-GATE directives and
 anti-rationalization tables. These push the agent to use the skills rather
 than skip them.

 -

 Get Shit Done
 <https://substack.com/redirect/c04aa507-15aa-4917-98a0-6c76a9297eb2?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>
 does something similar, but through slash commands, hooks, and
 meta-prompting instead of skills.

 -

 Compound Engineering
 <https://substack.com/redirect/ef680097-ab86-48cb-a387-4461d9b19835?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>
 splits work into Plan, Work, Review, and Compound phases. The last phase
 captures patterns and solutions into a searchable knowledge base, so each
 feature makes the next one easier.

They differ in shape but share a thread: get the agent to understand what
it is building, tell it how to go to work, and check the result against
what you actually wanted.
------------------------------
*8. Prompt caching*

Prompt caching stores the *stable prefix* of the conversation so the model
does not pay the full price to re-read it every turn.

Most agentic tools handle this in the background, but it is worth
understanding because it changes the economics of long config files.

The mechanism is the same across providers:

The first call sends the full prefix, including the config file and any
reusable workflows the agent has loaded, and pays a small premium to write
that prefix into the cache. Later turns re-read it at a steep discount.
Latency improves, too, because the model does not reprocess the same tokens.
<https://substack.com/redirect/8a910fbd-9fc7-4be8-9b79-f5ea279b2ff9?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

The catch is the cache time to live (*TTL*).

Caches expire after a period of inactivity, somewhere between a few minutes
and an hour, depending on the provider. An active session keeps the cache
warm. A long pause (coffee, Slack, a spec to read) lets it expire and
forces a rewrite on the next call.

If your tooling lets you configure the TTL, longer windows cost more to
write but stay warm through bigger gaps in activity. The choice depends on
how active you expect to be. Longer TTL when you will keep coming back.
Shorter TTL when you might not.

Without prompt caching, you would think carefully about the size of your
config file. With it, the cost is closer to a rounding error.
------------------------------
*9. Context rot*

Model performance drops as the context window fills up.

Prompt caching saves your wallet, but those tokens still sit there, and the
model has to look past each one to get to what you want it to focus on.

*Even the latest models show this:*

 -

 Drop a few specific facts into a long document and ask a model to find
 them. GPT-5.5
 <https://substack.com/redirect/b503953e-9b79-4774-a396-420fbe2f6959?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>
 scores 98.1% on short documents and 74.0% on documents stretching to a
 million tokens.
 -

 Claude Opus 4.7
 <https://substack.com/redirect/207b0ad0-34ca-4707-a22a-99b9f13432d1?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>
 drops from 59.2% at 256K to 32.2% at 1M.

A
<https://substack.com/redirect/c13d552a-c949-44ef-94cc-72ae43de1624?j=eyJ1IjoiMms0NnVpIn0.eN3Vqw52i_cVt1Oub17rQ-NLdq4q4wOcSrCuI-WZ-GI>

Excel, Fable Ban, GLM-5.2, and DR-DCI

Read online
<https://link.mail.beehiiv.com/ss/c/u001.sWRNP4iE09-YiMbq86AiNA9dka5ZutgppfteQjEmca1NVIAQg84U1jZjiwKkHmE2m4RLMMnCSYzL2kt824aj6r-8PouZHP5aWDWiXan6j69F7cWEzrEupSCd5Jc4DrImUsIgGaYBXrH5PI0c9gQFVJwdTIiNPq0AWxuo6x8G48Lnyz2KYJO6xoPQRq0wpDOh61R5RdT6etnxZPfgA7YRe8Tl81yqOq2XHN9FAYXYHFrl1Z3ppEqCS_-vwspDsx-3z1FTVROMEB_96R2D223csaXmy_6dkH8aGjMupiQOphUX4XjMXMXlTaRXbw1Ek8LWfmHC0aDj-8ga1o4GtRlRLS1jQ5S-BJIXarhvS5b6L_hl6rY6lrQK6IPTmKsqHS710E_Nq5fhYRqKaN0r68pN04JYiaQlHzoqaU51CYRNbQidPb1se9HjLRqByUXmoQGjIUB2adj95ee0E8V9fk7XGW2KGUEMLJldPRXy1yvIMtNDaiS87-9D_5NntEfI9tO0K8btlf_M54e09SJ2M2rmFdmq-KtSQ3x7FvwBSyJWhqaT1nZDxM0ZQPm3MPHoIiOKfnLFyHF_thN-wOQykWLzzG2qOFH41ba0z4NDpeO2SDJrES6QhskcmCPEGytCsTOeI_OPm3vTXY0lhlnxNzfuodFT_3WihfuSorGUi53XtAJv5ppe9JclJyhva15P6o7pJEUKTpu8Gq5Kk9JA26wl9U4ytQDMPgDEtY5g__iP54VlQUT0btroW8SeUze88qCLqrNZONenUi2Xh5istCUHhOMuJntszqbnuz1ONOQza0c/4rm/2_NJKj_3TB2RDv6Z_iUFxg/h0/h001.HZ1LGOuFKHdWHmnItwUqEcNoH91FozQaEaYQmN4-FQI>

Between the Fable ban and the GLM-5.2 release, it was a pretty big week for
the local LLM community I would say. One showed why local AI is so
important, the other showed that it was actually possible to have open
weights models that approach the top closed frontier models. We just need
to lower the hardware barrier somehow and I think local AI will really
explode.

*If you want to support my work and get more of my content, **please
consider joining Team Garage
<https://link.mail.beehiiv.com/ss/c/u001.K7o6GO8hPpeLCugXurZ54XxEfG8gGVwlkN6jAzvj2E0MNbTl3G4NogtUm_9aylxE8urgRq4egGSjJSw_7UHYHqU2ue-JuUS-9L9oellrNylLWuGPgsspuHA9p1p2uJJLkBJ3ioqs4fDakWQqUqZhBgBAHcoJz3h7ib-yvK6VsmlRe9H50wtfCn9Tk45d1UvIj3iqfDg9pOUxk05NcHQj82bzH0IhWBmywPpgiSr7L6Q7EtGVKuS44vqjok0CTmBEa5hSI_LC9SehqpSCqpNFE0nHk5pg13EYK-rshtV_Smg/4rm/2_NJKj_3TB2RDv6Z_iUFxg/h1/h001.bBIq2g-gD5u7M6e3NOuzXs7YJv0pMFlsSmQUI1IReto>**.
Thank you!*

*🎬 Video of the Week: Hermes Agent in Excel.* So my wife was talking about
using Claude in Excel for her work, and I had the thought: Could I do that
with Hermes Agent? I hadn’t heard of that type of integration, I wasn’t
even sure if it was possible, but I started to research a way to do it. The
first attempt I actually recorded a full video using a much more
complicated, much more frustrating technique. I got to the end of it and
asked my agent: Isn’t there an easier way to do this? And after some extra
research, they said yes! "YOU SHOULD'VE TOLD ME THAT TO BEGIN WITH” I
screamed in my head, not wanting to upset my agent too much (lol). In the
end that was the method I ended up recording for this video, it does
require some prep work to start up, but the end result is pretty cool. You
get a chat window right in Excel, plus you can create custom functions with
your Hermes. I was excited for this video and while I got a lot of nice
comments on it, it didn’t go super viral as I had hoped (lol). I would
still like to try more integrations like this, I think this would be
genuinely useful for a lot of people.

How I Put Hermes Agent Inside Excel (And Made It a Custom Function)
<https://link.mail.beehiiv.com/ss/c/u001.maopPdr3OQftpzkXs5OR4-tDkO_ZYupC2QTCbId7IkHkiJxZzzIEuleA0Zxl07q_mIlFIjX1d-qo0fTV8ktpwzR6AzV7NOC9JaoERjTgGRKKpgQr8t9M6Ax6eRdxc2VS1KSZt9LH0au-x505YOJCIHODQTRnFuG4RF0Fca33iwrdB49JxH-Wpzn-QIBG9tCOiNcW0-lZMJIx8ziuwfUR4WLoyIC_B8CQQdw3BksEQ74jMfWREhLH2w5wWjK9POqQ/4rm/2_NJKj_3TB2RDv6Z_iUFxg/h2/h001.Uo1z3BXiQOSpNs0ZoYyWZCZTVSRrzmTQWvnIe6dPxT8>

If you want to try it out yourself, check out my open source github.
<https://link.mail.beehiiv.com/ss/c/u001.qJ5Rzv1y5w9Ogi8ibfVtWgZcfXE2VG8Vw54KSfDWY6h70LFeL-Q7Hx42sHfgOTOHMfMA8Tvh0i2lXuut8kk0h-HXg9zZbozz-7x3AickIscmvoz47v_X2unfOY-IPfm5OqSPCBDoJ7W6GB2UFlen122VfcEGkssl7vh2JO8LNdhT5quXBuoVWvc_bqV3T5MZ0ZZSS0xTlNNgdAuBmngLXc6nngpFV3y34Goi5ri2vNdx-hdK2WRft7oI-HEsR61LRab8ztti1FMpsJDIdZ8pXA/4rm/2_NJKj_3TB2RDv6Z_iUFxg/h3/h001.0XFq9A22xcSOelVpwn2BUywwViPilIFy83_G8h69g1U>

📡* AI News & Model Releases*

*Claude Fable 5 banned. *So last Friday after I had sent out my newsletter,
the big news came out that Anthropic was removing Fable 5 access due to a
new directive from the US government. It was really surprising news, a lot
of people were really blown away by Fable and disappointed by this move. I
was disappointed too, but I’ve been convinced this was the direction we
were headed anyway. These models are so powerful, the government isn’t
going to let private companies and individuals have power like that.
They’ll want to control it. I think this specific case is just some weird
Anthropic and the current administration negotiating in public and it will
get cleared up eventually, but it still highlights the need for local and
decentralized AI moving forward. You can’t let all that power be handed to
only the current administration, or 1-2 companies, people should have
access to these tools.

*GLM-5.2 released.* I’ve been impressed with GLM models before, so when I
heard about this new open weights model release, I was excited to try. It
was really impressive! I gave it some tasks in my first look video
<https://link.mail.beehiiv.com/ss/c/u001.maopPdr3OQftpzkXs5OR46A_jo6Y7Xo1nEzS7v9wnTevikoVdIWk6LsE_39HxqJzcDxXDoWqo_u6UB9-LcV2K3XS3F0WTnYXwTY2eOHTxz05WfVyjU00dTp-LiJvdDyRZ46YLxfiOh04gXO4-OcEWNqfKZQrqgW0vD1vdYEV1PS1-rdH-HHYsmBoi37TuPodvWwKo5xTE9HccUnl6Nk7W1Pe-eIr7VCBt_Gdu5AJwisjYySBMaX5zdml2oOONzbW/4rm/2_NJKj_3TB2RDv6Z_iUFxg/h4/h001.-Ba0GbC3tRZ_TluxDRGlISpj4jFyFN5yb4i0KxTrSoM>
and I’ve been using it a bit since then, and it does feel like only
slightly behind Opus 4.8/GPT5.5, maybe one generation behind. This is still
an incredible feat for an open source model, and it does make me think that
we will get genuine frontier-level open source models sooner than we think.
Now of course most people won’t be able to run them locally, it would take
some serious hardware to be able to run it that isn’t super slow and
quality-degraded, but it just shows what is possible for the future.

📄* Research & Experiments*

*Dr-DCI: Scaling Direct Corpus Interaction via Dynamic Workspace Expansion *
(https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14885
<https://link.mail.beehiiv.com/ss/c/u001.sWRNP4iE09-YiMbq86AiNKCz3_c_sEFJdpf586h1ytRwjwrYeyP5z5CRAlm3L6vCawTbrn9WFUey6hN7nQQHcjr-WG_IbzlZjgSXULx8xco3bdimt6OEzfRm8lK9KbiHmnXtTZENt1HOjjt08xS6c_LcN2jkLYGtoOYyek2cyWGbIVoW62tQj1KXXYd9MpncGX9mmu7OiVHBmYlk7bGbYWtx9xGdP89-iWIfNn5tl41hYcrml4e7CzvR6MHhsNTgLcGUwc5lMg4KZB8hfKvfDg/4rm/2_NJKj_3TB2RDv6Z_iUFxg/h5/h001.DZm6lNmTfMV-U0jnH-0EJhgtW7G3p-OEdXUk8Pwgbh8>)
With Agent Wikis, one of the big things I’ve been researching is retrieval,
how agents are able to collect and process information. I wanted to see if
I could find a way to go beyond RAG, or enhance RAG in some way. This paper
caught my eye. It describes an agent retrieval/workspace pattern that could
be effective for Agent Wikis, or having any agent handle any large corpus.

With normal RAG, you have the agent ask a retriever, get snippets, and then
answer from those snippets. With DCI, and earlier version of this method,
the agent gets direct shell/file access to the corpus, and can use tools
like find, read, compare files, inspect evidence. The problem with this is
that it breaks when the corpus is huge. Search millions of files directly
is slow and noisy. So with DR-DCI, that huge corpus is hidden behind a
retriever, but the agent can call pull(query, topK), to bring relevant
documents into a local workspace, then use normal file tools on that
smaller workspace. So it actually gives you RAG-like recall with filesystem
agency from DCI. They found that using this method increased accuracy as
well as significantly improved wall time and costs.

So the applications for Agent Wikis is pretty clear, I experimented with
RAG over the wikis and found that to be one the best methods, but some of
these wikis are quite large, so it could become challenging. Since these
are specifically agent focused, I think having this smaller workspace to
pull relevant chunks out could be useful, because I imagine people are
using this knowledge not for just one-off questions, but for ongoing
project work. Still need to figure out how or if I can even do this, but I
thought it was interesting.

 💭* Random Brainstorming & Project Progress*

*Agents Wikis: *Nice release for Agent Wikis
<https://link.mail.beehiiv.com/ss/c/u001.tU_53qzZnJgdpDP9xqcKJNH9KLd6ZMrr7Fw9dHouGfRFhgCmmRic0LbbRzts6ZHTrDVYi2YF0FQA4ANZbc2gB1wPTDbLp4NR5IHNcMZ8-PEDxshbnJd8JPeVctMv85ilbn0VDrrmsu9A1cn1hI6-26lmY7rFq4J0VGa_U7_JMYtxw1guNG7R1q7pNozK2Wp2vl7s23JyR7gQhwkCJfbBGhrB-JLzvQDzLQIiTueLCynueQGxUz72qWKf8-ocJQhj/4rm/2_NJKj_3TB2RDv6Z_iUFxg/h6/h001.l5vIV_RtRiC0OSGWGzlkN-z_QMCCLl8PmW8k0XZETMY>,
people seemed to really like it and the first release day got nearly 10k
visitors on the site (humans and agents), and we’ve been doing 5k-7k since
then. Very large improvement from the last time I released a project before
my YouTube channel (I was happy with 10 visitors, lol). I’ve been refining
the pipeline to maintaining these wikis, trying to make it as autonomous as
possible without ruining everything. The one thing I did was to create a
loop so that missed topics will get researched and then autonomously added
to the wikis. This will ensure that the wikis actually answer the questions
that people have on these topics (without any extra work from me).
Yesterday’s video showed this in action.

I’ve been exploring other ways to allow agents to maintain and enhance the
product. I think once I’m ready to do premium wikis with the stripe
integration, that would be a good place for it. I may also explore doing
some small ads after that to see how those can drive traffic. I would love
to do an advertising pipeline where the agent develops all the ad material
(so I can be lazy), releases the ads, then analyzes the results and hones
the ads based on that. I’ve heard of people doing this, but that would be
an amazing loop to have. The ads themselves would cost money though, so
I’ll need the site to be profitable before I try that.

*AI Garage Weekly publishes every Friday.*

*Tonbi’s AI Garage
<https://link.mail.beehiiv.com/ss/c/u001.K7o6GO8hPpeLCugXurZ54ZFCdyD6EDV06lwMM6OqsySZWBrQiVXiQyghi5O4fkDog8hAHZDebn_KuMrVI6Top6LF6gZfA3AWSwiX49n8dNJF1x8Ih4iyK8o3ZJtmHR1VgZjGHWVFxs7Xtq3dmiM2Mh8omarqqsthd_MV7P6C9aBsQdKwhsIxX7WkJesJb2XqbNhZOyNvU1p7tWrtCK_YblCbszOrTNYPJFhpyKQ1d_OrrjKguFRA7hk-Acqn2e4gNnqQP9YwzMeiZ_39GZ1Mxw/4rm/2_NJKj_3TB2RDv6Z_iUFxg/h7/h001.1Rg1pOdr5o-_HOwAVbntEnBl1D-LIqSnCMG30SbJDWg>*

pages.title: Every Level of a Claude Second Brain Explained
pages.body:
# Every Level of a Claude Second Brain Explained

**Source:** email (Nate Herk / AI Automation Society via sayf)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee2dfa87f86548
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T12:10:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf on 2026-06-20 (originally from Nate Herk via AI Automation Society newsletter, June 19, 2026)

> A Claude second brain breakdown, a client pipeline automated, and Codex hits 5M users with a free bonus through June 24.

## In This Issue

- Five levels of building a Claude second brain, from basic file search to fully autonomous recall.
- Robert automated a client's outbound email pipeline and got their call center actually following up.
- OpenAI's Codex just crossed 5 million weekly users, with a free bonus available through June 24.

## New Video: Every Level of a Claude Second Brain Explained

There are five distinct levels to building a Claude second brain, and most people are stuck at level one.

- **Level one**: Finding a file by searching an exact word.
- **Level five**: A fully autonomous system that knows your business, your client relationships, and your IP better than you do.

*The insight that changes how you build:* Start from the end. Before you organize a single file, ask how you'll access this data later. The shape of the basketball determines the size of the hoop. Jon shows his actual setup (a Herku project, just markdown files and folders) and walks through what separates each level, including semantic search (finding by meaning, not exact match) and full relationship mapping.

*How this helps you:* Your moat is your data. This video shows you how to actually organize it so your agents can use it, not just find it.

## Winning Is All We Do — Robert's Client Pipeline

Robert automated a client's outbound email pipeline from scratch:

- The client's call center was calling leads but nothing followed up by email.
- Sales managers emailed Robert their daily lead lists as Excel files.
- He built an automation: when the file lands in his inbox, it extracts email, address, and name data, normalizes it, and uploads every contact directly into Instantly as a ready-to-run campaign.
- No manual cleanup. No copy-paste. Just results.

*The opportunity:* Businesses running call centers almost always have messy lead data and no email follow-up. An automation that converts a spreadsheet into a live campaign solves a problem they didn't know anyone could fix.

## In the News — Codex Hits 5M Weekly Users

OpenAI Codex has crossed 5 million weekly active users and launched two features:

1. **Banked rate limit resets (permanent)**: Instead of your weekly limit resetting on a fixed timer, you can now save resets and use them when you need them.
2. **Referral bonus (through June 24)**: Plus and Pro users can invite up to three friends to try Codex. When a friend sends their first Codex message, you both get a bonus reset.

*The bigger signal:* Codex is growing through referral mechanics at 5M weekly users — a tool people are actively building with and recommending.

## Entities

- Nate Herk — author, AI Automation Society
- Robert — AI Automation Society member who built the client pipeline automation
- Jon — creator of the Claude second brain video (shown setup with Herku project)
- OpenAI — company behind Codex
- AI Automation Society — newsletter publisher

June 19, 2026
Every Level of a Claude Second Brain Explained

A Claude second brain breakdown, a client pipeline automated, and Codex
hits 5M users with a free bonus through June 24.
In This Issue

 - Five levels of building a Claude second brain, from basic file search
 to fully autonomous recall.
 - [[Robert]] automated a client's outbound email pipeline and got their call
 center actually following up.
 - [[OpenAI]]'s Codex just crossed 5 million weekly users, and there's a free
 bonus available through June 24 if you use it.

------------------------------

New Video:
Every Level of a Claude Second Brain Explained[image: Every Level of a
Claude Second Brain Explained]
<https://elinkb0c.aiautomationsociety.ai/ss/c/u001.VkheyC2iT8s9YixUfVE0FN6mOGbCz03lVLbDvGOnPMRbj1rwVrXHTck2hIU01ytm6ClnUpm2K0lB7mfU5yrnXJjbg8QOjFt3YAAKynvRly7AZ5RR698TUVLB1zYkbta24co1HZnzOxjFXLUZv3ry3OJUJaQO2r6SdQ3rs1ZE-tuIy_yHTO_Bfo-2VgtVkOcj2AoD3b_3MEPaz31dIy6HzPc_NXe6k-vE4IDjB0d-OGcjLL4MASMx2vqbk5JDz52cpqavnEF7OLT0BiCFKjmxKk0A8cDqQ-BCgG9eRr69RPo/4rm/nAjOFF8fS2ql8mcgXnK77A/h0/h001.wfRbjLyik5QKffJyHRlg6z_7nnGpBaTl3dZ0bHQfX5o>

There are five distinct levels to building a Claude second brain, and most
people are stuck at level one.

Level one is just finding a file by searching an exact word. Level five is
a fully autonomous system that knows your business, your client
relationships, and your IP better than you do. The difference between them
isn't the tool. It's how the data is organized.

*The insight that changes how you build:* Start from the end. Before you
organize a single file, ask how you'll access this data later. The shape of
the basketball determines the size of the hoop. [[Jon]] shows his actual setup
(a [[Herku]] project, just markdown files and folders) and walks through what
separates each level, including semantic search (finding by meaning, not
exact match) and full relationship mapping.

*How this helps you:* Your moat is your data. This video shows you how to
actually organize it so your agents can use it, not just find it.
Watch and Discuss
<https://elinkb0c.aiautomationsociety.ai/ss/c/u001.VkheyC2iT8s9YixUfVE0FN6mOGbCz03lVLbDvGOnPMRbj1rwVrXHTck2hIU01ytm6ClnUpm2K0lB7mfU5yrnXJjbg8QOjFt3YAAKynvRly7AZ5RR698TUVLB1zYkbta24co1HZnzOxjFXLUZv3ry3OJUJaQO2r6SdQ3rs1ZE-tuIy_yHTO_Bfo-2VgtVkOcj2AoD3b_3MEPaz31dIy6HzPc_NXe6k-vE4IDjB0d-OGeeOo2QBr9_Zujgy33ZMhAJ26EFSpJ1GgSYK6UgmwphKgn5xFJBlGtnXyMu9ewrr_M/4rm/nAjOFF8fS2ql8mcgXnK77A/h1/h001.D54kEdiOKmTE1gdR6HxnPRIS-rw5zfBHCdsutPNTL-U>
------------------------------

Winning Is All We Do
[[Robert]] automated a client's outbound email pipeline from scratch[image:
Student win screenshot]

*What happened:* [[Robert]]'s client had a call center calling leads, but
nothing was following up by email. Sales managers emailed [[Robert]] their
daily lead lists as Excel files. He built an automation that handles the
rest: when the file lands in his inbox, it extracts the email, address, and
name data, normalizes it (no more "N123 MAIN STREET" showing up as a
deliverable address), and uploads every contact directly into [[Instantly]] as
a ready-to-run campaign. No manual cleanup. No copy-paste. Just results.

*The opportunity for you:* Businesses running call centers almost always
have messy lead data and no email follow-up. An automation that converts a
spreadsheet into a live campaign solves a problem they didn't know anyone
could fix. [[Robert]]'s words say it best: "Life got easier."

This is one of many success stories from [[AI Automation Society]] Plus. If
you're not a member, you're missing out.
Join the Fun
<https://elinkb0c.aiautomationsociety.ai/ss/c/u001.sUMBhgQeRd9Qnhv4nbgBFzMOQQ8QVEsqhqtd_5TB8UqmpZv-n0v97IvP73VQj3nS0vJkAiw4-xW9XasYLpq76t-NylJoDnO8KnziSeij3ZXIPAKpKHh0SvzKSmTSWkOG86up6fBwdKpjfJvUrTyz_wAleTC4JE-qKsBTKbBV4suvZli6biHo7OncF_3NLGUME_E_r8wV2PGolDvCHKSs7V2j2KdYb7e5gRlWV86KvVbCgV_BUtoPKpDLELKE0d2wdIoXSoR7kyqWxmIEle846Yk9vCXnRjhhvljQnMJRK8nyWAyp4sKB8YbG0enOrWKG/4rm/nAjOFF8fS2ql8mcgXnK77A/h2/h001.PjDesWsrOd1Wad7G_NZfe_abdyRPpfR4QW_wlSAm-IE>
------------------------------

In the News
Codex just hit 5 million weekly users. If you use it, there's a free bonus
through June 24.

*The news:* [[OpenAI]] Codex has crossed 5 million weekly active users and
launched two features alongside the milestone. One is permanent: banked
rate limit resets. Instead of your weekly limit resetting on a fixed timer
(whether you need it or not), you can now save resets and use them when you
actually need them. The other runs through June 24. Plus and Pro users can
invite up to three friends to try Codex. When a friend sends their first
Codex message, you both get a bonus reset.

*How it affects you:* If you've been hitting Codex rate limits at the wrong
moment mid-build, the banked reset system fixes that. You decide when to
use your reset, not the clock. And if you're not hitting limits yet, the
referral is easy headroom to bank before the window closes. The bigger
signal here: Codex is now growing through referral mechanics at 5 million
weekly users. That's not hype. That's a tool people are actively building
with and recommending. Read more here.
<https://elinkb0c.aiautomationsociety.ai/ss/c/u001.vIwap7F5BoKkFByTrDP9CaAdcRmbimcRKrenySEvwCOGzAL7MTRbp2P19av8ncI1VtPSY-daqrNz4JlOEqrllmil71-gSYtJuMslC6VwayE04FHOne3hQWyiaYkHF32z2rcp0nQwB-DhRZHDoAq8duXcO9Ctgjf7x7QWRCghvtcZxSpFLG4pc-abRrzwR1Hbs2foiQJYhXICPwV6cHeU173HVqLHSCRKYVJFUIDVAe05i_Q_UH1jyJOcC0P0r1j5TnHO2YQpzAa9-DKXhB1LkkNxppoXdcoh5A1uR3vt4MzrUfKtFhcz-Avnp9JGFRuh/4rm/nAjOFF8fS2ql8mcgXnK77A/h3/h001.4laLyHKiBhNRqHm_u_ny8DZ7yLsXcC8CMOSWEkCyuXA>

pages.title: Openclaw Anatomy 2026 06 09
pages.body:
# The Anatomy of OpenClaw

**Source:** email (sayf forward)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ea8014fa960cb7
**Ingested:** 2026-06-22T18:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by Sayf via System Design One (Neo Kim) #151, 2026-06-09

## Summary

A deep dive into OpenClaw, an open-source autonomous AI agent that runs on your machine, connects to messaging apps, and executes tasks end-to-end. The newsletter explains how OpenClaw differs from browser-based AI tools and covers its five-layer architecture.

## Key Ideas

- **OpenClaw as the "hand" to LLM's "brain":** LLMs (Claude, GPT, Gemini) provide reasoning; OpenClaw provides execution — connecting to files, messaging apps, shell, and APIs.
- **Always-on Gateway:** A Node.js server running on port 18789 that orchestrates everything. This persistence is the foundation — scheduled jobs, proactive alerts, multi-channel messaging, and long-term memory all depend on a never-stopping process.
- **Channel Layer abstraction:** Each messaging platform (Telegram, WhatsApp, Slack, Discord) is a plugin adapter that normalizes messages into a standard internal format. The agent is platform-agnostic.
- **LLM-agnostic:** Swap models (Claude, GPT-4, DeepSeek, Gemini, local Ollama) without reconfiguration. System prompt is assembled dynamically from personality, available skills, memory, and conversation history.
- **Tool-call loop:** Model signals tool calls → OpenClaw executes → captures output → feeds back to model. Loop continues until no more tool calls are needed.
- **Three-tier memory:** Short-term (session files on disk), long-term (indexed DB for cross-session recall), and standing notes (persistent Markdown files).
- **Plugin system:** Almost everything non-core is a plugin (Telegram, GitHub, memory). Hot-config reloads — change model/permissions/channels without restart.
- **Long-running tasks:** Agent acknowledges, starts working, messages when done. Cron jobs in isolated sessions for truly background work.
- **Security:** Shell commands are intercepted for approval before execution. Control UI at `http://localhost:18789` for real-time monitoring.

## Architecture (5 Layers)

1. **Gateway** — Control center, always-on server coordinating everything
2. **Channel Layer** — Platform adapters (Telegram, WhatsApp, Slack, Discord) normalizing messages
3. **LLM Layer** — Model-agnostic reasoning with dynamic context assembly
4. **Plugin System** — Modular capabilities, hot-reloadable
5. **Memory System** — Short-term + long-term + standing notes

## People Mentioned

- [[Neo Kim]] — Author of [[System Design One]] newsletter

## Related Entities

- MyClaw — Cloud-hosted OpenClaw service
- ClawHub — Skill marketplace with 5,400+ capabilities

pages.title: Openrouter Auto Routing 2026 06 10
pages.body:
# OpenRouter: openrouter/auto — Per-Request Model Routing

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eb1db6efeaffa6
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T04:30:00Z
**Provenance:** OpenRouter Team newsletter to info@malohacoast.com, 2026-06-10

One model name. The router picks per request based on task complexity. No routing fee.

**Key points:**
- `openrouter/auto` as the model name lets OpenRouter classify the request and pick a model from the auto pool based on task complexity
- No routing fee — pay standard rate for whichever model serves the response
- ~63% of auto traffic routes to models priced under $1/M tokens
- Simple prompts stop hitting expensive models without any routing code
- Can pass `route="fallback"` alongside auto for a named backstop
- Auto pool changes as new models launch — no code update needed
- Read `response.model` to see what the router picked (useful for logging cost drift)
- **When not to use:** If you already know the exact model for a given call, pin the model string. Auto is for the 80% where choice doesn't matter.

[Source: OpenRouter Team newsletter, 2026-06-10](https://openrouter.ai/models/openrouter/auto)

pages.title: Chain models for complex tasks
pages.body:
# Chain models for complex tasks

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ed0c0b31514494
**Ingested:** 2026-06-16
**Provenance:** OpenRouter onboarding email to info@malohacoast.com

## Summary

OpenRouter onboarding newsletter covering chain models for complex tasks.

## Key Content

Use a fast model for classification, a strong model for generation. One endpoint handles both.  

OpenRouter
OpenRouter (  )

 

Hi there,

Use a fast model for classification, a strong model for generation. Same endpoint, same auth, different model parameter. That is the point of having 300+ models on one API: you mix them per step instead of committing to one for the whole workload.

Browse models (  )

 

PATTERN 1: CLASSIFY, THEN GENERATE

# Step 1: classify with a fast, cheap model
classify = client.chat.completions.create
(   model="openai/gpt-4o-mini",
  messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Classify: {query}"}], )


# Step 2: generate with a strong model
result = client.chat.completions.create
(   model="anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6",
  messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"{classify.choices[0].message.content}: {query}"}], )


GPT-4o-mini runs around $0.15 per million input tokens. Claude Sonnet 4.6 runs around $3 per million. Using the cheap model for routing and the expensive one only where it earns its cost can cut total spend 5 to 10x on mixed workloads.

 

PATTERN 2: EXTRACT, THEN WRITE

# Step 1: structured extraction
extracted = client.chat.completions.create
(   model="openai/gpt-4o-mini",
  response_format={"type": "json_object"},
  messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Extract fields as JSON: {doc}"}], )


# Step 2: long-form writing from the structured fields
draft = client.chat.completions.create
(   model="anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6",
  messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Write a report from: {extracted.choices[0].message.content}"}], )


Structured outputs are cheap and fast on small models. Long-form generation is where the frontier models actually earn their per-token rate. Splitting the work keeps the expensive calls short.

Why this works on OpenRouter specifically. One endpoint means no separate SDK per provider, no second billing account, no new authentication. The model parameter is the only thing that changes between steps, and the activity dashboard shows both calls side by side so you can see exactly where the cost went.

Long-form generation is where the frontier models actually earn their per-token rate. Splitting the work keeps the expensive calls short. Why this works on OpenRouter specifically. One endpoint means no separate SDK per provider, no second billing account, no new authentication. The model parameter is the only thing that changes between steps, and the activity dashboard shows both calls side by side so you can see exactly where the cost went. openrouter.ai ( https://openrouter.ai?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A7+Model+Chaining&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )  ·  Discord ( https://discord.com/invite/8TpuzNXQYr?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A7+Model+Chaining&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )  ·  X ( https://x.com/openrouter?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A7+Model+Chaining&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io )  ·  Docs ( https://openrouter.ai/docs?utm_campaign=Onboarding+Flow+-+v4+April+2026&utm_content=A7+Model+Chaining&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=customer.io ) Manage preferences ( https://track.customer.io/manage_subscription_preferences/dgSE_QoBALa-kAS1vpAEAZ7QwKu_lQ8DgQpjUtxd4A== )   |  
Use a fast model for classification, a strong model for generation. One endpoint handles both.  
 

Hi there,

Use a fast model for classification, a strong model for generation. Same endpoint, same auth, different model parameter. That is the point of having 300+ models on one API: you mix them per step instead of committing to one for the whole workload.

Browse models
 

PATTERN 1: CLASSIFY, THEN GENERATE

# Step 1: classify with a fast, cheap model
classify = client.chat.completions.create(
  model="openai/gpt-4o-mini",
  messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Classify: {query}"}],
)

# Step 2: generate with a strong model
result = client.chat.completions.create(
  model="anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6",
  messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"{classify.choices[0].message.content}: {query}"}],
)

GPT-4o-mini runs around $0.15 per million input tokens. Claude Sonnet 4.6 runs around $3 per million. Using the cheap model for routing and the expensive one only where it earns its cost can cut total spend 5 to 10x on mixed workloads.

 

PATTERN 2: EXTRACT, THEN WRITE

# Step 1: structured extraction
extracted = client.chat.completions.create(
  model="openai/gpt-4o-mini",
  response_format={"type": "json_object"},
  messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Extract fields as JSON: {doc}"}],
)

# Step 2: long-form writing from the structured fields
draft = client.chat.completions.create(
  model="anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6",
  messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Write a report from: {extracted.choices[0].message.content}"}],
)

Structured outputs are cheap and fast on small models. Long-form generation is where the frontier models actually earn their per-token rate. Splitting the work keeps the expensive calls short.

Why this works on OpenRouter specifically. One endpoint means no separate SDK per provider, no second billing account, no new authentication. The model parameter is the only thing that changes between steps, and the activity dashboard shows both calls side by side so you can see exactly where the cost went.

pages.title: How provider data policies work on OpenRouter
pages.body:
# How provider data policies work on [[OpenRouter]]

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee03492356403f
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T04:00:00Z
**Provenance:** OpenRouter newsletter to info@malohacoast.com

Every provider behind [[OpenRouter]] has its own retention and training policy. [[OpenRouter]] reflects those policies as structured data on each endpoint, publishes the full table in the docs, and gives you controls to filter routing on them.

## What You Control

1. **Block routing to providers that train** — Account-wide toggle in privacy settings for whether routing may use providers that train on prompts, with separate switches for paid and free models.
2. **Require a specific data policy per request** — Override account default on any single call via data policy filtering in provider routing docs.
3. **Pick only zero-retention providers** — Many providers on [[OpenRouter]] are zero retention: Amazon Bedrock, Azure, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, Together, and others.

## EU Routing

[[OpenRouter]] supports EU in-region routing for enterprise accounts. When enabled, prompts and completions are processed inside the European Union via the https://eu.openrouter.ai base URL.

pages.title: Openrouter Editor Integrations 2026 06 06
pages.body:
# OpenRouter: Use OpenRouter in Cursor, Continue, or Cline

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19e9d3c7f616e13b
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T04:30:00Z
**Provenance:** OpenRouter Team newsletter to info@malohacoast.com, 2026-06-06

An OpenRouter key works inside editors and agents most developers already have open. No SDK to wire up.

**Integrations:**
- **Cursor:** Settings > Models > OpenRouter. Paste key — every model in the OpenRouter catalog becomes available
- **Continue:** Add OpenRouter as a provider in config.json. One block, one key
- **Cline:** Set OpenRouter as API provider. Auto-routing picks the right model per request
- **Claude Code:** Use OpenRouter as backend to centralize usage across a team

**Why multi-model swap matters:** Coding agents make 20-50 model calls per task. Routing structured steps to a small model and writing steps to a frontier model cuts total cost by 5-10x on realistic workloads.

[Source: OpenRouter Team newsletter, 2026-06-06](https://openrouter.ai/docs/frameworks)

pages.title: Openrouter Fallback Models 2026 06 09
pages.body:
# OpenRouter: Add Fallback Models in One Line

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eacb4dc07cd44c
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T04:30:00Z
**Provenance:** OpenRouter Team newsletter to info@malohacoast.com, 2026-06-09

Pass a list of models. OpenRouter retries the next one if your primary fails. No app changes.

**Key points:**
- OpenRouter accepts a list of models on every request via `extra_body.models`
- If first choice fails or rate-limits, the request runs against the next in the list automatically
- Same response shape, same SDK, no changes to app code
- List order = priority order
- No extra fee for fallback routing
- Rate limits, 5xx errors, content refusals, and context-length errors all count as failures
- Can mix providers in the same chain (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google together)

[Source: OpenRouter Team newsletter, 2026-06-09](https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/provider-routing)

pages.title: Openrouter First Response 2026 06 04
pages.body:
# OpenRouter: 30 Seconds to Your First Response

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19e92f0ffcc95db9
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T03:50:00Z
**Provenance:** OpenRouter Team welcome email to info@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-04

OpenRouter onboarding email — fastest path from signup to a working API response.

## Quickstart

Two code blocks (Python SDK or curl). Swap API key, run.

**Python (OpenAI SDK):**
```python
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI(
    base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1",
    api_key="YOUR_API_KEY"
)
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="openai/gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Hello"}]
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)
```

**cURL:**
```bash
curl https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"model":"openai/gpt-4o-mini","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello"}]}'
```

## Error Codes

- **401** — almost always the API key header
- **402** — free-tier daily cap hit or credits needed for paid model
- **404** — model slug format: OpenRouter uses dots, so `claude-sonnet-4.6` not `claude-sonnet-4-6`

## Free Tier

10 requests per day across 29 free models. No credit card required.

## Auto Routing

`openrouter/auto` picks models per-request based on the prompt. ~63% of auto traffic routes to models under $1/M tokens — simple tasks stop hitting expensive models without any routing code.

pages.title: Openrouter May Recap 2026 06 04
pages.body:
# OpenRouter: What Shipped in May 2026

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19e94ddbf92825b4
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T04:30:00Z
**Provenance:** OpenRouter Team newsletter to info@malohacoast.com, 2026-06-04

Spend limits, prompt-injection blocking, voice in/out, and one answer from many models. Major May 2026 release recap.

## Platform Features

1. **Workspace Guardrails** — Centralized security and governance. Set per-member and per-key spend limits, lock traffic to model/provider allowlist, enforce zero data retention, block prompt injection (30+ OWASP patterns), redact PII. Layer rules into one guardrail or scope to specific keys/members.
2. **Speech and Transcription APIs** — Text-to-speech and speech-to-text through the same API key. Speech-to-text: Whisper, GPT-4o Mini Transcribe, Voxtral. Provider failover built in.
3. **Model Fusion** — Route one prompt to multiple models in parallel and synthesize responses into a single, higher-quality answer. Runs as API plugin, server tool, and in chatroom composer.
4. **Private Models (Enterprise)** — Route to custom, fine-tuned, or dedicated endpoints through standard completions/responses API.
5. **Pareto Code Router** — Set `min_coding_score` and route to the cheapest code-capable model clearing your bar.

## Also Shipped

- IP allowlist enforcement (403 on unauthorized requests)
- BYOK management API for provider keys
- Observability destinations API (Datadog, Langfuse, LangSmith)
- Per-provider ZDR toggles
- Copy guardrails across workspaces
- Model comparison page (up to 5 models side-by-side)
- Presets API with Anthropic Messages and Responses skins
- Human-in-the-loop tools (SDK tool type that pauses for human input)
- Session stickiness (same provider/model across turns for cache hits)
- Auto router `cost_quality_tradeoff` dial (0-10)

## New Models

20 launched in May: Claude Opus 4.8, Gemini 3.5 Flash, Grok 4.3, Qwen3.7 Max, Grok Imagine Video, Recraft V3/V4/V4 Pro, and more.

## Business

OpenRouter raised $113M Series B led by CapitalG. Crossed 100 trillion tokens/month.

[Source: OpenRouter Team newsletter, 2026-06-04](https://openrouter.ai/announcements/may-release-spotlight)

pages.title: OpenRouter Model Fusion Frontier Deep Research 2026 06 24
pages.body:
# Model Fusion shows frontier-level deep research performance at half the cost

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ef9f9bd5c77629
**Ingested:** 2026-06-24T22:15:00Z
**Provenance:** [[OpenRouter]] newsletter, June 2026

## Summary

[[OpenRouter]]'s June 2026 newsletter covering three major feature launches (Fusion multi-model deep research, Advisor/Subagent server tools, sharper Models API), six new models, and the 11-model battle royale benchmark analysis.

## Key Features

### Fusion: Multi-Model Deep Research
- Experimental multi-model research workflow
- Fans one API call across a panel of up to 8 models, returns synthesized best answer
- On deep-research benchmarks: lower cost than single frontier model with strong results
- Each panel model + judge runs with web search by default
- Judge maps agreement points, conflicts, unique catches, and gaps — not majority vote
- Call as `openrouter/fusion` model slug or let any model invoke it as server tool
- Best for research-heavy work, not coding agents or general chat

### Server Tools: Advisor and Subagent
- **Advisor**: model consults a stronger one only when stuck; now with multiple named advisors, memory across requests, streaming
- **Subagent**: hands self-contained subtasks to a smaller, faster worker mid-generation — expensive model doesn't do routine work
- Both push work onto cheaper models

### Sharper Models API
- Filter catalog by price, modality, context, provider
- Benchmark scores on every model page
- Pull single model's full details by author and slug

## New Models (since May recap)

| Model | Provider | Context | Pricing (in/out per M tokens) | Notes |
|-------|----------|---------|-------------------------------|-------|
| GLM 5.2 | Z.ai | 1M | $1.40 / $4.40 | Flagship reasoning, long-horizon agent work |
| Kimi K2.7 Code | Moonshot AI | 262K | $0.74 / $3.50 | Coding agent, long context without long-context pricing |
| Qwen3.7-Plus | Qwen | 1M | $0.32 / $1.28 | Generalist, cheap workhorse |
| Nemotron-3 Ultra 550B | NVIDIA | 1M | $0.50 / $2.20 | Open-weight 550B, free eval endpoint |
| Nemotron-3.5 Content Safety | NVIDIA | — | Free | Guardrail model, moderates inputs/responses |

## Key Insight: Battle Royale

11 models through 30 rounds. The cheapest model won the most games, and the model you'd actually ship was a different one. Benchmark rank and real-task fit come apart — a recurring theme that reinforces the importance of task-specific evaluation over leaderboard chasing.

## BYOK Pricing Update (effective July 1, 2026)
- Free tier changes from 1M requests/month → $25K of list-price inference/month
- 5% fee above free tier unchanged

pages.title: Openrouter Model Fusion Mid June 2026
pages.body:
# OpenRouter Mid-June 2026: Model Fusion, New Server Tools, Six New Models

**Source:** email from OpenRouter Team
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ef9f9bd5c77629
**Ingested:** 2026-06-25T14:30:00Z

## Overview

OpenRouter's mid-June 2026 newsletter covers three feature launches (Fusion, Advisor/Subagent server tools, improved models API) and six new models. Key theme: lowering cost per answer through multi-model orchestration.

## Fusion: Multi-Model Deep Research

An early experiment where one API call fans a prompt across a panel of models and synthesizes the best answer. On deep-research benchmarks, this approach produced strong results at lower cost than a single frontier model.

- Call it as `openrouter/fusion` model slug, or let any model invoke it as a server tool
- Every panel model and the judge run with web search by default
- Choose up to eight models and the judge that synthesizes them
- The judge maps where models agree, where they conflict, what only one caught, what all missed — not a majority vote
- Built for research-heavy work, not coding agents or general chat (experimental)

Usage example:
```
{ "model": "openrouter/fusion",
  "messages": [{ "role": "user", "content": "What changed in EU AI Act enforcement this year?" }],
  "plugins": [{ "id": "fusion", "preset": "general-budget" }] }
```

## Server Tools: Advisor and Subagent

Two tools that push work onto cheaper models:

- **Advisor** — lets your model consult a stronger one only when stuck, now with multiple named advisors, memory across requests, and streaming
- **Subagent** — hands self-contained subtasks to a smaller, faster worker model mid-generation, so the expensive model is not doing routine work

## Sharper Models API

Find models by price, modality, context, and provider. Benchmark scores on every model page. Pull a single model's full details by author and slug.

## New Models

- **GLM 5.2** (z-ai/glm-5.2) — Z.ai's flagship reasoning model with 1M-token context window, $1.40 in / $4.40 out per M tokens
- **Kimi K2.7 Code** (moonshotai/kimi-k2.7-code) — Moonshot AI's coding model, 262K context, $0.74 in / $3.50 out
- **Qwen 3.7 Plus** (qwen/qwen3.7-plus) — 1M-context generalist at $0.32 in / $1.28 out
- **NVIDIA Nemotron-3 Ultra 550B** (nvidia/nemotron-3-ultra-550b-a55b) — 550B open-weight, $0.50 in / $2.20 out, 1M context, free eval endpoint
- **NVIDIA Nemotron-3.5 Content Safety** (nvidia/nemotron-3.5-content-safety:free) — compact guardrail model, free to run
- Additional models mentioned in full catalog

## Notable Blog Posts

- **Battle Royale** — Eleven models in 30 rounds. Cheapest model won most games; the model you'd actually ship was different. Benchmark rank vs real-task fit come apart.
- **Dinner is Served** — Case against standardizing on one LLM; "going family style" with multi-model orchestration
- **Model Routing Diagram** — How OpenRouter spreads requests across 60+ providers: default load balancing, :nitro/:floor shortcuts, model fallbacks, Auto Router

## BYOK Pricing Update (Effective July 1, 2026)

Free tier changes from 1M requests/month to $25,000 of list-price inference per month. 5% fee above free tier unchanged.

## Key Takeaways for Maloha Coast

- Fusion's multi-model approach aligns with the multi-agent orchestration direction
- Advisor and Subagent server tools are directly applicable to agent cost optimization
- GLM 5.2 (1M context, $1.40/M in) and Qwen 3.7 Plus ($0.32/M in) are worth evaluating for long-context agent workloads
- The battle royale conclusion — benchmark rank != task fit — reinforces the need for real-world agent evaluation

pages.title: Openrouter Paid Key Features 2026 06 11
pages.body:
# What comes with a paid key

**Source:** email from OpenRouter Team
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eb701d76b2923f
**Ingested:** 2026-06-21T03:15:00Z

A paid OpenRouter key gives access to 300+ models from 60+ providers, including every frontier model (Claude, GPT, Gemini, Llama). Same API, same code, same key.

## Features

- **Provider fallback** — When a provider goes down, the request re-routes to another provider serving the same model. No retry logic needed.
- **Prompt caching** — Repeated context gets cached on supported models. Cuts repeated-context cost by up to 90%.
- **Model variants** — Append `:nitro` for speed, `:floor` for the cheapest provider, or `:exacto` for precision routing. One string change.
- **Dedicated rate limits** — Paid keys get their own limits; other users' traffic never touches yours.
- **No daily request cap** — Free tier: 10 requests/day. Paid keys: no ceiling.
- **No provider markup** — Pay the per-token rate each provider sets.
- **Activity dashboard** — Shows every call with model, tokens, and cost in real time.
- **openrouter/auto** — Selects the best model for each request from 26+ options. For minimal spend without micro-managing models.

No minimum purchase. Credits expire after one year.

# How provider data policies work on OpenRouter

**Source:** direct email from OpenRouter Team
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee03492356403f
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T08:44:20Z
**Provenance:** Direct email to info@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-19

OpenRouter - How provider data policies work on OpenRouter

Hi there,

Every provider behind OpenRouter has its own retention and training policy. OpenRouter reflects those policies as structured data on each endpoint, publishes the full table in the docs, and gives you controls to filter routing on them.

WHAT YOU CONTROL

1. Block routing to providers that train. Your privacy settings have an account-wide toggle for whether routing may use providers that train on prompts, with separate switches for paid and free models. If you opt out, OpenRouter will not route to training providers.

2. Require a specific data policy per request. You can override your account default on any single call.

3. Pick only zero-retention providers. Many providers on OpenRouter are zero retention: Amazon Bedrock, Azure, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, Together, and others.

IF YOU NEED STRICTER GUARANTEES

OpenRouter supports EU in-region routing for enterprise accounts. When enabled, prompts and completions are processed inside the European Union via the eu.openrouter.ai base URL. Contact the enterprise team to enable it.

One API key, the same privacy controls across 300+ models.

## Topic Tags

openrouter, privacy, data-policy, llm-providers, zero-retention, provider-routing

pages.title: Three levers that lower your per-token cost
pages.body:
# Three levers that lower your per-token cost

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ed5e7b3a86e916
**Ingested:** 2026-06-17
**Provenance:** OpenRouter onboarding email to info@malohacoast.com

## Summary

OpenRouter onboarding newsletter covering three levers that lower your per-token cost.

## Key Content

Tiered pricing, provider preferences, and prompt caching. All per-request, no extra wiring.  

OpenRouter
OpenRouter (  )

 

Hi there,

Three things quietly cut your per-token cost on OpenRouter. They stack, and none of them require changing models or rewriting your app.

Compare model pricing (  )

 

1. TIERED PRICING

Most models run on tiered pricing set by the provider: the more tokens you send through, the less each token costs. You do not opt in. The discount applies automatically once you cross the threshold. Open any model page on openrouter.ai/models (  ) and click the Effective Pricing tab to see the live per-provider rate, including any tiers you have already hit. Consolidating traffic onto one or two models unlocks the next tier faster than scattering tokens across five.

2. PROVIDER PREFERENCES

The same model is served by multiple providers at different rates. provider in the request body lets you prefer cheaper providers, sort by price, or set a price ceiling. It is a per-request setting, so you can apply it selectively to traffic where latency matters less than cost. Docs: Provider Routing (  ).

3. PROMPT CACHING

On supported models, OpenRouter reuses cached prompt prefixes across requests, so repeated context costs a fraction of the first call. Useful for long system prompts, RAG, agents that replay history, and anything that ships the same context every turn. Per-request, no extra wiring. Docs: Prompt Caching (  ).

# Three features worth trying next — OpenRouter

**From:** OpenRouter Team <welcome@openrouter.ai>

## Summary

Three OpenRouter features accessible via single parameter changes, no new integration needed.

### 1. Streaming
`stream=True` — tokens arrive as they generate. Same endpoint, same auth. Perceived latency drops from seconds to first token. Agents that stream partial results to frontend get a noticeable UX lift.

### 2. Structured Outputs
Pass a JSON schema via `response_format`. OpenRouter enforces it at the API level. No regex parsing, no retry loops for malformed JSON, no "please respond in valid JSON" prompt engineering. Works on every model that supports the format. Router respects it with `openrouter/auto`.

### 3. Provider Preferences
Pin specific providers, exclude others, set latency floors and throughput minimums. Via `extra_body={"provider": {"sort": "price"}}` or allowlists to lock routing to trusted providers.

### All Three At Once
```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="openrouter/auto",
    stream=True,
    response_format={"type": "json_schema", "json_schema": schema},
    extra_body={"provider": {"sort": "price"}},
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "..."}],
)
```

- openrouter.ai/models — browsing and pricing
- openrouter.ai/rankings — real usage volume data

**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19edb0e0abd83fb0

## Summary

Three [[OpenRouter]] features that are each a single parameter change, don't require a new integration or endpoint, and each earns its place in production code.

## Key Features

### 1. Streaming

Set `stream=True` and tokens arrive as they generate. Same endpoint, same auth, one parameter. Perceived latency drops from seconds to first-token time — the difference between a chat UI that feels alive and one that feels broken. Agents that stream partial results to a frontend get a noticeable UX lift for zero code cost.

### 2. Structured Outputs

Pass a JSON schema via `response_format` and [[OpenRouter]] enforces it at the API level. No regex parsing, no retry loops when the model returns malformed JSON, no "please respond in valid JSON" prompt engineering. Works on every model that supports the parameter.

### 3. Provider Preferences

Use the `provider` parameter to control which providers handle a request: set `ignore=`` to skip specific providers, set `order` to prioritize by latency or cost, or set `allow_fallbacks` to keep the request reliable even when preferred providers are down.

pages.title: Three features that quietly save you money
pages.body:
# Three features that quietly save you money

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ebc285fe5e7458
**Ingested:** 2026-06-12
**Provenance:** OpenRouter onboarding email to info@malohacoast.com

## Summary

OpenRouter onboarding newsletter covering three features that quietly save you money.

## Key Content

Prompt caching cuts repeat tokens by up to 90%. Fallback keeps you up. Healing fixes broken JSON.  

OpenRouter
OpenRouter (  )

 

Hi there,

Three OpenRouter features quietly do work for you in production. They are off by default on most platforms and on or one-flag-away here. Each one pays for itself within the first week on any workload that hits real traffic.

View the docs (  )

 

PROMPT CACHING

If you send the same system prompt or context window repeatedly, OpenRouter caches the prefix and bills cached tokens at up to 90 percent off. It runs automatically on supported models with no setup. The savings show up in your usage report. Agents with long system prompts, RAG pipelines that reuse context, and multi-turn chat all benefit here without any code change.

 

PROVIDER FALLBACK

Pass a list of models with route="fallback" and OpenRouter retries the next one in the list when your primary fails. Same response shape, same cost as whichever model serves the request. Provider outages stop cascading to your app, and the only thing you added was one extra array entry.

 

RESPONSE HEALING

When a model returns malformed JSON, OpenRouter repairs it before sending the response back to your app. Enable it under Settings > Plugins (  ). Useful for any pipeline that parses model output downstream, which in practice is most production workloads.

 

client.chat.completions.create
(   model="openrouter/auto",
  extra_body={
    "models": ["openrouter/auto", "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6"],
    "route": "fallback"
  },
  messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "..."}] )


 

Auto-routing with a named fallback. The production pattern.

Why these three compound. Auto-routing picks a cheap model when the prompt does not need a frontier one. Prompt caching drops the per-call cost of whatever the router picks on repeat traffic. Provider fallback keeps you up when a backend misbehaves. Layered together, the cost curve stays flat as traffic grows and the uptime profile looks nothing like single-provider integration.

The next email walks through structured outputs: how to get valid JSON from any model on the catalog with one parameter.

pages.title: Secret Architecture Behind Ai Data Centers 2026 06 20
pages.body:
# The Secret Architecture Behind AI Data Centers

**Source:** System Design Newsletter (Neo Kim & Ashish Prajapati)
**Published:** Jun 19, 2026
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T07:35:22+08:00

#155: Why AI breakthroughs are really infrastructure breakthroughs

*Guest author: Ashish Prajapati — AI infrastructure specialist, technology educator. Training programs have reached 39,000+ learners. Free mentoring initiative "Become a Solutions Architect (BeSA)" has helped 23,000+ professionals. NVIDIA-Certified Associate: AI Infrastructure and Operations (NCA-AIIO) bootcamp instructor.*

## Mental Model: AI Data Center as a Factory
- AI Data Center = Factory
- Data = Raw material
- GPUs = Machines on the factory floor
- Network = Internal transport system
- Storage = Warehouse holding raw materials
- Software = Assembly line logic
- Output = Trained intelligence

Remove any one ingredient, and the entire system stalls.

## First Problem: Physics

### 1. Power
A single GPU server rack draws 80-120 kW — roughly equivalent to 40 homes at full load. Limited by how much electricity the facility can physically receive and distribute.

### 2. Cooling
More power = more heat. Without removal: hardware fails, performance throttles, systems shut down. Modern data centers use **liquid cooling** — coolant circulated through cold plates attached directly to GPUs. Enables higher rack densities, lower cooling power consumption, consistent performance.

### 3. Space
Unlimited chips and power still need racks and floor space. Scaling AI is real estate, energy infrastructure, and thermodynamics combined.

Takeaway: A data center is a power plant combined with a massive, precisely controlled air-conditioning system.

## Compute: Why GPUs Took Over
- **CPU** = manager (decision-making) — a few powerful cores, handles complex sequential tasks
- **GPU** = factory workers (mass production) — thousands of smaller cores, designed for parallel matrix multiplications
- **DPU** (Data Processing Unit) = logistics and operations — handles networking, storage I/O, security so CPU and GPU aren't interrupted

## Hidden Bottleneck: Memory and Storage
GPUs don't fail because they're slow. They fail because they're hungry — constantly starved for data.

Memory hierarchy: GPU Memory (HBM — fastest, tiny, tens of GB) → CPU RAM (hundreds of GB, slower) → SSD/NVMe (enormous) → Network/Object Storage (nearly unlimited, slowest).

**AI performance is determined by how fast data can be fed into the GPU, not by how fast the GPU computes.**

Tiered storage: hot (NVMe SSDs for active datasets/checkpoints), warm (parallel file systems like Lustre/GPFS for shared access), cold (object storage like S3 for raw datasets).

## Networking: Hidden Bottleneck
When training large models with thousands of GPUs, they must constantly share what they've learned. Every second a GPU waits for data or synchronization is wasted investment.

- **Leaf-spine architecture**: every server can reach every other server in at most two hops
- **NVLink**: intra-node fabric, fastest path, direct GPU-to-GPU within a server
- **InfiniBand/RoCE**: inter-node fabric, "bullet train" between servers
- **Ethernet**: general-purpose, not designed for AI's extreme bandwidth/latency demands

**Amdahl's Law**: parts of the system must wait — adding more workers eventually stops helping. More GPUs = more computing power but also more communication overhead.

Smarter data movement: RDMA (low-latency direct memory transfers between servers), RDMA kernel bypass, GPUDirect (GPUs read/write to storage/network bypassing CPU), zero-copy pipelines.

Goal: make thousands of GPUs behave like one giant, unified processor.
