   is your primary tool

------------------------------

New Video:
The Skill That 10x'd My Claude Code Projects[image: The Skill That 10x'd My
Claude Code Projects]

If your Claude outputs feel generic — technically correct but missing your
voice — the problem is not the model. Everyone using Claude Opus 4.8 starts
from the same foundation. The difference between outputs that sound like
you and ones that sound like everyone else is context.

The Grill Me skill fixes that. Instead of a five-minute brain dump before
you start building, it interviews you until it actually understands how you
think. It asks one question at a time, checkpoints your answers to a
markdown file in your project, and keeps going until there are no gaps. By
the end, you have reusable context your AI pulls from every time you touch
that skill or project.

*The mistake most people make:* Starting with a brain dump and calling it
good enough. Five minutes of info-dumping gets your output quality to about
70% on iteration one. The Grill Me approach gets you to 90% from the start
— fewer revision cycles, faster time to a deliverable clients actually like.

*How this helps you:* When Claude knows your voice, your decisions, and
your process, it stops generating generic output. Your projects feel like
yours. Clients notice. You spend less time fixing what the AI got wrong.
Watch and Discuss
------------------------------

Winning Is All We Do
Off the sidelines — Carson landed his first automation client[image: Carson
Bates win]

*What happened:* Carson built a lead automation for a local aesthetics
clinic as a free pilot. The workflow routes contact data and handles
follow-ups automatically. He's waiting on Facebook credentials to go live,
but the clinic owner is already highly receptive. His plan: deliver the
pilot, then position for a paid monthly retainer.

*The opportunity for you:* Local service businesses (clinics, salons,
studios) are some of the easiest first clients to land. They have real lead
problems, a low technology bar, and if you deliver, they talk to other
business owners. A free pilot with a clear upsell path is one of the
cleanest ways to get your first paying automation client.

Stories like this happen every week inside AIS+. AIS Live (July 11-12) is
where you see exactly how: practitioners walking through real projects on
stage, from first outreach to signed invoice. Early bird GA tickets are $97
through June 7. That price goes up after this weekend.
Get Your Ticket
------------------------------

In the News
Anthropic filed for IPO at $965 billion

*The news:* On June 1, Anthropic — the company behind Claude —
confidentially submitted an S-1 to the SEC for a proposed initial public
offering. Valuation: $965 billion, putting it ahead of OpenAI ($852 billion
after their last funding round). Anthropic joins SpaceX and OpenAI in what
analysts are already calling a race to go public — all three expected to
list this year.

*How it affects automation:* When your primary AI tool's parent company
goes public, two things typically follow: more capital to build better
models (good for you), and quarterly earnings pressure that tends to push
enterprise-first roadmaps and price adjustments upward. This is not a
reason to panic. It is a reason to build your skills and client base now,
before the landscape shifts again. The practitioners who get in early
always have the advantage.

pages.title: Three Features Three Claudes 2026 06 11
pages.body:
# Three Features. Three Claudes. One Afternoon.

**Source:** JS Mastery (Adrian), forwarded by Sayf
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eb960b3c2b3ec2
**Ingested:** 2026-06-24T04:30:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf on 2026-06-12

## Parallel Agent Development with Git Worktrees

Adrian ran three Claude Code sessions in parallel, all working on the same project simultaneously:
- One building a dark/light mode toggle
- One writing API endpoint tests
- One adding code quality tooling

None of them knew the others existed.

**The setup:** Git worktrees — check out multiple branches of the same project into separate directories and work on them independently. Each directory gets its own Claude session, its own context window, its own isolated set of files to modify.

**What it solves:** Two agents writing to the same file at the same time. With worktrees, that can't happen — they're operating in completely separate environments.

## The Merge

When all three were done:
1. Each branch was committed with a descriptive message (Claude wrote those)
2. A fourth Claude session pulled everything together and resolved conflicts
3. Found one conflict (pyproject.toml modified by two branches) — resolved in ~30 seconds

## The Real Insight

It wasn't the speed that stuck with Adrian — it was that he spent the afternoon thinking about architecture, what each feature needed to know, how the pieces would fit together after the merge. Not implementing. Directing.

"That's the shift I keep coming back to."

pages.title: Three Features Three Claudes One Afternoon
pages.body:
# Three Features. Three Claudes. One Afternoon.

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eb960b3c2b3ec2
**Ingested:** 2026-06-22T19:05:00Z
**For:** sayf


newsletter shared

---------- Forwarded message ---------
From: Adrian | JS Mastery <contact@jsmastery.pro>
Date: Thu, 11 Jun 2026 at 23:58
Subject: Three Features. Three Claudes. One Afternoon.
To: <sayfcodes@gmail.com>


I ran three agents in parallel...Then merged everything.
                 
                 
                
                 
                
                 
                
                 
                
                 
    
February 5th is in my diary. I'm looking at three terminal windows, all
running Claude Code simultaneously, all working on the same project.

One is building a dark/light mode toggle. One is writing API endpoint
tests. One is adding code quality tooling. None of them knows the others
exist.

I watched all three work at the same time and remember thinking, "This is
genuinely new." This isn't a faster way to do what I was already doing.
This is a different thing entirely.

Here's the setup. Git has a feature called worktrees, you to check out
multiple branches of the same project into separate directories and work on
them independently. Each directory gets its own Claude session, its own
context window, its own isolated set of files to modify.

What this solves is the thing that makes parallel AI development feel
risky: two agents writing to the same file at the same time. With
worktrees, that can't happen.

They're operating in completely separate environments.

When all three were done, I committed each branch with a descriptive
message. Claude wrote those too, which matters more than it sounds when
you're about to merge three sets of changes, and then asked a fourth Claude
session to pull everything together and resolve any conflicts.

It found one. A pyproject.toml that two branches had both modified.
Resolved it in about thirty seconds.

The whole thing, three features built simultaneously, merged, tested, took
an afternoon. Features that would have been sequential, each requiring
their own setup, their own context, their own careful hand-holding through
implementation.

But here's what actually stuck with me. It wasn't the speed. It was that I
spent that afternoon thinking, about architecture, about what each feature
needed to know, about how the pieces would fit together after the merge.
Not implementing. Directing.

That's the shift I keep coming back to.

Have you ever tried running more than one AI session in parallel on the
same project? Did it work, or did it create more problems than it solved?

Talk soon,
Adrian
JSM

P.S. Next email, I want to talk about something I noticed when I spoke to a
business owner with 30 developers on his team.

Almost none of them were using AI seriously. What he said next is the
reason this course needs an enterprise angle, and why the timing right now
is unlike anything we've seen before.
You are receiving this email because you opted in to receive email from the
list AI course waitlist from JS Mastery

**Ingested:** 2026-06-23T00:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by sayf on 2026-06-10

[[System Design One]] #148 by [[Neo Kim]] and [[Ayaan]] (guest author). Part 1 of the "DevOps Mastermind" series.

A thorough architectural deep dive into virtual machine internals, from emulation vs virtualization to hypervisor internals.

## Key Concepts

### Emulation vs Virtualization
- **Emulation:** Software pretends to be completely different hardware. Every instruction translated. Slow but flexible (retro gaming emulators).
- **Virtualization:** Creates a version of the same hardware the machine already has. Most instructions run directly on the real processor. Near-native speed.
- Key analogy: Emulation = translator for every sentence. Virtualization = both people speak the same language.

### What a Virtual Machine Actually Is
- A complete computer abstraction built on top of a physical machine.
- Presented with CPU, memory, storage, network interface — the guest OS has no awareness it's sharing resources.
- Three-layer design: Physical hardware → Hypervisor → Guest OS.

### How the Hypervisor Works
- Controls access to CPU, memory, storage, and networking.
- Every request from the guest OS goes through the hypervisor.
- Ensures isolation between VMs while sharing physical resources.

### Key Capabilities
- **Live migration** — moving a running VM between physical hosts without downtime.
- **Resource controls** — CPU pinning, memory limits, disk quotas.
- **Isolation** — one VM's crash doesn't affect others.

### Practical Context
- Cloud platforms (AWS, Azure, GCP) run apps inside VMs.
- Cloud infrastructure is built on this foundation.
- Explains the difference between VMs and containers (deferred to later parts).

## DevOps Mastermind Series

This kicks off a new series covering:
- Architecture breakdown of core DevOps systems
- Deep dives into Docker, Kubernetes, Terraform
- How real-world systems achieve scalability, automation, and reliability

## Guest Author

[[Ayaan]] — self-taught DevOps engineer focused on Kubernetes & cloud infrastructure. Builds in public, writes about cloud-native systems, shares daily technical tips and projects.

## Relevance

Foundational knowledge for cloud infrastructure understanding. Directly relevant to VPS management, container orchestration, and understanding how Hermes itself runs on cloud infrastructure.

The annual goal trap A year is too long to create urgency and too vague to act on. "Get a new job this year" feels fine in January. There's time. So nothing happens. By June, the goal is still technically alive, but you haven't done anything that moves it forward. By December, you're setting the same goal again for next year. I went through this myself but with weight, not career. In January 2025, I set an annual goal: lose 6kg by December. Standard SMART goal. Looked fine on paper. pages.title: Zaim Career Goals 2026 06 16
pages.body:
# Why Your 2026 Career Goals Are Already Dead

**Source:** email
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ece778ab23e039
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T03:50:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded by Zaim Mohzani via info+zaim@malohacoast.com on 2026-06-16

> Forwarded by [[Zaim Mohzani]] from Nation Building School newsletter.

It's June. How's that career goal from January going?

If you're being honest, probably: forgotten by March, barely remembered (and feeling guilty) in June and quietly dropped by the end of the year.

It's a timeframe problem, not a discipline problem.

## The Annual Goal Trap

A year is too long to create urgency and too vague to act on. "Get a new job this year" feels fine in January. There's time. So nothing happens. By June, the goal is still technically alive, but you haven't done anything that moves it forward. By December, you're setting the same goal again for next year.

[[Zaim Mohzani]] went through this with weight, not career. In January 2025, set an annual goal: lose 6kg by December. Standard SMART goal. Then came across Brian Moran and Michael Lennington's book "The 12 Week Year," which argues most people don't actually operate in years — they operate in the last few weeks before a deadline. The fix is to shrink the deadline.

Rewrote the goal: lose 6kg in 90 days, by 31 March. Hit it. The habits built in those 90 days kept running on their own for the rest of the year.

## Why This Works for Your Career Too

The same applies to a job search, a promotion case or a pivot. "Get promoted this year" doesn't tell you what to do on Monday. "By 30 September, I will have had 3 conversations with people in my target department, applied to 5 internal roles, and presented one project to leadership" tells you exactly what to do on Monday. One is a wish. The other is a plan.

Zaim has worked with clients on exactly this. Those who set a 90-day goal like "land 3 offers from MNCs by end of Q3" with weekly actions attached mostly hit it or get close. Those with "find a better job this year" mostly don't, because there's no Monday task.

## How to Set Yours

Pick ONE career goal for this quarter. Just one. Follow this format:

- My 90-day career goal is...
- This matters to me because...
- To complete this, I commit that by 30 September 2026, I will have... (make it checkable — "3 offers" is checkable; "more clarity" is not)
- To make sure I complete it, I'm going to... (weekly actions, not the outcome)

Write it down. One goal. One sentence you can check off or not, no grey area. Then build it into your week, not your year.

**P.S.** Zaim is running a free workshop on setting this 90-day goal for Q3 2026. Early next month. Sign up: https://t.ly/getclearoncareer6

## Key Ideas

- The annual goal trap: a year is too long to create urgency and too vague to act on
- Shrink the deadline: 90-day goals with checkable outcomes create Monday-level actions
- Habits outlast goals — the 90-day sprint builds systems that persist
- Format: one goal, checkable outcome, weekly actions

## People Mentioned

- [[Zaim Mohzani]] — career coach, Nation Building School
- Brian Moran — co-author, "The 12 Week Year"
- Michael Lennington — co-author, "The 12 Week Year"

pages.title: How provider data policies work on OpenRouter
pages.body:
# How provider data policies work on OpenRouter

**Source:** email (OpenRouter Team)
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee03492356403f
**Ingested:** 2026-06-19T22:07:00Z
**Provenance:** OpenRouter onboarding email to info@malohacoast.com

> Every provider behind OpenRouter has its own retention and training policy. OpenRouter reflects those policies as structured data on each endpoint, publishes the full table in the docs, and gives you controls to filter routing on them.

## What You Control

1. **Block routing to providers that train** — account-wide toggle for whether routing may use providers that train on prompts, with separate switches for paid and free models. If you opt out, OpenRouter will not route to training providers.

2. **Require a specific data policy per request** — override account default on any single call.

3. **Pick only zero-retention providers** — Many providers on OpenRouter are zero retention: Amazon Bedrock, Azure, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, Together, and others.

## Stricter Guarantees

OpenRouter supports EU in-region routing for enterprise accounts. When enabled, prompts and completions are processed inside the European Union via `https://eu.openrouter.ai`.

## Key Takeaway

One API key, the same privacy controls across 300+ models. Useful reference for sayf's Hermes setup — OpenRouter already has the infrastructure for per-provider data policy filtering.

pages.title: How Provider Data Policies Work on OpenRouter
pages.body:
# How Provider Data Policies Work on OpenRouter

**Source:** Email from OpenRouter Team, 2026-06-19
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19ee03492356403f
**Ingested:** 2026-06-19T22:14:00Z

OpenRouter onboarding email explaining each provider's data retention and training policy, and the controls available to users.

## Key Points

- Every provider behind OpenRouter has its own retention and training policy. These are reflected as structured data on each endpoint and published in the docs.
- Users have three levels of control:
  1. **Account-wide toggle** — block routing to providers that train on prompts, with separate switches for paid and free models.
  2. **Per-request override** — require a specific data policy on any single API call.
  3. **Zero-retention filtering** — filter to providers that keep no data.

- Zero-retention providers include: Amazon Bedrock, Azure, Cerebras, DeepInfra, Google Vertex, Groq, NVIDIA, SambaNova, Together, and others.

- **EU in-region routing** — available for enterprise accounts. Prompts and completions processed inside the EU via `https://eu.openrouter.ai` base URL.

pages.title: Zaim Mohzani
pages.body:
# Zaim Mohzani

> **Role:** AI & Career Strategist | ASEAN Policy Specialist | Senior Director at KSI
> **Location:** Federal Territory of Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia
> **LinkedIn:** https://my.linkedin.com/in/zaimmohzani
> **Website:** www.zaim.my
> **1st-degree connection with sayf** [Source: User, LinkedIn, 2026-06-09]

---

## Compilation of Facts (Compiled Truth)

Zaim Mohzani is a Malaysian career strategist, ASEAN policy specialist, and social entrepreneur. He helps mid-career professionals change careers without starting over, using a lifestyle-centric career design approach. He has directly helped 2,600+ professionals in Malaysia since 2015. [Source: LinkedIn profile, 2026-06-09]

He is Malaysia's first certified Designing Your Life (DYL) coach, trained by Bill Burnett and Dave Evans at Stanford University. [Source: LinkedIn profile, 2026-06-09]

### Core Philosophy: Lifestyle-Centric Career Design

Zaim's approach rejects the "follow your passion" advice. Instead:
- Start with the lifestyle you actually want
- Work backwards to careers that support it
- Build skills with market value
- Make moves based on reality, not feelings

His key thesis: **Passion is a by-product of doing good work, not a prerequisite.** [Source: LinkedIn About section, 2026-06-09]

### Personal Story

At 25, Zaim had strong credentials but was burnt out — hundreds of applications, zero offers. He was told "you just haven't found your passion yet." That advice kept him paralysed. He pivoted through five industries and at 28 became chairman of a Malaysian government agency — not because he found his calling, but because he stopped chasing passion and started designing for the life he wanted. [Source: LinkedIn About section, 2026-06-09]

---

## Current Roles (as of June 2026)

| Role | Organization | Since | Type |
|------|-------------|-------|------|
| AI & Career Strategist | [[Nation Building School]] | Jan 2024 | Full-time |
| Principal | Nation Building School | Aug 2015 - Dec 2023 | Previous role |
| Senior Director of External Relations | [[KSI Strategic Institute for Asia Pacific]] | Jan 2024 | Full-time |
| Director of External Relations | KSI Strategic Institute for Asia Pacific | Nov 2020 - Dec 2023 | Previous role |
| Co-Chair | [[Inclusive AI Association]] (IAIA) | Aug 2025 | Volunteer |
| Advisor | Asia House (London) | Oct 2020 - Feb 2023 | Seasonal |

[Source: LinkedIn profile, 2026-06-09]

---

## Education

- **MSc in International Strategy & Diplomacy (Distinction)** — London School of Economics and Political Science (LSE), 2019-2020. Chevening Scholar. Dissertation: "Disrupted by AI? Impact of AI on the U.S-China Arms Dynamics."
- **BA in Political Science and Government** — Monash University, 2010-2013

[Source: LinkedIn profile, 2026-06-09]

---

## Key Certifications

- **Designing Your Life Certified Coach** — Stanford University (via DYL), issued Jul 2023
- **AI For Good** — AI Singapore, issued Sep 2025
- AI Trainer (UK), ChatGPT Prompt Specialist (Singapore), ChatGPT Expert (India)

[Source: LinkedIn profile, 2026-06-09]

---

## Major Awards & Recognitions

- Best Perdana Fellow Award (2023)
- Dalai Lama Fellows (2022)
- Youth Advisor to Prime Minister of Malaysia (PM2PM, 2021)
- British Council Future Leaders Connect (2021)
- Acumen Academy Fellowship Malaysia (2021)
- Queen Elizabeth Prize for Engineering Ambassador (2020)
- Founding Chairman, Impact Malaysia / Impact Integrated (2018)
- The Edge Inspiring Young Leaders Awards (2019)
- U.S. Department of State YSEALI Professional Fellowship — ACYPL (2018)
- ASEAN Youth Fellowship (2018)
- Associate Fellowship, Royal Commonwealth Society (2017)
- NGO/Youth Advisor — ACTYVATE/Blue Ocean Youth Movement, Ministry of Finance Malaysia (2017)
- Advisory Chair, AIESEC in Malaysia (2017)
- Community Ambassador, Impact Hub Malaysia (2016)
- Selected for World Economic Forum Global Shapers
- Asia Society Asia 21 Next Generation Fellow
- New York Southeast Asia Network (NYSEAN) member

[Source: LinkedIn profile + KSI speaker page + web search, 2026-06-09]

---

## Organizations Founded / Led

### [[Nation Building School]] (est. Aug 2015)
Social enterprise started as a bootstrapped NGO in Bukit Tunku, now empowering next-generation leaders. Has engaged over 10,000 youths in Malaysia and Southeast Asia. Focus: career workshops and leadership programmes for urban poor, single mothers, and youth at risk. During the pandemic, launched virtual career workshops. [Source: LinkedIn profile, 2026-06-09]

### Impact Integrated (est. Dec 2018)
Founding Chairman. Government agency under Malaysian Ministry of Youth and Sports. Oversaw transformation from "reputationally controversial" to sustainable and impactful. Strengthened corporate governance. Notable project: #BeliaBerkeja (Youth Working) Virtual Career Fair. Engaged 10,000+ youths. Served until Feb 2023. [Source: LinkedIn profile + KSI speaker page, 2026-06-09]

### KSI Visiting Fellowship
Founded the KSI Visiting Fellowship programme, bringing in 10+ regional experts to strengthen policy and research at KSI. [Source: LinkedIn profile, 2026-06-09]

### KSI Academy
Leads KSI Academy, delivering AI training programmes for corporations across ASEAN. [Source: LinkedIn profile, 2026-06-09]

### KSInsights
Built KSInsights into a regional platform, publishing 30+ articles on ASEAN, technology, and geopolitics. [Source: LinkedIn profile, 2026-06-09]

### [[Inclusive AI Association]] (IAIA)
Co-Chair since Aug 2025. Community-based NGO dedicated to making Malaysians AI-Literate (MAIL) by 2030. Focus areas:
- **Inform:** Public awareness campaigns and simple AI explainers
- **Contribute:** Platforms for citizen feedback on AI policy

pages.title: Cold Lead Response Conference Events
pages.body:
**Context:** Response to a conference producer who asked for examples after a cold outreach. Crafted for LinkedIn DM — punchy, concrete, pilot-friendly.

## The Response

Thanks for asking. Here are some practical ways I think a Hermes agent could fit into your events workflow if we set one up on your laptop or VPS:

**Speaker sourcing:** You feed it your target criteria once, and it scans hundreds of profiles, past talks, and decks. It comes back with a shortlist plus solid briefing notes in about 15 minutes instead of 3–4 hours of manual digging.

**Post-session repurposing:** Right after a talk, you drop in the recording or transcript and it generates a full LinkedIn article, 3-5 social posts, a newsletter summary, and key highlights. Turns one session into 30+ usable pieces.

**Sponsor deliverables:** It pulls from your existing docs and data to create pre-event copy, staff briefs, lead reports, and post-event ROI summaries — cutting out a big chunk of the 20 hours per sponsor tier your team usually spends.

**Business brain / company memory:** I'm actually building one of these for myself right now. We can set up a "gbrain" where the agent learns your past events, speakers, sponsors, what worked and what didn't. Then you can just ask it things like "What sponsors performed best last year?" or "Give me the checklist for a 500-person event" and it pulls from your own history.

**Closer:** Which one sounds like it would save you the most time or headache right now? Happy to walk through any of them in more detail or hop on a quick call to map it to how you currently work.

## Notes

- Crafted to be realistic, specific, and pilot-friendly
- No AI jargon — pitched as a tool they control
- Each example maps to a known pain point for conference producers
- The gbrain example doubles as a subtle demo of the product in use

# The Maloha Coast Navigator — Test Edition

**Source:** email forward
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eca4c3e7e0cefc

**Provenance:** Self-BCC to sayf's inbox

**The Maloha Coast Navigator**  
*Your weekly guide to AI strategy for SMBs*

---

## This Week: Why Most AI Pilots Fail

80% of AI proofs-of-concept never reach production. Here's the pattern:

1. **Scope creep** — POCs that try to boil the ocean
2. **Data rot** — Models trained on stale data that's 6 months old
3. **No owner** — AI sits in IT without a business sponsor

### The Fix

Start with a **single workflow**, not a platform. Pick the one manual process your team hates most. Automate that. Measure the time saved. Then expand.

---

## Client Spotlight

A 12-person accounting firm deployed an email triage agent. Result: 14 hours/week saved. ROI in week one.

---

## What We're Reading

- [[Second Brain Architecture]] — How we structure knowledge for SMBs

---

## Upcoming

Next week: Pricing models that work for SMB AI consulting.

*— Sayf, Maloha Coast*

pages.title: Vps Hardening
pages.body:
# VPS Hardening

## Overview
Security hardening of the Maloha Coast production VPS (Ubuntu, 6.8.0-124-generic). All changes applied and verified post-reboot.

## Changes Applied

### SSH Hardening
| Setting | Before | After |
|---------|--------|-------|
| PermitRootLogin | yes | prohibit-password |
| X11Forwarding | yes | no |
| MaxAuthTries | 6 (default) | 3 |
| ClientAliveInterval | none | 300s (5 min) |
| ClientAliveCountMax | 3 (default) | 2 |
| AllowUsers | any local user | ubuntu only |
| DSA host keys | present | removed |
| PasswordAuthentication | yes | no |

### Firewall (UFW)
- **Default**: deny all incoming, allow all outgoing
- **Open ports**: 22/tcp (SSH) only
- Status: active

### fail2ban
- **Jail**: sshd (aggressive mode)
- **Max retries**: 3
- **Ban time**: 1 hour
- **Post-reboot**: banned 4 IPs within 2 minutes of coming online
- See [[2026-06-03#VPS Hardening]] for timeline

### System Updates
- 78 packages upgraded
- Kernel updated to 6.8.0-124 (was pending reboot, now active)

## Verification
- [x] SSH config validated via `sshd -T`
- [x] UFW active with correct rules
- [x] fail2ban running with active bans
- [x] Hermes gateway auto-starts on boot
- [x] Ubuntu NOPASSWD sudo preserved (Hermes requirement)

## Related
- [[Google Workspace Setup]]
- [[2026-06-03]]
- [[Gateway Infrastructure]] — gateway health monitoring and watchdog

Zaim here. Wanted to capture this before I forget.

Between KSI sessions I have these 15-minute gaps. Started using them to jot down one thing I noticed about each student — what they're stuck on, what lit them up. After a month of this, patterns emerged that I'd never have spotted otherwise.

Three buckets emerged:
1. Career pivoters — stuck on 'what if I fail' not 'what do I want'
2. Fresh grads — over-indexing on salary, under-indexing on growth trajectory  
3. Mid-career — all variations of 'is this it?'

The 15-minute rule: never let a gap go empty. Even one sentence per student compounds.

— Zaim

͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏
͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏
͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏
͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏ ͏­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­­


It's June. How's that career goal from January going?

If you're being honest, probably: forgotten by March, barely remembered
(and feeling guilty) in June and quietly dropped by...the end of the year.

It's a timeframe problem, not a discipline problem.
The annual goal trap

A year is too long to create urgency and too vague to act on.

"Get a new job this year" feels fine in January. There's time. So nothing
happens.

By June, the goal is still technically alive, but you haven't done anything
that moves it forward. By December, you're setting the same goal again for
next year.

I went through this myself but with weight, not career. In January 2025, I
set an annual goal: lose 6kg by December. Standard SMART goal. Looked fine
on paper.

Then I came across Brian Moran and Michael Lennington's book "The 12 Week
Year," which argues most people don't actually operate in years. They
operate in the last few weeks before a deadline, however long that deadline
is. So the fix is to shrink the deadline.

Btw, this book is available on Shopee!

I rewrote the goal: lose 6kg in 90 days, by 31 March.

I hit it!!

The best part was that I never had to set that goal again. The habits I
built in those 90 days kept running on their own for the rest of the year.
Why this works for your career too

The same applies to a job search, a promotion case or a pivot.

"Get promoted this year" doesn't tell you what to do on Monday.

"By 30 September, I will have had 3 conversations with people in my target
department, applied to 5 internal roles, and presented one project to
leadership" tells you exactly what to do on Monday.

One of these is a wish. The other is a plan.

I've worked with clients on exactly this. The ones who set a 90-day goal
like "land 3 offers from MNCs by end of Q3" with weekly actions attached,
mostly hit it or get close. The ones with "find a better job this year"
mostly don't, because there's no Monday task attached to it.
How to set yours

Pick ONE career goal for this quarter. Just one.

Follow this format and write down:

   - *My 90-day career goal is...*
   - *This matters to me because...*
   - *To complete this, I commit that by 30 September 2026, I will have...*
   (make this checkable. "3 offers" is checkable. "More clarity" is not.)
   - *To make sure I complete it, I'm going to...* (weekly actions, not the
   outcome)

Your homework this weekend

Write it down. One goal. One sentence you can check off or not, no grey
area.

Then build it into your week, not your year.
------------------------------

P.S. I'm running a free workshop exactly on setting this 90-day goal for Q3
2026. If you're interested to join, it's happening early next month in
June. For more info or to sign up, head over to
https://t.ly/getclearoncareer6
<https://preview.kit-mail3.com/click/dpheh0hzhmh4/aHR0cHM6Ly90Lmx5L2dldGNsZWFyb25jYXJlZXI2>

Unsubscribe <https://preview.kit-mail3.com/unsubscribe> | Update your
profile <https://preview.kit-mail3.com/preferences> | I Write on LinkedIn
Every Day - Follow Me For Career, AI and Productivity Insights
<https://preview.kit-mail3.com/click/dpheh0hzhmh4/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubGlua2VkaW4uY29tL2luL3phaW1tb2h6YW5pLw==>

# The 3-Month Career Pivot Framework

**Source:** email forward
**Source URI:** gmail://msg_19eca58bcdb09c70
**Ingested:** 2026-06-20T04:00:00Z
**Provenance:** Forwarded to Zaim's workspace via self-BCC

Zaim shared this during a coaching session with a KSI senior analyst looking to move into product.

### Phase 1: Audit (Month 1)
- [[Skills Inventory]] — map current skills to target role requirements
- Identify the 20% skill gap that blocks 80% of interviews
- Don't learn everything — learn the gatekeepers

### Phase 2: Signal (Month 2)
- [[Side Project Strategy]] — build one public artifact, not ten private ones
- Write 2 LinkedIn posts/week documenting the pivot journey
- Engage with 3 target-company hiring managers

### Phase 3: Convert (Month 3)
- [[Salary Negotiation Playbook]] — comp bands for product roles in KL
- Reference calls with 2 people who made the same pivot
- First offer within 90 days

### Key Insight
> 'The career pivot gap isn't a skills problem — it's a positioning problem. You're not underqualified, you're framed wrong.' — Zaim

**Client:** KSI Senior Analyst → Product Manager
**Engagement:** Weekly coaching, 12 sessions
**Status:** Phase 2, on track

Zaim here. Adding a quick profile on a new client.

Name: Aisyah Rahim
Role: Senior Brand Manager at Unilever Malaysia
Context: Referred by Liyana from the KSI workshop last month. First session next Tuesday.

Key points from our intro call:
- 8 years in FMCG brand management, feeling stagnant
- Wants to pivot into tech but unsure where to start
- Main blocker: 'I don't have a technical background'
- Has actually done more data-driven work than she realises — ran consumer analytics for 3 product launches
- Salary anchor: RM 18k/month, worried about resetting to junior level

My read: Classic mid-career pivot case. The real problem isn't skills — it's confidence and narrative. She needs help reframing 'brand manager' as 'growth strategist who uses data.' Tech companies would kill for someone who can bridge consumer insights and product.

Plan for session 1: Audit her last 2 years of work through a tech lens. Find 3-5 specific examples where she already did 'product thinking' without calling it that.

— Zaim
